column16: Where did you hear about this application?
column17: If you selected "Organizational Outreach", please specify which club you heard about this application from
column18: If you selected "Generate Referral", please indicate who referred you to apply to this position (First + Last Name)
column19: Are there any other commitments that are not reflected elsewhere in your application?
column20: I understand the commitment and am certain I can make it. *
column21: Please customize your resume to match the unique responsibilities and qualifications required for this role. Then, attach your resume (PDF only). *Your resume should be one page, and should highlight past experiences which make you an excellent fit for this specific role. This will almost certainly require a resume different from the one you use for co-op applications.
column23: If you have been in Generate before, please describe what Generate has meant to you. If you haven't been in Generate, please elaborate on how your professional and/or extracurricular experiences have impacted your growth.
column24: Tell us about a project / learning experience that has fundamentally shaped you as a software engineer.
column25: What unique combination of skills and experiences positions you to excel in this role and within a team? In other words: what lets you stand out?
column26: Please describe one technical skill you have developed that you are confident in and one that you would like to learn or improve upon. What motivates you to learn new things and take on challenges?

column19: I am a TA for CS 2000 this fall semester.
column23: Although I haven’t been in Generate before, my experiences in technical roles and personal projects have shaped me as both a software engineer and a teammate, preparing me to make a positive impact at Generate. This past summer, I served as a TA for Fundamentals of Computer Science II, where I guided students through complex programming concepts and helped them debug their code. Teaching reinforced my understanding as I often had to revisit topics, relearn them from new angles, and explain them clearly and concisely under pressure. This experience sharpened my technical acumen, strengthened my communication skills, and taught me how to adapt quickly to shifting challenges, skills I now carry into every collaborative environment. My leadership roles, including officer positions in high school and joining Hall Council at Northeastern, taught me how to take initiative, manage responsibility, and advocate for change. Planning large-scale events, working with administrators, and leading diverse teams pushed me to become more organized, decisive, and proactive. These experiences gave me the confidence to step into unfamiliar roles, listen actively, and contribute to a shared vision. These are skills I look forward to applying on a team at Generate. Outside of teaching, my research and project work helped me grow by pushing me to navigate unfamiliar systems and solve problems independently. Whether debugging large codebases in the YCSB project or testing Synth’s capabilities as a research assistant, I’ve consistently found that taking the time to deeply understand problems leads to better solutions.
column24: This past summer, I worked on extending Yahoo! Cloud Serving Benchmark (YCSB) to use a standardized input format that could map to different databases, allowing us to test multiple systems without changing the workload interface, and it fundamentally shaped how I approach software engineering. At the start, I barely understood what drivers, replication, or even EC2 clusters were. Throughout the project, I had to refactor a legacy Cassandra client to integrate a new Datastax v4 driver, replace deprecated APIs, and troubleshoot a series of complex build and runtime errors. Each problem, from SSL context failures to authentication bugs, forced me to dig into unfamiliar documentation, read logs closely, and systematically test solutions until things worked. Beyond the code, I learned how important persistence and collaboration are when working on large systems. At times, progress felt slow and frustrating, but working with my team, asking questions, and pushing through trial and error taught me how to stay resilient. By the end, I not only understood distributed database replication and AWS Keyspaces integration, but also gained confidence in my ability to debug and deliver under pressure. In addition, I took on each of these challenges head, open to making mistakes and treating these struggles as an opportunity to learn, and I carry that mindset into every new project I take on.
column25: I turn uncertainty into progress and have the self confidence to ask questions. I’ve found that asking the right questions, whether to clarify goals or uncover underlying problems, can move a project forward faster than anything else. For example, when I first joined the Synth project, I asked how the AI assistant would handle a specific edge case. That single question led me to design test cases that revealed deeper usability issues and shaped how we improved the tool.   This mindset has helped me contribute meaningfully on every team I’ve been part of, whether I was leading or supporting others. I bring adaptability shaped by working across various domains, from robotics teams and research labs to collaborative software development projects. These experiences taught me how to adapt to changing contexts, switch roles as needed, listen to others’ expertise, and contribute to solutions as part of a cohesive team. I’ve found that I learn the most when I put myself in unfamiliar or uncomfortable positions/situations that force me to grow. I actively seek those opportunities, whether that means choosing to work with a new tool like Cassandra instead of the more familiar MongoDB, or stepping into a role that stretches my abilities. I’m confident in my ability to learn quickly and adapt. I’m excited to bring this mindset to Generate, where I can continue stepping into unfamiliar territory, learn from every challenge, and contribute meaningfully to my team from day one.
column26: One technical skill I’ve developed and feel confident in is debugging and refactoring complex codebases. I was first introduced to this during high school robotics, where I worked on software subsystems for our competition robot. As a research assistant at Northeastern, I improved Synth, an AI-powered coding assistant, by designing test cases on large codebases to uncover edge cases and usability challenges. This skill became even more critical in my YCSB+R benchmarking project, where I reworked a Cassandra client to support a new driver, updated outdated APIs, and resolved various build and runtime issues. These experiences taught me to methodically debug, navigate unfamiliar systems and architectures, and adapt quickly to evolving technical requirements. A skill I want to strengthen is front-end engineering and UI/UX implementation. While I’ve built very basic React projects, I want to learn how to create interfaces that feel seamless to users and enhance overall product experience. I'm excited by how small design choices can dramatically improve usability. I’m motivated by the challenge of transforming broken systems into impactful and useful systems. I learn best by doing, especially when I can collaborate closely with people who bring different perspectives or deeper expertise. I enjoy pair programming and team-based development because it gives me the chance to see how others think, share ideas in real time, and grow faster through feedback. Working with the driven peers at Generate will push me to expand my skills, contribute meaningfully, and help build tools that matter.

column23: Being a software engineer for Generate is one of the most beneficial opportunities I've been given. The Sherm community is CRACKED and I find myself learning something new every time I'm in the room.  In my work on Platnm and Dearly, I’ve grown attached to new tech stacks, had fun experimenting with new technologies, and enjoyed degenerating at night. Showcase is one of my favorite events of the entire semester, and I love talking to people to show off the cool app we built, being able to point out the features I’ve built/pulled out my hair over, all while representing this larger-than-life organization. And we get to dress up. The experiences I've had directly translate to other parts of my life, whether it be ground-up designing a system at co-op or building at my current role matching the "Generate" tech stack. It's a point of pride to have those stickers on my laptop.  Moreover, when it comes to something I haven’t yet encountered, the concept of learning by doing is something so intrinsic to Generate, and being a slack message or chair shuffle away from another brain is something so important to personal growth. This club has truly helped me grow on my own, and within a broader community.
column24: Ask anyone that was on Dearly last semester, you'll know how much I complained about my co-op LOL. However, the work I did at NExT really helped me refine my skills as a software engineer. NExT, an NU-backed industry consulting group, was modeled a bit after Generate, with its small (six-person) student-run teams and client work.  On the technical side, as one of the more experienced members on my team (Verizon), I took on a lot of the system design, as we chose our tech stack and delegated tasks. I dove deep into the minutia behind each system decision, and contributed heftily to some hard-hitting tickets, and I’ve now been introduced/accustomed to many new aspects of software engineering, including Cloud and DevOps, while also ramping up my understanding of front-end and back-end frameworks, as well as code efficiency and best practices. This experience was incredibly valuable because I learned new technologies, navigated challenges, and consistently managed project milestones by setting deadlines and evaluating progress—on a personal level and as a team.  I truly understand the importance of proper code design, reviewing and testing, and this is a result of all my experiences.
column25: I bring communication and drive to development. These are priorities I've held in everything; from my current role at a startup, to my work with Generate, and even at a two-day hackathon. I ask clarifying questions to design teams and tech leads to ensure I understand each ticket thoroughly and deliver exactly what's needed, from the GitHub project page through to completion. I take accountability for deliverables while keeping everyone motivated and connected to our goals. This means proactively communicating progress, especially when presenting to higher-ups, and escalating blockers before they stall my velocity. Transparent updates and early problem identification keep projects moving efficiently. I also contribute through ideation—suggesting features based on user experience insights, like recommending Dearly use the native photo gallery over building a custom in-app solution. These contributions come from actively listening and understanding both technical constraints and user needs. Beyond the technical work, I focus on creating spaces where everyone's talents can grow. Having navigated unfamiliar environments myself, I know how critical feedback and collaborative growth accelerate both individual mastery and team success. Fostering this kind of environment where people can take pride in their work—that's what drives the progress Generate is famous for, and the feeling of accomplishment that makes all the effort worthwhile.
column26: Shout out to Stone, because he and Generate have made me very confident in Git. I was able to teach others at co-op (including my boss) how to rebase, the reasons behind it, when to rebase and when not to (i.e. not into main), the power of interactive rebase, how to revive a force-push with git reflog, when to cherry-pick, how to use git blame, even the aura of having a verified commit with -S. Version control is a tool that I initially felt to be elementary, and while simple, I now understand the nuances and capabilities of this vital tool. On the other hand, a technical skill I’d like to improve is my DSA. I’ve noticed that I seemingly shy away from algorithms, whether it be an event recommendation algorithm at co-op or a LC Medium in a high-stakes technical interview. Thus, along with the innate motivation from taking on a project I love, I’ve learned I gain motivation from the people around me. I remember sitting in the Sherm and hovering behind Zainab as Mai and Stone talked her through a Leetcode problem, or talking with Melody about her interviews and some of my upcoming ones. Beyond the actual project itself, it’s the Generate community I also need—a community that’s engaging, humble, and helpful.

column23: From childhood until the end of high school, I was sure I would become a doctor. I loved imagining meeting patients, running tests, and diagnosing illnesses. Ironically, volunteering at a hospital made me realize this wasn’t for me, but that desire to impact lives never left. Through technology, I have been able to continue to impact lives, especially in healthcare applications. During my co-op at The Burnes Center, I discovered how rewarding public sector work could be. We streamlined the review process for small business loans, getting critical funding to owners faster. I talked to real government employees and past applicants who were frustrated by the complexity and wait times. Seeing their excitement about our AI tool made everything worthwhile. While I don't plan to work in the public sector full time, I need my work to matter. My projects have shifted from fun experiments like online drawing games to meaningful solutions such as an addiction recovery website and a medical document organizer. Each project reinforces that technology can genuinely improve lives. I'm excited to continue pursuing this passion with Generate, creating solutions that address real problems for real people.
column24: Working in a lab group with three other students taught me crucial lessons about teamwork and perspective. One member wasn't contributing much, and while others wanted to immediately escalate to our professor, I suggested talking to him first. That conversation revealed he was dealing with serious personal issues at home that affected his schoolwork. He apologized to our group, and together we created a plan ensuring everyone contributed fairly while maintaining constant communication. The semester proceeded smoothly after that. This experience fundamentally changed how I approach software engineering teams. I learned that every situation has multiple perspectives, and jumping to conclusions rarely helps. Clear expectations and open communication are essential, but so is understanding that teammates are whole people with lives outside our project. When developing solutions now, I always consider different angles and stakeholder perspectives. This human-centered approach means focusing on the actual problem rather than rushing to implement a solution. It's made me a better collaborator and more thoughtful developer.
column25: Outside coding, I spend hours video editing and playing violin, and both have surprisingly shaped my approach to software development. In video editing, I'll zoom into millisecond adjustments, tweaking tiny details until everything flows seamlessly, then zoom out to see the complete story unfold perfectly. I’ve brought this meticulous attention to my code, knowing that small oversights can cause major issues. Just like how a single frame or sound effect can transform a viewer's experience, a well-placed function or optimized query can make software feel effortless to users. Playing violin in orchestras taught me about harmonizing different strengths within a team. Each section has its role, and when everyone plays their part with precision and awareness of others, something beautiful emerges. In development teams, I've learned to appreciate how different skill sets complement each other. My combination of technical precision from coding, creative problem-solving from editing, and collaborative spirit from music helps me contribute meaningfully while supporting my teammates' growth.
column26: I'm confident in my database skills, particularly SQL and MongoDB, along with backend development and API design. Through my addiction recovery app and co-op work, I've become comfortable architecting data structures and creating efficient endpoints that handle complex queries. I understand how to optimize database performance and ensure data integrity across systems. However, I want to improve my frontend development skills and create smoother connections between frontend and backend components. While I can build functional interfaces, I know there's so much more to learn about creating truly intuitive user experiences. I'm also eager to learn web scraping, which would open up entirely new project possibilities by accessing and analyzing data from across the internet. What motivates me to tackle these challenges is seeing how each new skill vastly widens the scope of projects I can build. When I struggled with API integration on my recovery app, the satisfaction of finally connecting everything properly was incredible. That feeling of breakthrough and satisfaction from understanding something new keeps me pushing to learn more.

column23: After spending a semester in Generate I learnt so much, but there is still so much more I would like to learn. I am very grateful for the connections that I was able to make within my team during the semester and it truly made the whole process so much more enjoyable. Being friends with the people on my team made me a lot more comfortable to reach out for help, not only to my TLs but also to other engineers on my team. This environment of collaboration allowed me to learn so much more. I thoroughly enjoyed all the time that I spent with my team. I think that this experience of getting to work on technical projects while also prioritizing social activities and connections is unique to Generate. It’s this working environment that Generate has created that makes me so eager to do it all again this semester.
column24: One project that fundamentally shaped me as a software engineer was my first sizable team based experience, a hackathon I participated in during my second semester. Our goal was to develop the backend of a drawing game website, something that sounded exciting but also daunting at the time, especially since none of us had much experience with JavaScript. What I brought to the team was a strong willingness to learn and an eagerness to problem-solve. I dove into unfamiliar tools and concepts, and worked closely with my teammates to troubleshoot, debug, and iterate quickly under time pressure. It was an intense learning curve, but it taught me how to stay adaptable and focused, even when the solution wasn’t immediately clear. Just as importantly, it was a crash course in team dynamics. I hadn’t worked with this group before, so I made a conscious effort to communicate clearly, ask questions openly, and foster a collaborative environment.  Looking back, that experience shaped both my technical growth and my approach to working on teams. It instilled a deeper confidence in my ability to pick up new technologies quickly and reminded me how essential communication and mindset are to a project’s success.
column25: I believe what truly sets me apart is my strong communication skills, especially in collaborative settings. In addition to my technical background and hands-on coding experience, I’m also an active member of Husky Ambassadors. In this role, I not only work with prospective students and their families, but I also work closely with individuals across different areas of the organization to collaborate, address concerns and implement feedback. These experiences have allowed me to refine my ability to actively listen, communicate clearly with diverse audiences, and help build strong, cohesive teams. I firmly believe that when communication and collaboration are prioritized, teams are not only more productive but also more innovative. Personally, I do my best work when I can exchange ideas with teammates, challenge perspectives, and build on each other’s strengths. Fostering this kind of environment, where people feel comfortable sharing and growing together, is something I value deeply and actively contribute to which I think helps me to excel. This blend of interpersonal strengths and technical ability allows me to thrive in both the role itself and the team around it.
column26: The technical skill that I am most confident in is programming in Java. Whilst I have by no means mastered it, I feel that after going through Fundies 2 and OOD I have spent enough time messing around with the language to gain a level of confidence in my ability to use it effectively. After completing a semester in Generate I am interested in getting more experience using Go. I enjoyed learning a new language that I had never seen before and now I would love to improve my abilities using it. I often question what motivates me to take on challenges, among the reasons is the sense of accomplishment that I feel when my code finally runs, or my frontend is displaying the information how I want it too. For me this feeling is unmatched and is what drives me to try my best to solve any problem I face.

column23: My time with the Oasis club at Northeastern was a huge step for my development as a programmer. Before joining, I'd only worked on frontend projects by myself. Oasis introduced me to backend development for the first time, such as working with databases and the Node.js runtime environment. But honestly, the biggest learning curve was collaborating with others. I'll never forget those merge conflicts and then learning the importance of creating side branches. My current DevOps co-op has taken things to the next level. I've discovered how much you can automate in the development process, from building and testing code to deploying applications. It's been eye-opening to see how these practices make development so much smoother and more reliable. I've also dived into infrastructure as code and gotten comfortable working in AWS, which was completely new territory for me. I have also learned about security practices such as code scanning, VPCs, and how to create secure IAM policies following least priveldge access. I've had to learn new tools quickly such as Terraform, Packer, and Jenkins, and I've been able to adopt better techniques to picking up new skills fast.
column24: My personal project 'QRuestBook' shaped me as a developer. This is because after creating many messy endpoints, I had to learn about developing in small, reversible chunks to avoid mistakes that are impossible to debug. While debugging in the VS Code editor, alongside making changes and using the Chrome inspector, I gained a stronger understanding of the client-server relationship. This also helped me develop better intuition for the problems that could be causing 400 vs 500 error codes, for example. This was also my first solo project working with a database, so I learned about valuable concepts such as pagination and integrating SQL queries within code. Overall, this was my first time diving into a full stack project. Frustrating bugs taught me to plan from the start things like a DB schema, endpoints, user inputs/data needed for those endpoints, etc., before just jumping straight into code.
column25: What sets me apart is my solid DevOps foundation, particularly in automation and security. I've worked on several automation-focused projects, including creating Amazon Machine Images with Packer and launching instances through Terraform. These experiences taught me how infrastructure as code eliminates repetitive manual work and significantly reduces deployment time. I've also developed a security-first approach to DevOps. I've created IAM policies following least privilege principles, ensuring users and services only access what they absolutely need. Additionally, I've integrated automated code scanning into CI/CD pipelines, allowing vulnerabilities to be caught and addressed before code reaches production. This combination of automation expertise and security awareness means I don't just focus on making things work – I make them work efficiently and safely. I understand that modern development requires thinking beyond just writing code to considering how applications are built, deployed, and secured at scale. My DevOps background gives me a unique perspective on the entire software development lifecycle.
column26: A skill I'm confident in is my ability to quickly learn new frameworks and technologies. I've consistently picked up new tools efficiently because I understand the value of investing time upfront to read documentation and research best practices before diving into implementation. This approach has served me well across various languages and platforms. One area I'm actively working to improve is my networking knowledge. While I have a solid grasp of the fundamentals, I want to deepen my understanding of network security practices, particularly around securing traffic with protocols like TLS/SSL and implementing proper firewall configurations. I'm also interested in learning more about network monitoring and how to identify potential security vulnerabilities in network architecture. The main thing that motivates me to learn new things is curiosity. I like to understand how the tools I use work in depth because then I can have more control over my development process and a deeper understanding always helps me have a clearer vision for whatever I am trying to accomplish/build.

column16: I heard about this application from peers, online search, and networking events.
column19: Only for the summer I am working on a mobile app with a small group of developers.
column23: As rising second year student at Northeastern, I would say that the extracurricular experiences I had during my freshman year greatly impacted me by strengthening my web development skills.   During the Spring semester, I joined several software development clubs, including Oasis and Northeastern Electric Racing (NER). In Oasis, I learned how to work in a group environment using Git/GitHub, and there I had my first experiences using React, along with the Tailwind CSS framework and Material UI. In NER, I was placed in a agile, ticket-based environment, so I was able to use what I learned in Oasis to start working on featured components in their FinishLine website.   These two clubs have fundamentally shaped both what I have come to learn technically, and behaviorally. In terms of technical learning, I was able to build off the frontend experience I gained from both clubs to eventually learn more about full-stack development overall, leading to my current positions development end-to-end products in multiple startups. In terms of behavioral learning, I've come to learn that great pieces of software come from effective communication and teamwork, and both clubs I have mentioned place great emphasis on collaboration, so I am now better able to communicate and work with peers/colleagues.
column24: Currently, I work for a startup called Quak Travel, and this has been the first startup endeavor I have been a part of. In terms of what I do for this startup, I'm currently working on developing an all-in-one stop and go platform for young travelers in the form of a website. This experience has greatly affected the way I seek guidance when I do not know something technically and may not have the support required. Startup culture tends to place less emphasis on guidance and more emphasis toward getting a minimum viable product out, so I've had to learn how to implement many things in web development that I previously didn't have experience with such as database design, API service integration, and caching systems. However, as I've spent more time building this website, I've come to learn that learning doesn't always mean being told what to do, but rather can come about when you're resourceful, curious, and systematic with both the features you decide to implement, and the research you perform outside of your work time. Thus, I feel more confident now as a software engineer to explore concepts I'm unfamiliar with because I've learned how to independently break down problems and research new topics.
column25: What makes me stand out is the depth and variety of my collaborative experiences across multiple fast-paced teams and projects along with my experiences working with startups.   In almost every technical experience I've had, I've collaborated with other developers in a meaningful way, so I am quite familiar with the workflows that lead to developmental progression. In particular, I have contributed to projects both as an individual contributor making small features to be combined with the work with other contributors, and as a tech lead delegating tasks to developers and making sure features are being utilized and merged correctly.   My experiences in these positions have allowed me to gain skills in both software development and project management. Particularly with my experience working with startups, I've learned to balance technical excellence and moving quickly without sacrificing code quality. This has also helped me to build my communicative skills with those I work with as efficient and regular conversation with people you work with leads to less confusion and more productive experiences.
column26: One technical skill that I have confidently developed is my ability to create and leverage database structures. In particular, I have gained a lot of experience using Prisma to create schemas that maintain cohesiveness and data integrity for a given platform. Additionally, I am currently taking Northeastern's Introduction to Databases course (CS3200), so I have also learned more about SQL and the proper way to structure a relational database in an environment separated from specific website applications.   One technical skill I'd like to improve on is cloud services. I've taken a Northeastern course on AWS technologies so I am familiar with many services and their purposes, but I do not have much experience actually implementing them as I have not reached stages of technical development in the startups I am involved in that require the usage of them.   Seeing the direct impact that learning new skills has on real projects and teams has pushed me to learn technologies that I may not know/be comfortable with. Whenever I start to learn a new skill, I feel as if I'm able to contribute more meaningfully to a team or take on further responsibilities that help other developers succeed, so I think that learning new things allows me to both better myself, and help others too.

column23: Although I haven’t been in Generate yet, my growth as an engineer has been shaped by diving deep into both solo and team experiences. Building UniMart, a student-exclusive marketplace, has been my most rewarding project. I designed the entire backend with Spring Boot and MySQL, added Docker to streamline environments, and even handled frontend development and deployment. It pushed me to problem-solve in ways that felt real and high stakes. At the same time, working as an SDET co-op at MFS has helped me understand what writing clean, scalable code looks like in a large, enterprise setting. I’m currently assisting the migration of an entire Selenium framework to Playwright, reducing test runtimes by 75% and cutting flakiness drastically. I also worked with APIs, CI/CD pipelines, and learned how to write testable code in a fast-moving Agile team. Both experiences made me realize how much I enjoy building and improving systems, especially when surrounded by passionate people. That’s what excites me most about Generate, the chance to grow with and contribute to a community of builders.
column24: My main project, UniMart, has completely shaped how I think about software engineering. It started as a simple idea, what if there was a secure, student-only marketplace; but quickly became something I poured countless hours into. I built the backend with Spring Boot and MySQL, created a .edu authentication system, and even Dockerized the services to keep everything consistent. I also designed the frontend using React and Figma, which was a big learning curve on its own. But what really changed me was hitting real-world roadblocks. Deployment delays, authentication bugs, and handling edge cases in transactions all pushed me to think deeper about how systems work. I stopped viewing backend as just “writing API routes” and started understanding things like database normalization, transactional integrity, and performance. This project gave me ownership, and I learned how to build something from scratch that could actually serve people. It’s what made me realize I want to become a strong backend engineer who can confidently take an idea and make it real. That mindset of building with care and purpose is something I hope to bring to every team I'm part of, including Generate.
column25: I think my biggest strength is that I’ve seen both sides: I’ve built solo projects from scratch and contributed to large-scale enterprise systems. With UniMart as well as my other projects, I wore every hat: backend, frontend, DevOps, and got to really understand what goes into building a real product end to end. It gave me a lot of technical depth but also taught me how to plan, prioritize, and stay motivated. At my MFS co-op, I joined an Agile team where I focused on automating UI and API tests, working in a large codebase with strict standards. I learned how to write clean, testable code and how to navigate a mature dev workflow with real impact. My automation work reduced testing time by hours each sprint. On top of that, I was a TA for Discrete Structures and Database Design, which has helped me get better at explaining concepts and collaborating with others. I care a lot about building strong team culture and lifting people up, whether it’s through code reviews or just being someone you can rely on. I see Generate as more than just a club, I see it as a space where I can grow alongside other builders and elevate our skills to something past just programmers but rather architects of code.
column26: I feel really confident in backend development, especially with Java and Spring Boot. After working on UniMart and handling everything from user authentication to transactional-safe order processing, I’ve gotten very comfortable designing APIs, managing SQL databases, and building reliable backend systems. I genuinely enjoy thinking through the logic of how things connect behind the scenes. One skill I really want to improve is DevOps. I’ve started containerizing services and deploying with AWS, but I want to take it further. Understanding CI/CD deeply, writing better deployment scripts, and owning the entire release pipeline. It’s something I’ve seen done well at MFS, and I’d love to gain more hands-on experience with it at Generate.  What motivates me most is the feeling of making progress. When I struggle with something for hours and finally get it to work, that “aha” moment is incredibly satisfying. I’m also just curious by nature. If I don’t understand something, I need to figure it out. And being around other passionate builders makes that process even more fun. That’s the kind of energy I hope to bring into every challenge I take on at Generate.

column19: I will be TAing in the fall, but otherwise nothing major.
column23: Throughout my journey as a software engineer, I've struggled with finding a clear niche in computer science, as I’ve enjoyed various aspects of development, whether frontend, backend, or cloud computing. I often questioned if my versatility might hinder my ability to make a deep impact in any one area. My co-op at NExT, where I delivered a client project for Verizon, was pivotal in shifting this mindset. It gave me the chance to immerse myself in a high-stakes project, translating complex client requirements into actionable solutions while working fluidly across development. I even had the opportunity to serve as scrum master, where I facilitated team collaboration and drove progress, ensuring we stayed aligned with project goals and client expectations. Most importantly, however, I was part of a growth-oriented, agile team that helped me recognize that learning is not a linear journey. Whenever I doubted my depth in a particular area, my team was there with feedback, support, and encouragement. This experience changed my perspective, and I’m excited to bring this collaborative, growth-driven mindset to Generate, where I can contribute to a project with real-world impact while continuing to learn and grow in a team that values both personal and collective development.
column24: A project that significantly shaped me as a software engineer was CleanSweep, a community-driven waste management platform. I worked with a partner who led the UI design, while I focused on the backend. Integrating their designs into the platform pushed me to think more holistically about the user experience and the overall platform goals. Despite UI/UX not being my strength, collaborating with them taught me essential design principles such as flow, accessibility, and visual hierarchy. These principles have become a core focus in my own projects, helping me balance usability with functionality when working on the frontend. Meanwhile on the backend, I tackled the challenge of database design, an area I was initially unfamiliar with. As the project expanded, I gained hands-on experience designing tables, setting relationships, and optimizing queries in PostgreSQL. These skills became invaluable in my co-op, where I applied them to take on more complex tasks and expanded my understanding of backend development. Overall, CleanSweep made me a more well-rounded software engineer, sharpening both my frontend and backend skills, and reinforcing the importance of learning from teammates with diverse strengths.
column25: What sets me apart is my ability to excel in both backend and frontend development, quickly adapt to new technologies, and collaborate effectively in high-stakes, client-facing projects. My diverse experience enables me to contribute at various stages of a project and tackle challenges across the entire development process. I am deeply committed to continuous learning, always striving to sharpen my expertise and expand my skill set. For example, I’m currently focused on mobile app development, an area I wasn’t previously familiar with, as I aim to broaden my technical abilities and stay adaptable. Additionally, my co-op at NExT, while unique compared to traditional experiences, taught me invaluable skills in product development for clients. The process of translating vague client requirements into actionable solutions was a challenge in itself. Regular communication with clients and within my team helped me understand the importance of persistence, adaptability, and maintaining focus on deadlines and quality. This experience has shaped my iterative approach to development, which I apply to all my projects. These qualities, along with my commitment to learning and collaboration, not only position me to deliver impactful results individually but also enable me to contribute effectively within a team, ensuring that we meet client needs while driving forward collective success.
column26: One technical skill I’m confident in is creating REST APIs. I’ve had extensive practice in designing and building APIs through personal projects, my web development class, and my co-op. I’ve developed a strong understanding of API design principles, and I’m comfortable designing endpoints, handling request-response cycles, and ensuring efficient communication between the frontend and backend. I also take pride in ensuring APIs are well-documented and easy to integrate with. An area I would like to grow in is CI/CD pipelines. While I had exposure to them during my co-op, I wish I could have experienced more in depth implementation, and I feel as though I have a lot of room to grow. I hope to dive deeper into building and optimizing these pipelines so I can further build my understanding of software automation processes. Overall, what motivates me to learn new things is the opportunity to solve real-world problems and see the direct impact of my work. I thrive on tackling challenges that push me out of my comfort zone and allow me to expand my skill set, and I always look for ways to expose myself to new people and technologies so I can learn from every project and team interaction.

column23: While I haven’t been part of Generate, my professional and extracurricular experiences have shaped my growth in ways that closely align with its mission. Through iHusky, Northeastern’s iOS development club, I’ve helped lead workshops, teach Swift, and guide teams building real-world apps. It’s taught me how to balance technical execution with collaboration, and how to create an environment where people feel excited to learn and build. In my co-op at Saint-Gobain, I was tasked with solving a real production issue end-to-end. I developed a software solution that involved working closely with non-technical stakeholders, iterating on feedback, and communicating clearly across roles. It taught me the importance of empathy, user-driven design, and building with impact in mind. These experiences have shown me that growth as an engineer doesn’t come just from writing code, it comes from working with people, staying curious, and building things that matter. That’s what draws me to Generate: the chance to learn and contribute in a community that values both the technical and the human side of engineering.
column24: During my first internship at Saint-Gobain, I was tasked with designing a program to track the runtime efficiency of our metal bearing-producing machines. After weeks of work, I proudly demoed the finished product to my manager and several plant leaders. I expected praise, but instead I got a stern critique from the plant manager. The tool I had built missed the mark entirely, and the list of required changes was long and specific. It was a humbling moment and a blow to my pride, but ultimately one of the most important experiences in my growth as a software engineer. I realized that technical skill alone wasn’t enough; understanding user needs and aligning with stakeholder expectations were just as critical. I went back to the drawing board, this time meeting regularly with operators and managers, gathering continuous feedback, and testing iteratively. The final product was far more impactful, and the process taught me how to build software not just for myself, but for others. That mindset has stuck with me ever since.
column25: What sets me apart is a balance of technical experience, leadership, and calm, people-first communication. I’ve worked on several software teams such as co-ops, class projects, and iHusky, the Northeastern iOS development club I was part of starting, where I’ve helped build real products and supported others as both a peer tutor and team lead. I bring a relaxed, steady energy to every team I join. I don’t panic under pressure; instead, I try to keep myself and those around me focused and composed. I’ve found that teams work best when people feel calm, heard, and supported, especially during high-stakes situations like bug fixes or last-minute changes. That mindset has helped me navigate everything from tight deadlines to big pivots mid-project. Whether leading meetings or writing code alongside teammates, I value clear communication and empathy just as much as technical skill. I want to be someone people enjoy working with, someone who helps the team succeed, not just the task.  I believe this mix of hands-on software experience, leadership, and people-first collaboration positions me well to contribute meaningfully and supportively to any team I’m part of.
column26: One technical skill I’ve developed that I’m confident in is my ability to quickly get up to speed with unfamiliar tools, languages, or frameworks. Over time, I’ve become comfortable diving into the unknown, whether it's picking up a new tech stack for a club project or navigating undocumented systems during my co-op. That competence in learning itself has become one of my biggest strengths; I trust that I can figure things out, even if I don’t know the answer right away.  What I’d like to improve is my consistency in taking initiative. While I do well when given responsibility, I’m working on being more proactive, seeking out ownership early, proposing ideas without prompting, and taking the first step even when things are ambiguous. What motivates me most is the feeling of not knowing something. That discomfort is exciting to me, because it means there’s room to grow, and it’s often where the most meaningful learning happens. Whether it's debugging a problem I’ve never seen or leading something I’ve never done before, I’m driven by the challenge of turning confusion into confidence.

column23: My class projects and extracurricular experiences have played a big role in my growth. Working on group projects in class has taught me how to communicate effectively, share responsibilities, and adapt to the needs of my team. Outside of class, participating in an OASIS project to build a website gave me hands-on experience with real world development. We were also given deadlines which (in my opinion), made it even more realistic. It pushed me to apply what I’ve learned, pick up new technical skills quickly, and take ownership of my contributions. Both experiences helped shape me into a more capable teammate and a more confident developer.
column24: Creating a website (Schedulytics) has significantly impacted my technical and personal growth. Building a fullstack web application from the ground up taught me how to design scalable systems and improved my understanding of both frontend and backend development. I learned to work with modern tools like React, TypeScript, and Supabase, along with learning how to deploy projects using Vercel. The project, which is meant to be an enhanced professor review platform powered by data from TRACE surveys and RateMyProfessor, also sharpened my skills in data scraping, preprocessing, and presenting complex insights in a user-friendly way (the css). This experience helped me grow a ton as a software engineer.
column25: One of my strongest skills is my ability to work with others to troubleshoot and figure out what’s going wrong in a finished piece of code (through testing everything). I also work best with teammates to pinpoint issues that might not be immediately obvious and do this by bouncing ideas off others and solving them together. In fact, I find I work more effectively in a group setting than solo, as collaboration helps me think more clearly and creatively. I’m also highly adaptable and always eager to learn new coding languages, tools, or frameworks to meet the needs of the project.
column26: One technical skill I’ve developed and feel very confident in is working with JavaScript. I’ve used it extensively in both personal and team projects, and it’s become second nature to me when building interactive web applications. Lately, I’ve been wanting to improve my skills in TypeScript. While I’ve used it a little, I want to dive deeper and get more experience with it. What motivates me to learn new technologies is the ability to create things from scratch as seeing an idea turn into something real through code is incredibly rewarding. I also enjoy pushing myself outside of my comfort zone as taking on new challenges keeps me engaged, and helps me grow.

column23: I’ve been seriously trying to get into software engineering for less than a year. I’ve learned a lot and made solid progress, but I don’t feel like it’s enough to write a deep story about yet. So instead, I want to talk about something that’s been with me much longer: music. I’ve been playing guitar for the last five years, and other instruments before that. It’s always been a part of my life, so I tried to post my playing online as I thought I’m a solid player. But it was such a humbling experience. Not because people’d say something bad, but because I started hearing every tiny mistake I used to ignore. Notes slightly off, rhythm not quite right. I’d spend hours replaying the same few seconds, trying to make it sound clean. It was frustrating, but it taught me a lot. I realized real growth happens in the small details, and that doing something “well enough” is not the same as doing it right. That mindset is now what I’m trying to apply to all parts of my life. Not always successful, but well, I guess that’s how people grow.
column24: This year, I tried myself as a freelance web developer. One of my clients was a real estate agent who needed a website for her business. I was genuinely excited because the project had some tricky technical parts, and I saw it as a chance to learn and grow. But it didn’t take long to realize that writing code was just one part of the job. Her vision kept changing as we went, and she kept adding new features and pages to the project. I found myself constantly reworking layouts, adjusting designs, and trying to make it all fit together. At one point, she asked me to integrate Excel APIs, which I hadn’t worked with before, and figuring it out took way longer than expected. That part alone pushed us past the original timeline. Looking back, it taught me how important it is to talk through all the details up front. Aligning expectations, defining scope, and building in extra time for the unexpected is just as important as knowing how to code. Even though the project never got deployed and communication eventually faded, the experience gave me a much clearer picture of what it means to build software in the real world.
column25: I genuinely think my strongest trait is the way I learn. I pick things up quickly, but more than that, I’m a very observant type of person. I pay close attention to how other people do things, and I try to understand what makes their approach effective, why they do it the way they do. If I like something, I kinda “steal” it in a good way, and incorporate it into my own workflow. I really enjoy learning from others and improving through observation. When I get into something, I go all in. If I’m really interested, I can literally lock myself in a room for days and just grind. I actually forget about everything else. That’s how I’ve approached most of my hobbies. For instance, I started project coding seriously just half a year ago, but I feel like I learned a lot and improved significantly in such a short timeframe. So out of everything, I think this is the quality I rely on most to keep improving.
column26: One technical skill I’ve developed and feel confident in is web scraping. I used to do it a lot for personal projects just because I found it interesting to collect data from different websites. I liked figuring out how the site loads content, finding the right patterns in the HTML, and writing scripts using BeautifulSoup and Selenium to extract what I needed. At one point, I wanted to build my own large database of information for science olympiads with all problems, solutions, and files from all around the world. Back in school, I dreamed of going to IPhO (didn’t work out), so I just wanted to make something that could help future students access everything in one place. Databases are really something I want to get better at. I know the basics of SQL and how to connect and query, but I’d like to get better at designing, managing, and editing more complex databases. What motivates me to learn is pretty simple. I don’t want to live life with regrets. A while ago, I promised myself that I want to look back one day and feel proud of what I’ve done, and happy with how I got there.

column23: Though I haven't been in Generate before, joining has been a huge goal since meeting upperclassmen who excited me about the growth and learning opportunities here. While not in Generate, I’ve made sure to keep myself involved within our campus community to ensure I keep learning in (and out of) the tech space. As a software developer at TedXNortheasternU, I collaborate with our Tech team to build new components and features using React for our website. Additionally, we work on integrating Sanity to manage our content dynamically. Through TedX, I’ve been able to grow technically while also getting introduced to a team setting of college students. Furthermore, my research assistant position at the Movement Neuroscience Lab (MNL) has pushed me to work with unfamiliar technologies like C# and Unity, forcing me to adapt and find comfort in new waters. My strength in teamwork stems from my internship at WRE, where I was exposed to an Agile environment in a professional setting, allowing me to take those skills and apply them here in a college environment as well. Generate would serve as the perfect next-step, allowing me to contribute what I’ve gained, while continuing to learn outside my comfort zone.
column24: Anyone pursuing tech will admit that achieving that satisfactory feeling of “Wow it really works…” is a canon event. While I’ve built things before, I got my moment of major satisfaction when I completed my SHIELD Threat Monitoring project. The inspiration came from my millionth Marvel movie marathon, but taking the project from initial idea to fully functional system was an extremely rewarding development experience. A brief overview: a threat monitoring platform that automatically scans news articles from NewsAPI, uses Gemini AI to identify potential threats, and provides a REST API for reviewing the detected threats. I was new to most concepts needed for this project, so research became crucial. Finding the right APIs was my biggest challenge, and diving into documentation taught me how different tools work and how to implement them. Most importantly, this project taught me that computer science isn’t always about solving small puzzles, but also about building systems that work in the messy, unpredictable real world. The time spent on debugging for this project can back that claim up as well! This shifted how I approach my projects; I now account for the messy parts from the beginning to be as efficient as possible.
column25: What makes me stand out is deep-diving curiosity and adaptable teamwork. When something catches my interest, I become absorbed in mastering it. My current obsession is pickleball; since discovering this summer, I’ve spent countless nights with my friends perfecting my game. This same intensity drives everything I tackle, from diving deep into fantasy worlds like Game of Thrones (currently working through the books) to optimizing my gym routines and nutrition. This curiosity translates directly to my technical work. When I first joined MNL and TEDx, there was a lot to catch up on, so I dove into the codebases and watched Youtube videos until I was comfortable working with Unity, C#, React, Sanity, etc. Making the leap to these domains has been extremely rewarding, I now understand how to work in different technical constraints and expectations. Apart from this curiosity-driven approach, my positions have ranged from being a leader, to a member of many teams. As an engineer, this perspective keeps me focused on the team’s success. I prioritize making everyone’s life easier: writing detailed documentation, staying current with project progress, and ensuring everyone's on the same page, considerations that may be lost when you're a developer working alone.
column26: I'm most confident in my ability to quickly learn new technologies. As a rising sophomore, I've embraced every chance to work with new technologies. This varied exposure to diverse tech stacks has made learning routine. At Generate, I’m sure I won’t be 100% familiar with everything I would work with, and that excites me. Beyond this, I want to learn and improve my system design and architecture skills in collaborative settings. While I’m no stranger to building and working in teams, shipping a large scale production-grade system from scratch is something I can definitely learn from. Generate would be ideal for working in an industry-adjacent setting, as I’d collaborate with my team and really understand the inner workings of such a large scale project. Designing something huge from scratch is a learning experience I am ecstatic to take advantage of as I progress in my career. What motivates me is that feeling when it clicks, and something I made is working. It would only get better at Generate, seeing my hard work translate into functional solutions that create real impact for clients.

column10: Computer Science and Business Administration (Concentration: Finance)

column23: While I haven’t been in Generate yet, a lot of my growth has come from building things with people who care just as much as I do. Whether that’s designing a GUI for a marine robot in Northeastern Robotics or replacing an international nonprofit’s paper system with a clean Airtable dashboard. Each project taught me how to problem-solve in real-world contexts, communicate with both tech and non-tech people, and stay calm when things go wrong.   But beyond the code, what’s helped me grow the most is the people like the mentors, teammates, friends who made those experiences feel collaborative, not transactional. That’s what draws me to Generate. I want to build meaningful projects with people I can also grab a bagel with after a late-night bug fix. Growth to me is technical and personal, and I’ve heard Generate offers both in one very cool, very kind package.
column24: One of the most transformative experiences for me as a software engineer has been working with the International Transformation Foundation. I helped develop a digital data management system that replaced their outdated paper-based process, making it easier for over 20 staff members in Kenya to track and analyze their work. This involved building custom tools, automations, and dashboards that not only improved data accuracy but also significantly reduced manual work.   Beyond the technical challenges, what truly shaped me were the weekly standup meetings with the international team. Connecting with people like Venuste and Sandra, hearing their stories, and seeing how the improvements we built translated into real changes, like more reliable water stations, helped me understand the human side of engineering. It became clear that clean code and beautiful data visualizations are only part of the picture; the ultimate goal is the impact on people’s lives.   This experience taught me to approach software development with empathy and intention, values I know Generate holds dear. It’s about building tools that empower teams and communities, not just completing tasks. That mindset drives me in every project I take on.
column25: I approach every project with a focus on clarity, impact, and collaboration. I’ve worked across software, data, and finance, building systems that not only function well but serve the real needs of the teams using them. Whether I’m developing an analytics dashboard, writing backend logic, or refining documentation, I care about creating tools that are thoughtful, reliable, and easy to understand.   Unlike others, I have worked with engineers, marketers, professors, and nonprofit staff in Kenya, which taught me how to adapt fast, ask good questions, and keep things moving even when the answer isn’t in a textbook. But what really sets me apart is that I care: about the work, about the people I work with, and about making things better than how I found them. I show up ready to collaborate, experiment, and learn something new.
column26: One technical skill I feel confident in is writing clean, well-organized Java code. I’ve spent a lot of time learning how to structure my programs in a way that’s modular and maintainable, and I really enjoy the logic behind object-oriented design. It’s the kind of work that feels satisfying when everything fits together just right.   Something I’m less confident in is web development. I recently built my first full website and taught myself JavaScript, TailwindCSS, and a bit of backend with Flask. It was a fun challenge, but I know I’m still early in that journey. There’s so much more I want to learn, especially when it comes to building dynamic, user-friendly applications.   What motivates me most is the feeling of turning something unfamiliar into something I understand and then actually getting to use it to build things that work. I like pushing myself out of my comfort zone, especially when I know the skills I’m gaining will help me contribute more to a team. Plus, nothing beats the moment when something finally runs after hours of trial and error.

column23: My co-op at the National Internet Observatory was extended beyond the original 6 months and has been my most valuable experience. I led a major refactor of our Chrome extension’s initialization and storage systems, improving clarity, efficiency, and documentation. Working on a two-person remote web team taught me technical ownership and asynchronous collaboration—though I now look forward to working with teammates in person too. I gained a strong appreciation for privacy-first engineering, including avoiding collection of PII and reimplementing the Sentry SDK to remove external dependencies. Collaborating closely with our Data Science team helped me bridge engineering and research needs. I found this work interesting, challenging, and rewarding, and it’s also pointed me toward a growing interest in DevOps and systems engineering.
column24: Refactoring the extension initialization and storage system at the National Internet Observatory changed how I think about software. The original code was fragile and undocumented, so I redesigned and rewrote key parts, then documented them to make future changes easier. I also built logging features under strict privacy constraints, including a custom Sentry SDK that avoids collecting user data. The project required debugging, planning, and cross-team coordination, often working asynchronously. It gave me confidence in owning complex backend systems and pushed me toward an interest in infrastructure over frontend work.
column25: I focus on backend engineering and have real experience shipping production code in a research setting. At the National Internet Observatory, I’ve worked on a small team making architectural decisions, refactoring legacy code, and building custom logging with privacy in mind. I’ve collaborated closely with our Data Science team to translate research goals into code and fix data issues. My TA and co-op roles reflect how I value clarity, careful thinking, and helping others—I enjoy making complex ideas easier to understand and documenting systems clearly. I’m motivated by building tools that are reliable and long-lasting. That mindset, along with my interest in systems work, aligns closely with Generate’s mission.
column26: I'm confident in designing and refactoring backend systems, especially in JavaScript and storage logic. I've built maintainable systems for Chrome extensions that handle real-world edge cases and support cross-team work. I’d like to deepen my DevOps skills, particularly around container orchestration, CI/CD, and monitoring—areas I’ve started exploring through Docker and WireGuard in my home lab. I'm most motivated by solving hard, often invisible problems that make systems more reliable. That kind of work drives me maybe more than UI, and it's why I'm drawn to communities like Generate.

column23: My professional experiences have shaped the way I approach building. Initially, I had a narrow mindset: label the requirements, set deadlines, build the MVP. But my experiences at National Grid and Webacy have taught me to think differently. By learning security at its fundamental level, before a system even touches a user, I’ve learned to break down problems into actionable parts, from system design to threat modeling. I developed a deeper understanding of how to prioritize what to build, focusing on what will truly help users rather than adding features for the sake of it. Building with a team has also given me a stronger sense of purpose in development. During my current internship at Grid, I’ve realized the weight of every incident I triage and every piece of software I put out. Guided by my mentors, I’ve seen the impact my role has and the people affected by our work. Working alongside them has fostered not only a sense of community but also a sense of justice, defending against malicious threat actors, valuing our customers, and protecting the information they have entrusted to us.
column24: A learning experience that shaped me as a software engineer was studying abroad in Greece. While living there, I noticed how many local businesses didn’t have websites or any online presence, relying on word of mouth or posters to grow. Simple tasks like paying bills or making appointments often required waiting in long lines or navigating old systems, creating unnecessary friction in people’s daily lives. Coming from America, where everything is digital, made me realize how much well-intentioned technology can remove barriers and give people back time, opportunities, and peace of mind. It shifted my mindset from building tech just to build to building with purpose: to create tools that help communities grow, support local organizations in need, and make everyday life easier for people. It taught me that technology development, when done thoughtfully, is one of the most impactful ways to help the world, and that’s a responsibility I want to carry into every project I take on.
column25: Although I’m not a direct computer science major, I believe my background in cybersecurity uniquely positions me to excel in a software engineering role. A common misconception is that cybersecurity isn’t technical, but I’ve found the opposite to be true. To truly understand security, you need to reverse-engineer software, breaking it down to its core components to understand not just how it works, but all the ways it could work, and all the ways it could break. In my internships, I’ve gained hands-on experience in system design, threat modeling, and debugging software under pressure. I’ve worked across the stack, analyzing APIs, network protocols, and infrastructure vulnerabilities, which has trained me to think systematically and anticipate edge cases that others might miss. This mindset helps me build with intention and clarity, prioritizing secure, scalable solutions over quick fixes. These experiences have shaped me into someone who builds with purpose, asks questions, and values the bigger initiative behind the code I write, qualities I believe will help me excel within a team at Generate.
column26: One technical skill I’m confident in is scripting, especially in Python. Through my projects and coursework, I’ve written scripts to automate tasks, review data, and scrape services. Lately, I’ve been working on a script that tracks trending releases on underground fashion archives, a project tied to my bigger goal of creating a brand that promotes these communities and building a culture. An area I’m excited to improve is full-stack development, particularly learning frontend frameworks like React and Angular. I’m passionate about building products that have an impact, and I’ve realized that even the best ideas need an engaging interface to connect with people. I once read how the founder of Notion, after going bankrupt, spent two years studying the Japanese philosophy of attraction to understand what makes a product captivating. That stuck with me, it's not just about what you build, but how it feels to the user. Learning full-stack development will help me bring projects to life from idea to brand while ensuring they genuinely serve the people using them. What motivates me most is my curiosity and the drive to build products that people want to use. I’m most fulfilled when I can apply my skills to turn those ideas into reality, using technology to create something that makes people’s lives a little bit more exciting.

column23: While I haven't been in Generate yet, I have grown immensely through various opportunities that I've had in these 2 years of college. I spent my first year in Oakland, where I worked as a "peer" teaching assistant for Fundies 2, and was heavily involved in organizing the PawHacks hackathon. Through these, I learned a lot about working with people/teams and deadlines. I have also been an avid hackathon attendee - my hackathon experiences with friends have taught me a lot about teamwork and leadership, and we were even able to win one of the major prizes at HackHarvard last year!  My co-op at Priceline was my first "professional" experience, and I gained hands-on exposure to large-scale systems with millions of daily users. It deepened my interest in system design, and I decided that I want to work on backend technologies through a career in software engineering after graduation. My biggest takeaways from Priceline were the soft skills, agile methodologies, and enterprise design/workflows. I have grown to become a more thoughtful and pragmatic software engineer, and have strengthened my ability to design, build, and work with others.
column24: The most formative experience I've had was during my co-op at Priceline, where I (sort of) led a project to evaluate whether we could replace part of our service with a GCP SaaS product.  This service provided hotel results to the chatbot, and I was tasked with evaluating result relevance improvements. I was the sole engineer working on the system, with support from another engineer who helped me understand the existing infrastructure. From the start, I was given an unusual level of ownership — my manager’s manager explicitly gave me control over the project and permission to cc him to unblock any work. This rare autonomy was both intimidating and empowering.  I collaborated directly with Google Cloud engineers who were tuning this system for our use case, and coordinated internally with many colleagues and teams. I also developed a testing suite using multiple evaluation methods. We saw a 25% improvement in relevance, and I presented the project and its results in a company-wide meeting of over 200 people, including VPs and the CTO.  This experience fundamentally changed how I approach engineering. I learned what it means to drive a project across teams, handle ambiguity, and think like a system owner.
column25: What sets me apart is a combination of long-term programming experience, a strong understanding of systems, and the ability to take initiative and lead when needed. I’ve been programming for close to 10 years, and I’ve built 19 projects in the last 3 years — from simple mobile apps in high school to full-stack systems with CI/CD, observability, and scalable architecture. I really enjoy creating software, and have a keen interest in making clean, maintainable, and practical systems.  I learn a lot when I'm working with people, and I try to work in teams as much as possible. I go to a lot of hackathons, and even led a team that won one of the major prizes at HackHarvard 2024. These experiences have taught me how to work under pressure, communicate clearly, and adapt quickly to new tools and ideas.  I think my biggest skill is being able to contribute technically with the bigger picture in mind. I enjoy writing code, but I also care about how that code fits into a system or a user experience. This mindset helps me work well in teams and take on meaningful responsibilities, and stand out as a top performer.
column26: I am confident in my system design/general programming knowledge. One thing I would like to improve on are my skills working with websockets/webrtc/etc. forms of bidirectional network communication.  My curiosity for technology motivates me to learn new things. I enjoy learning about computer science because this knowledge helps me think better and solve problems more effectively. I love building things that address real challenges I or my friends face in the daily life. When I find a problem I care about, I naturally start thinking about the technical aspects of a solution.   At the core of it, I want to achieve excellence as an engineer. I want to be the person with the deepest understanding of systems inside-out - because that knowledge will help me contribute meaningfully, build better software, and make an impact. This combination of learning, ambition, and love for building software keeps me motivated and always looking for a new challenge.

column23: Generate has meant two things for me: a place for me to grow individually in my technical and soft skills as well as being a place where I can call home, as a community. First, as a place where I have grown as a person, I have learned so much from being in this club. As someone who has worked on various projects as a SWE, I understand the intricacies of software development, whether that is frontend development, backend, or just project architecture as a whole. As my previous role as Project Lead, I developed key leadership skills to lead the whole team and have everyone motivated to continue working towards our mission. But beyond that, the community here at Generate is what keeps me coming back. Despite the various backgrounds and the different goals, everyone is both welcoming and motivated, a mix that helps lift me up even when I don’t know what to do. The spread of people who will help you as well as keep you company is what keeps me going in stressful times. Because everyone has the qualifications to be in this prestigious club, you can never underestimate anyone’s capabilities in the slightest, which helps shape what Generate and what it means to me
column24: My time on Apprenta really shaped my path as not just a developer, but a senior in the team. Going into the project, I knew the project stack was going to be different from everything that we have seen before, as the project already had a set backend with Hasura and used GraphQL instead of Rest APIs which I was familiar with. On top of that, we had a team that also had never used any of those frameworks and were generally new to the club as a whole. But I learned quickly how to use everything that was assigned to me, whether that was learning how to develop in this unfamiliar stack or connecting the various tools together. Beyond all of this, I learned how to support fellow engineers, by supporting them with their tickets and having small group sessions where all of us would work together to get our tickets done. This project as a whole made me realize the value of pair programming and how important it is to build together.
column25: My greatest strengths come from my familiarity with Generate, a strong understanding of the code, and the ability to support leads without stealing the spotlight. Because of my time with Generate, I understand the various nuances that come with the club as a whole, which is where I can help when I see cracks forming in teams, whether that’s coming from a lack of technical capabilities or leadership. Regardless of the situation, I know exactly how to apply myself in various situations to be the glue in a team. If the team is struggling to keep up with tickets, I know I can help the engineers out along with my TLs because I have experience with software development. If its something to do with team morale, I know how to lift people up and naturally guide the project lead into the spotlight because I have been in those shoes before. Because I’ve seen so many angles of this club before, I know exactly how to help in any given situation.
column26: One technical skill that I have developed that I am confident in is my ability to design frontend components and structure. Over my time in Generate and in previous internships, I have worked to build my skills with CSS and other various libraries on top of that in order to greatly improve website design and other facets to it.One thing I would like to improve on is seamlessly connecting the frontend and backend with simple but complete APIs. In various projects, there comes a time when we create 10 different endpoints that all serve the same functionality, and so I want to improve on that foresight and understand what the scope of the endpoint should be so there’s no unnecessary repetition. My motivation to learn new technologies and take on challenges comes from the people around me. Because I try to surround myself with very capable and intelligent people, I always have the motivation to keep myself going and try to keep up with their pace. I try to constantly push myself to be the best version of myself, very David Goggins style.
