column23: Generate has meant two things for me: a place for me to grow individually in my technical and soft skills as well as being a place where I can call home, as a community. First, as a place where I have grown as a person, I have learned so much from being in this club. As someone who has worked on various projects as a SWE, I understand the intricacies of software development, whether that is frontend development, backend, or just project architecture as a whole. As my previous role as Project Lead, I developed key leadership skills to lead the whole team and have everyone motivated to continue working towards our mission. But beyond that, the community here at Generate is what keeps me coming back. Despite the various backgrounds and the different goals, everyone is both welcoming and motivated, a mix that helps lift me up even when I don’t know what to do. The spread of people who will help you as well as keep you company is what keeps me going in stressful times. Because everyone has the qualifications to be in this prestigious club, you can never underestimate anyone’s capabilities in the slightest, which helps shape what Generate and what it means to me

Throughout my college career, I have had several real-world software engineering opportunities that have helped solidify my interest in development, build on my knowledge and skillset, and seek advice from coworkers in the professional world. Most recently, I worked as a software engineering co-op at Wayfair. I gained proficiency in several new programming languages and development tools. I worked in an Agile environment, building on my time management, organizational, and communication skills. Most importantly, however, I became comfortable with asking for help and learned when to do so. Software engineering is often thought of as a field mostly comprised of independent work. However, it is crucial to depend on and seek answers from others who have more experience. I am excited to use and improve on these hard and soft skills in future software development opportunities.
column24: Over last summer, I worked as a software engineering intern at Findhelp, a public benefit company that develops software that helps people find the social care that they need. In partnering with hospitals and universities, Findhelp ensures all members of the community have access to their organization's databases. Not only was this my first experience with software development, but also my first time working on technology that directly benefits my community. While I already had a passion for working on software, this experience helped me realize that company missions and values have a deep impact on me, and should always be considered before I decide to join a company. I am excited to use the technical and interpersonal skills I developed during my internship experience to make a more positive impact in a similar way.
column25: I believe that the hardest part of being a software engineer is understanding the software beyond the technical capacity. In other words, recognizing the business value that certain software applications provide. Outside of my software engineering experiences, I grew a lot from my time as a Husky Ambassador Tour Guide. Being a tour guide requires public speaking skills, adaptability, and flexibility. I have been able to apply these skills outside of that position as well. I feel comfortable talking to new people, giving presentations on my work, and problem-solving under pressure. On my tour, I explain my personal experiences working with software, which has allowed me to improve the way I speak about technology to those who may not be as familiar. I believe that if technology cannot be explained in a way that highlights its business significance, it loses a lot of its value, and this is primarily why I stand out. I have experience and practice with switching contexts, jargon, and language depending on my audience, and have improved my public speaking and communication abilities. I believe these skills will help me excel as a software engineer and team member at Generate.
column26: During my experiences with software development, I have learned a lot about asynchronous and synchronous communication between services. At Wayfair, I developed multiple consumers and producers of messages to a message queue, which allows for asynchronous work and more flexibility within a platform. I experienced the entire software development life cycle, working through code design, development, testing, review, and post-deployment monitoring. However, most of my experiences have been in backend work, and I have room to improve on my frontend development skills, specifically with UI/UX. When working on backend code, I often wonder how everything connects to provide a seamless user experience. I am motivated by this curiosity and desire to see the big picture, rather than just the backend portion of the puzzle, and I enjoy gaining new experiences and learning about new tools and languages. I look forward to seeing what I can learn as a Generate developer.

column23: My professional and extracurricular experiences have had a significant role in shaping my growth. Leading DevOps and the development of a cryptocurrency remittance protocol at Xade Finance enabled me to collaborate with diverse teams and deploy large-scale financial systems, allowing me to balance security, scalability, and user needs efficiently. Furthermore, projects such as NAV Tag, Studium, and GuestLink have strengthened my skills in building robust systems, RESTful API development, and real-time verification to ensure security. On the other hand, I’ve been involved in extracurricular activities that primarily involved mentoring folks. During my N.U.in semester at ACT, Greece, I co-founded the university’s cybersecurity club, wherein I helped create an inclusive space for students to explore security topics and organized the first cybersecurity conference and competition. Additionally, my role as a section leader for Stanford’s Code in Place program allowed me to mentor students from various backgrounds, introducing them to core CS concepts and organizing group projects to foster collaboration and improve their programming skills. Overall, I believe these experiences have not only allowed me to develop the necessary technical skills required as a software engineer but also soft skills to communicate, collaborate, and lead within any team effectively.
column24: An experience that has shaped me as a software engineer has been co-founding and leading DevOps at Xade Finance. At Xade, I spearheaded the development of RemmiteX, a cryptocurrency remittance protocol, allowing me to design solutions for complex, real-world financial challenges. I architected and deployed high-performance, EC2-based AWS infrastructure, engineered RESTful API services to facilitate address-less payments via phone and email, and integrated the Web3Auth SDK to enable secure, seamless onboarding for users. Through this role, I learned to balance security, scalability, and usability by building a transaction fallback system that increased sign-ups by 45% and deploying infrastructure on AWS that guaranteed 99% uptime for over 10,000 users. I believe the diverse nature of this experience forced me out of my comfort zone. I collaborated with engineers and designers from across the globe, adapted to shifting requirements and technical challenges, and overall developed key technical and soft skills. Ultimately, this experience taught me the importance of adopting a user-first mindset and the value of different work ethics and collaboration styles. It shaped my approach to software engineering, allowing me to develop and deploy efficient systems that solve real-world problems at scale.
column25: I believe what sets me apart as a software engineer is my ability to incorporate security directly into every project, anticipating vulnerabilities and writing secure logic in my code. This ensures that potential attack vectors are eliminated before they can become issues in production systems, protecting both users and infrastructure. As a result, the code I write is not just functional, but secure against several vulnerabilities. Through my experiences, I’ve developed an adversarial mindset, approaching software with the perspective of an ethical hacker to identify vulnerabilities before they can be exploited. For instance, while developing NAV Tag, a license plate-based toll payment system for 49 states, I implemented a real-time geolocation system using WebSockets to check a user’s location while processing a transaction and reject fraudulent transactions in under 100ms. Furthermore, I’ve been conducting independent security research and have documented and reported critical vulnerabilities such as SQL injection, remote code execution, and cross-site scripting to major organizations, including NASA, Cisco, and UC Berkeley. These experiences have allowed me to develop the skillset to anticipate and resolve critical threats early in the development process and build systems that always remain secure.
column26: A technical skill I’ve developed and am confident in is building secure backend systems, especially RESTful APIs and cloud infrastructure. Through projects like GuestLink and my current role as a SWE intern at Expand smERP, I’ve learned to integrate security measures into functional backend services, making them reliable and safe for users. Furthermore, they’ve also enabled me to work with multiple AWS services, allowing me to build low-latency and high-performance cloud infrastructure. However, a skill I would like to learn is programming in Rust. By learning Rust, I hope to expand my technical skillset for systems programming and improve the quality, performance, and trustworthiness of the software I build. When it comes to learning new things and taking on challenges, I’m motivated to adapt to emerging technologies and the challenges of improving systems by breaking and rebuilding them. As such, my experiences and projects have allowed me to further my passion for building innovative solutions and designing secure and reliable systems. Exploring new frameworks and methodologies puts me a couple of steps ahead and helps me expand my skillset. I’m always looking forward to challenging myself and building things that push my limits.

column23: My experiences across engineering, research, and tech have pushed me to grow as both a builder and a thinker. Most recently I worked on aerospace manufacturing projects where I operated 3D printing systems and coded a robot to automate part scanning, giving me hands-on exposure to advanced mechanical systems and team-driven problem solving. Through biomedical research, I collaborated with doctors to design a catheter aimed at helping patients with blocked coronary arteries. I also created 3D models, contributed to circuit design, and helped refine the prototype to meet real clinical needs. Knowing that the device could improve care for patients made the work especially meaningful and taught me the importance of user-focused design. More recently, I’ve worked on aerospace manufacturing projects, where I built a robotic system for scanning complex parts—strengthening my technical skills and teamwork in high-precision environments. I’ve also contributed to research in educational AI, building a language model that helps students better understand science topics through interactive responses. Each of these roles has helped me grow as a collaborative, mission-driven engineer with a deep interest in combining technical innovation with real-world impact.
column24: One of the most formative learning experiences for me was building a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) language model as part of an educational research project. The goal was to help middle school students better understand science topics through interactive, conversational AI. I worked on everything from preprocessing domain-specific educational content, to designing the retrieval pipeline, to fine-tuning the generation model. It was my first time building something that combined natural language processing, user experience, and real-world impact. What shaped me most wasn’t just the technical complexity, it was learning how to break down a big, open-ended challenge into manageable pieces, iterate through failures, and make design decisions that balanced performance and usability. I also had to communicate closely with both researchers and educators, which helped me see how software engineering doesn’t happen in isolation—it thrives in cross-disciplinary collaboration. That project showed me how powerful AI can be when built responsibly and purposefully, and it made me more confident and excited to take on bigger, more meaningful technical challenges.
column25: What sets me apart is my ability to apply machine learning in meaningful, real-world contexts while collaborating across disciplines. In my research, I designed and implemented a custom language model using Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to support student learning, this deepened my understanding of LLM architecture, data preprocessing, and model evaluation. I’ve also worked alongside both medical professionals and engineers to prototype biomedical devices, which taught me a valuable skill on how to communicate technical ideas clearly and design solutions that serve real human needs. This intersection of AI, and healthcare has given me a unique perspective on how technology can be used thoughtfully and impactfully. Combined with my teamwork skills and curiosity, this blend positions me to contribute effectively in fast-paced, collaborative environments.
column26: One technical skill I’ve developed that I’m confident in is building machine learning models end-to-end—from data preprocessing to model training, evaluation, and deployment. In particular, I designed a custom language model using Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to support student learning, which strengthened my understanding of natural language processing and deepened my experience working with LLMs. One skill I’d like to improve is my understanding of scalable backend systems and cloud infrastructure. I’ve worked with machine learning models locally, but I want to get better at deploying them in production environments where performance, reliability, and scalability are key. What motivates me to learn and take on challenges is the excitement of building something useful. I enjoy stepping outside my comfort zone, collaborating with others, and seeing an idea take shape through problem solving and iteration. That process drives me to keep learning.

column23: I have been a TA for the Mathematics department at Northeastern since January and previously worked as a peer tutor throughout high school. These experiences have taught me how to communicate ideas clearly, collaborate effectively, and support the growth of those around me. Furthermore, my time spent on technical projects, whether collaborative ones like my Calendar project for Object-Oriented Design or more independent ones like my NBA shot analyzer, has allowed me to apply and deepen the technical skills needed for brainstorming and developing my ideas. These experiences have shaped me into someone who values both learning and sharing knowledge. I’ve come to appreciate how much more impactful and rewarding work can be when it’s done with others, whether it's debugging code or brainstorming new ideas. This mix of teaching, learning, and building has pushed me to seek out communities like Generate, where I can keep growing alongside people who share the same drive to create.
column24: A project that has allowed me to dive deeper into being a software engineer is my NBA shot chart analyzer. It was created to visualize player shooting patterns through kernel density estimation, drawing inspiration from platforms such as StatMuse or NBA2K. This project pushed me to explore data cleaning, visualization, and predictive modeling in greater depth while prioritizing a subject I love. I learned how to ask myself the right questions for manipulating my data, iterate through design choices, and use statistical tools to ensure the output is meaningful rather than just visual. Of greater significance, it showed me how valuable it is to combine personal interests with technical skills. Working on something I genuinely cared about kept me motivated through sifting through bugs and documentation. It also sparked my interest in front-end development, as I realized how much more powerful a tool becomes when paired with an intuitive interface
column25: What sets me apart is my ability to blend technical skill with leadership and a passion for building things that matter to me. As the CS Club leader at ABRHS, I led workshops and guided peers through projects, turning shared interests into learning opportunities. At Northeastern, my experience in CS3200 sharpened my collaborative skills through team-based projects, where I designed relational database schemas and adapted to changing technical requirements. My expertise allows me to blur the lines between my work and personal interests to ensure that I can deliver my full efforts to any task. Whether I’m building a calendar tool for a class or a visualization platform for fun, I’m always looking for ways to make ideas tangible and meaningful. What drives me is the excitement of seeing a vision take shape, especially when it’s done alongside passionate teammates. That’s the kind of energy I hope to bring to Generate.
column26: I have developed Python through competitive programming(USACO and ACSL) and personal projects. One aspect I really enjoy about Python is the ease of use for manipulating data and machine learning in general. However, I would like to develop more front end skills such as Javascript through other projects to deploy more complex web applications that are full stack. This, and the idea of bringing my ideas and visions to fruition is what motivates me to learn new things and take on complex challenges. I would like the ability to actually create solutions that I want, and not just imagine them. That drive to keep building and improving is what keeps me excited about CS.

column23: I haven’t been in Generate before, but working on PlateMate, a past Generate client, gave me a glimpse into the kind of environment I’ve been looking for since. I joined after the core handoff from Generate, but even in finishing and iterating on the product, I got to work closely with designers, test ideas, and respond to real feedback. It felt collaborative, low-ego, and focused on building something people would actually use.  At Disrupt Consulting, I started as a developer and now serve as Director of Consulting and Technical Lead. I’ve helped build full-stack platforms for clients, onboard new developers, and work through messy problems with the team. At Forge, we ideated, designed, and built a wellness app to present at a semester-end expo. In both of these experiences, the focus wasn’t just on building fast; it was on building thoughtfully, with people I genuinely liked working with.  Across all of it, I’ve learned how to work on a team, take feedback, and not take myself too seriously. I’m not looking for a team where everyone’s perfect. I’m looking for one that’s curious, collaborative, and okay figuring things out as they go. That’s what Generate seems like to me.
column24: Clearview was the first project where I stopped coding just to make something work and started thinking about what makes something actually usable. I wanted to build a Chrome extension to detect political bias in news articles using sentiment analysis and NLP. The model came together quickly, but getting it to work across cluttered, unpredictable websites, all without disrupting the reading experience, was the real challenge.  I had to solve problems I hadn’t anticipated: parsing inconsistent DOMs, optimizing client-side performance, and keeping things responsive across dynamic layouts. I rewrote the background logic again and again, and kept asking my friends for feedback to figure out what real users found confusing or frustrating. What started as a technical challenge became a design problem and eventually turned into something I was proud to publish. Today, it has 150+ installs and has been used on over 2,000 pages.  Clearview taught me that building software isn’t just about clean code or clever algorithms in isolation. It’s about adapting when things break, listening to users, and being okay with trading perfection for utility. That mindset and a focus on iteration and practicality is something I’ve carried into every project since.
column25: I think what makes me a strong fit isn’t just the projects I’ve worked on, but the environments I’ve worked in. I’ve been part of small, fast-moving teams where the lines between engineering, design, and product weren’t rigid. At PlateMate, I built out new features on top of a Generate-built codebase, working closely with designers to make sure the final product reflected both vision and user feedback. At Disrupt, I’ve led client projects while helping new developers ramp up and contribute quickly. And at Forge, I worked alongside a team to turn a wellness app idea into a functioning product over the course of one semester.  Across all of that, I’ve learned how to listen before building, how to take feedback without ego, and how to move things forward even when no one’s totally sure what the next step looks like. I’m comfortable writing production code, thinking about system design, and figuring out what’s blocking the team. But I also know that none of that really matters if people don’t want to work with you. I care about that part too, and I think that’s what makes me a good fit for Generate.
column26: One technical skill I’ve built real confidence in is backend development. I’ve worked with Python, Go, and TypeScript across multiple projects to build and scale APIs, optimize database queries, and design clean service layers. I’ve gotten comfortable thinking about performance, reliability, and how backend choices affect the whole user experience. It’s where I feel most in flow when I’m coding.  One area I’d like to get better at is frontend architecture, especially managing state across large applications. I’ve used React a lot and can build clean, component-based UIs, but I sometimes rely too much on intuition rather than fully understanding the tradeoffs between different approaches. I want to get better at thinking structurally about frontend design, not just visually.  What motivates me to learn is pretty simple: I like not knowing something and then figuring it out. But more than that, I like it when that learning happens with other people. Whether I’m pairing with someone or helping a teammate debug, those moments push me to ask better questions and see things I wouldn’t catch on my own. That collaborative kind of challenge is what keeps me engaged and excited to grow.

column23: Throughout my life, I’ve been an adventurous individual, rarely afraid to dive into new experiences. This mindset has pushed me toward challenges that have changed my path for the better. As a high school sophomore, I gave up a season of varsity golf to join our FTC robotics team. After quickly learning the ropes, we won the school’s first league championship. The following year I returned as a leader for the team, and ended up winning our second title. Shortly after graduating high school, I had the opportunity to intern under a post-doctorate at the NYU ARPL Robotics Lab. The commitment required that I come into the lab at least once a week, with the lab being three hours from my home. As a result, once or twice a week for two months I made the six hour round trip down to NYU to work with my mentor. Since then he has now become an assistant research professor at WPI and has recruited me to be a student researcher in his lab. Throughout each of these experiences, taking on the difficulties that came with a presented opportunity, helped open the doors to even more experiences and rewards for my career.
column24: This summer, I had spent a month and a half studying abroad in Belgium, working in a team of four on a healthcare based machine learning web application. Throughout my time there, this experience provided invaluable learning as it teached me the fundamentals of complex data analysis and their implementation in machine learning such as cosine similarity and auto-regressive models. However in this experience it was the things which it did not directly teach me that were in my opinion more valuable than the things they did. It was the challenges that came with having to self teach myself how to write routes connecting models, database, and front-end, that taught me how to properly make use of documentation and effectively troubleshoot my errors using the internet. It was the difficulty that came with having to step between conflicting team members and helping resolve their disagreements, that taught me how to emotionally support my teammates and establish trust and unity within our group. It is all these attributes that helped my team and I successfully developed a fully functional machine learning application and helped me become a more seasoned and well-rounded software engineer from it.
column25: Throughout my career, I have worked in a materials science research lab as well as two drone/robotics research labs. Most recently, I have been working towards developing a program which utilizes a neural network alongside UAV dynamics simulation in order to help convert depth image inputs into actionable aerial manipulation that allows the drone to pick up and interact with objects while airborne. From my work within these labs, I have learned how to effectively dissect research papers and documentation in order to adapt novel innovations into the project. Through the countless struggles I faced in trying to implement these technologies I also learned how to deal with failure, understanding the critical difference between giving up, and pivoting in order to find an alternative solution. Additionally, from working in three different research labs, I’ve learned how to thrive in collaborative settings. Whether working alongside researchers, engineers, or other students, I’ve built a strong foundation in teamwork, adaptability, and technical communication. This blend of software experience, systems thinking, and team oriented mindset positions me to contribute immediately and grow continuously as a software engineer.
column26: From both my experiences in Belgium as well as my current research work, I feel I have developed a strong foundation in data analysis and visualization. Having created functional ML models that process real-time data from API calls, from scratch, and now working to implement a neural network using PyTorch, data analysis has become a great skill and passion of mine as I hope to integrate it into many more software applications I work with in the future. Additionally, it is my hope to familiarize myself with system design. I’ve worked on lower-level systems in robotics, where software closely interacts with hardware, but I’m excited to grow my understanding of how large software systems are built and maintained in industry. However, regardless of what I am currently working on, what consistently motivates me to learn new things is the opportunity to make meaningful progress. Whether it's moving a research project forward or building an app that others can rely on, I enjoy diving into challenges that stretch my skill set, especially when they include collaborative problem solving and lead to real world applicable results.

column23: I  haven’t been in Generate yet, but the mission and values strongly reflect how I’ve grown through my past experiences.  As a Software Engineer at Persistent Systems and now a co-op at 10th Inning Ventures, I’ve worked on real products used by real people — from internal tools to athlete-focused platforms. These roles have pushed me to think beyond code: to collaborate, ask better questions, and take ownership of outcomes. Even without being part of it (yet), I’ve lived many of the same values: staying kind, being curious, and doing meaningful work with others. I’d love the chance to keep growing with this community.
column24: One of the most defining experiences for me was building FitGenie, a fitness and nutrition assistant powered by AI. I didn’t just write code — I had to design a system end to end: from planning the architecture, to integrating OpenAI, to handling messy real-world data.  What shaped me the most wasn’t the tech stack — it was the process. I hit roadblocks I couldn’t Google my way out of. I had to think in systems, break complex ideas into small experiments, and keep the user at the center.  It also taught me that the best engineering isn’t about clever code — it’s about building something that actually works for people.  Working solo at first, and then with a team at 10th Inning Ventures, taught me how to give and receive feedback, manage tradeoffs, and ship fast without losing intention.  That project turned me from “someone who can code” into a product-minded engineer who values clarity, collaboration, and continuous learning.
column25: I think what sets me apart is the mix of builder’s mindset and team-first attitude I bring to every project. I’ve worked in fast-paced, real-world environments — both at Persistent Systems and now at 10th Inning Ventures — where I wasn’t just told what to do. I had to figure it out, talk to stakeholders, and make things work under pressure. That taught me to take ownership, stay resourceful, and always keep the bigger picture in mind.  At the same time, I love being part of a team where ideas bounce around, feedback flows openly, and everyone lifts each other up. I’ve been that teammate who helps debug someone else’s issue at 11 PM, who rewrites a doc so it’s easier for the next person, who cares just as much about how we work as what we ship.  I balance technical depth with clear communication, and I show up with curiosity — always asking how we can do things better together. That combo helps me click quickly into teams and make an impact from day one.
column26: One technical skill I’ve really built confidence in is backend development with FastAPI and SQLAlchemy. I’ve used it across multiple projects — from internal tools at Persistent to production-level apps at 10th Inning Ventures. Designing clean APIs, structuring databases, and thinking through real-world edge cases has become second nature. I enjoy the logic and structure of it, but even more, I like how it powers real experiences for users.  One area I want to keep improving is DevOps and cloud infrastructure — especially container orchestration and CI/CD pipelines. I’ve worked with Docker and deployed apps to GCP and Firebase, but I want to go deeper into tools like Kubernetes and Terraform. I know that understanding how software runs at scale will make me a stronger, more complete engineer.  What motivates me most is the feeling of building something real — solving a problem, making something smoother, or helping a teammate. I like challenges because they push me out of the comfort zone where real growth happens. Every project is a chance to level up, not just technically, but in how I think and work with others.

column19: I am on the E-Club Eboard for Fall 2025.
column23: Since starting my journey as a Computer Science major here at Northeastern two years ago, I have had a plethora of experiences that have launched my understanding of this career to whole new heights. After the boot camp that was Object Oriented Design, I learned the value and critical importance of not procrastinating, and to never underestimate the magnitude of errors a programmer can run into. After participating in hair-ripping, stress-inducing hackathons, I learned about the importance of understanding the technologies you are using and developed methods to troubleshoot efficiently and effectively. After TAing Fundamentals of Computer Science 1, I learned how to break problems down to effectively communicate complex ideas to people who had never had a frame of reference to these kinds of issues before. After working with a large corporate software team, I have learned how critical documentation is for understanding legacy codebases, and how to ask effective questions that not only advance your understanding of your own work, but also communicates your willingness to learn about whatever a mentor can teach you. After these various opportunities I have been blessed with, I consider myself a well rounded, effective member of any team I join.
column24: My first hackathon experience was harrowing to say the least. I was in a group of 5: 4 of which had never been to a hackathon before. We decided to use third party api integrations like Google and Spotify, both of which I had never used before, using a tech stack that I was partially unfamiliar with (using Flask for the backend and React Native instead of React). While the learning curve of the new tech stack was relatively easy, using complex developer APIs intended for professional development came with a frustrating onslaught of technical issues, ones that had no easy stack overflow walk throughs. Issues I had never needed to worry about before, like incorrect configuration files, incompatible versions of technologies, and seemingly fabricated compilation errors became a nightmare. I became forced to read each error three times over, looking up every reference and reading every document on how they function together. I can now say I have a far better understanding of these technologies and how they work, and I walked away from the experience with a far better understanding of the process of creating real working applications and the subsequent issues/preparations they need.
column25: With almost anything I create, personally or through school/work, I tend to only really understand what I'm doing if I can relate real world impact. Mindlessly completing busy work projects doesn't click for me, and thus, I tend to seek out the larger picture in everything I do. How would end users interact with this feature or method? What kinda of insights does this aggregation provide? How could this code by used to increase developer efficiency and generally improve a larger codebase? How this code be changed to be compatible with different systems? Where does this all fit in? Even if I can't answer those questions with my (regrettably) limited knowledge, just thinking through the answers provide other interesting pipelines that my brain explores in the larger context of Software Development. In an odd way, my brain feels properly wired to understand and interact with large scale products. In a team of developers, this skill is very useful for the purpose of making sure our solutions are painless in development yet ornate in execution.
column26: Over my various roles- working at my current coop on third party integrations, developing with the Notion API as the Director of Software at E-Club, and my involvement at hackathons- I feel confident and comfortable in my ability to implement backend solutions and working with RESTful API endpoints. I find backend engineering to be the most satisfying part of programming: seeing live data transferred and understood by programs because of the ingenious standard of HTTPS network protocols is like seeing the cogs in a machine turning. Exploring first hand how it worked in large scale projects is what drew me in and excited me to understand it more. In that vein, I have come to realize that transferring data is just part of it: understanding it is even more important. I would like to explore more in the field of ETL pipelines; about how convert and construct that data into something even more impactful and palatable, and the unique challenges that come with working with large data sets. After taking Machine Learning 1, I have gained a new appreciation for the beauty of machine learning algorithms, and crave to see the satisfaction of it working with a real world impact.

column19: I'm currently working as a Network Co-op on campus.
column23: Being fully transparent, I found out about Generate less than an hour ago while at dinner from a mutual friend, and I could not be more elated yet ultimately disappointed. I truly wish I could have pieced together a beautiful application, but unfortunately fate didn't work out that way. Rather than give 4 mediocre responses, I'm going to try to tell you as much as I can before 11:59. For more context, I'm currently a computer engineering student that has lost her sense of direction and has a lot to offer. While I may be more suited to the hardware role experience wise, I believe the projects I have worked on have provided me with the skills to become an excellent software engineer. The unpredictability and often problematic nature of hardware projects have provided me with excellent problem solving skills. Additionally, the teamwork required for said projects has truly shown me how to work well with others. I've gained an understanding of how much you can accomplish with a positive attitude and a little curiosity. Technical skills wise, I've taken and done will in Fundamentals of Computing and Fundamentals of Engineering Algorithms, where I gained experience in Python and C++. column24: In courses such as Algorithms & Data and Artificial Intelligence, I often used LaTeX to format mathematical expressions into clean PDFs for problem set assignments. However, converting handwritten math into LaTeX code was time consuming and disrupted my workflow. After recognizing this challenge, and realizing it likely affects thousands of CS students, I created TextRact, a tool that lets users take a photo of handwritten math and instantly converts it into LaTeX code. I built TextRact by creating a pipeline to process handwritten math data, implementing an encoder–decoder model with attention, and integrating evaluation methods for reliable LaTeX conversion. This project shaped me as a software engineer because it showed me how fulfilling it is to build something that genuinely impacts me and has a real-world application. Since I had little machine learning experience, I spent countless hours learning the necessary concepts and applying them to this project while enjoying every moment. This showed me that working on projects with a real purpose makes the process more exciting and rewarding, to the point where I’m happy to spend my free time on them as hours feel like minutes.

column24: Building Marble Solitaire from the ground up fundamentally changed the way I think as a software engineer. It was the first time I was fully responsible for both the game logic and the user interface, and it forced me to see software as more than just writing code that “works.” I had to consider architecture, user experience, and maintainability deeply—implementing an MVC structure to keep those concerns organized.  The most impactful part was debugging a frustrating move validation bug that seemed invisible. I remember spending hours tracing logic, only to realize that the issue came from a small assumption I had made about the board state. That moment taught me humility and the value of thinking rigorously, not just quickly.  What made it meaningful was how the experience blended creativity and precision—how I could build something interactive, visual, and logical all at once. It made me realize I don’t just want to write code—I want to craft experiences that make sense to people.  Since then, I’ve carried that mindset into every project: design with clarity, test thoroughly, and care about the person on the other side of the screen.
column25: What sets me apart is my experience on both the design and development sides of product creation and the ability to bridge them. column23: My professional experiences shaped my understanding of what effective product development should look like. During my startup internship, I experienced the harsh reality of disorganized workflows, being assigned tedious tasks without mentorship or clear direction. While challenging, this experience taught me invaluable lessons about what I don't want in a work environment: lack of structure, poor communication, and absence of growth opportunities. column24: This summer, I’ve been building LiftOff, a full-stack application catering towards powerlifters and centralizing all their tasks into one application. This project has allowed me to understand the perspective of not only the developer, but the user as well. While building the user profile update endpoint, I initially designed it to send single changed fields to minimize request size. However, I quickly realized that certain data like a powerlifter's competition division depends entirely on their federation affiliation. Users couldn't safely update their division without simultaneously updating their federation, or they'd end up with invalid combinations of athlete information. Rather than forcing users to navigate this dependency and update information in the “correct order”, I created a comprehensive "athlete data" form on the frontend that encapsulates all interdependent information. While this approach requires larger requests with possibly redundant data, it enables proper validation, so users can now confidently update their profile details without worrying about creating invalid states. This experience fundamentally shaped my approach as a software engineer, teaching me that technical efficiency isn't the only right choice. Great software engineering means prioritizing user experience and making thoughtful trade-offs.

As a Software Engineer Co-op at United Electronic Industries, I inclusively worked with my team members to migrate a legacy codebase as well as build cross-platform applications. During this experience, I fostered an even deeper belief in the importance of working with intention and defining identity at an organizational level.  Lastly, by taking on ownership at Disrupt, the Northeastern FinTech Initiative, and rebranding and building the initiative’s website with an improved user experience that increased user engagement and accessibility, I had the opportunity to directly affect the delivery of results and impact of projects.  These past experiences have made me an energetic, driven, and kind person that I believe would fit well in Generate’s community. I am enthusiastic about the opportunity to give these attributes to Generate and to grow more with its members.
column24: As a summer intern, one of my major projects was expanding and improving telemetry in order to provide greater reliability for node migrations. This involved working with complicated data pipelines and debugging in real-time distributed systems.  The primary challenge was initially grasping the full scope of interdependent systems and the data they handled at scale. However, with extensive mentorship from senior engineers and a focus on iterative problem solving, I began to see the paramount importance of clarity in diagnostics, comprehensive documentation, and iterative development practices. I became proficient in refining KQL queries for both performance and readability, which led to a noticeable enhancement in the system's diagnosability.  The key takeaway for me from this project was the approach to technical challenges with a methodical mindset, valuing transparency in all communications, and the continuous pursuit of feedback. Above all, this experience cemented my appreciation for software engineering as a deeply collaborative, iterative, and ever-evolving field. This project's influence persists in my approach to technical work to this day.
column25: I believe that my technical skills, flexibility, and team-oriented experience make me uniquely qualified to succeed in this position and in any team setting. As a software engineer with Microsoft, I have gained significant experience with distributed systems, working on intricate problems at a large scale and level of complexity. I have developed a mastery of big data, algorithmic thinking, and performance-driven optimization. My time at United Electronic Industries brought me a rich experience in cross-platform development and system programming, nurturing an ability to be flexible and adaptable.  I have also worked on teams with other groups at Northeastern on several projects at the FinTech Initiative’s Disrupt. Website optimization was the focus of one of the projects that I led, and the skills that I developed working closely with others to prioritize user needs while being cognizant of technology’s limitations. The technical skills I have acquired are substantial and rigorous, while the skills that I have gained from my other experiences have made me a flexible, effective communicator.  I feel that it is this combination of technical rigor, flexibility, and communication skills that will enable me to deliver results, work with complex situations, and be a strong and positive force in your team.
column26: One technical skill that I have mastered and am proficient in is query optimization for large-scale data processing. However, I still have room to grow in my proficiency with machine learning and artificial intelligence. The main reason for learning new things and taking on challenges for me is my natural curiosity and love for growth. I am excited by the opportunity to expand my knowledge and skills, and I am motivated by the satisfaction that comes from overcoming challenges and making a meaningful contribution to the work of my team and project.

column23: Throughout my work experiences, I have developed a wide range of technical and interpersonal skills. I’ve used Figma to create wireframes, built websites with WordPress, and learned how to communicate clearly and work effectively in teams. These experiences have taught me how to adapt and apply my skills in diverse settings.
column24: One project that fundamentally shaped me as a software engineer was the robot simulation in my C++ class. The project was over 1,000 lines of code and pushed me to understand correct memory management, and type casting. It showed me how much I enjoy low-level programming and problem-solving in C++.
column25: What makes me stand out is the breadth of topics I have explored. I have used JavaScript with htmx, written backend and scraping tools in Python, built systems in C++, and completed coursework in Java. More importantly, I have a deep enthusiasm for learning and constantly seek to improve.
column26: I am confident in my C++ skills, especially with pointers and memory handling. One area I want to grow in is web and mobile development, which I have only touched on briefly. I am motivated by the challenge of learning something new and the satisfaction of seeing how far I have come.

column23: During my internship at Prudent Accounting Services, LLC, I was responsible for designing and deploying the company’s website, handling both front-end and back-end responsibilities. I took it as a personal challenge and didn't really ask for feedback beyond my supervisor, partially because I felt out of place. Most of the team had accounting backgrounds, and I assumed they wouldn’t relate to my coding or design work. Early in the project, I hit a roadblock trying to balance professional tone with engaging design. Since I had spent more time with back-end development, UI/UX was still new to me, and I wasn’t sure which tools or approach to use. Eventually, I asked a co-worker for her thoughts, and that's when I learned she had a minor in graphic design. This experience changed how I think about collaboration. It showed me that support can come from those not directly involved in the same project. People often have layers you won’t see unless you’re willing to reach out. Since then, I’ve been more intentional about inviting feedback and stopped assuming I need to do everything myself just to prove I can.
column24: Last semester, I decided to enroll in a game design class out of curiosity, hoping to explore areas beyond my major. It came as an unexpected bonus in how much it influenced the way I approach software engineering. One of our projects was to build an escape room, which tested my ability to design puzzles that were engaging and intuitive for the player. I initially focused on making the puzzles clever and complex, but during playtesting with classmates, it became clear that what seemed obvious to me as the designer often left players bored and confused. I had mistaken complexity for quality. This experience taught me key principles about user-centered design, which applies just as much to software as it does to games. Through repeated feedback and iteration, I learned to prioritize clarity, usability, and user experience over personal preference. I had to let go of ideas I liked when they didn’t serve the player’s experience. That mindset of designing with the user in mind and embracing feedback is key to how I approach building software. Whether it’s a game or an application, success comes from understanding how people actually interact with what you build and being willing to adapt accordingly.
column25: I was a Patrol Leader in Boy Scouts over a troop of younger boys. I used to believe that leadership meant knowing it all and barking commands. As time went on, I realized the patrol worked much more effectively when I listened to others, asked for input, and made sure each scout had a part to contribute. Some of the most valuable ideas were from the little ones I was guiding, and I realized that leadership is about trust, communication, and mutual understanding more than it is about a position of power. It has influenced my approach to technical work as well. Regardless of whether I am leading or contributing, I try to make sure that everyone in the team is involved so that we can all learn and develop together. I always work to create the same kind of environment I enjoyed in scouts: one where people feel listened to and supported. It's that blend of initiative, collaboration, and people-focused leadership that I strive to bring to every team I become a part of.
column26: I’m confident in web development, having completed two internships that progressively built my skills. In my first internship, I created a website using a browser-based website builder, which introduced me to design and user experience. In my second internship, I expanded into full-stack development, working with Node.js, Express, and RESTful APIs to build dynamic, professional web applications. I want to improve my understanding of data-related skills because data and web development complement each other. Now, I want to improve my understanding of big data and data-related technologies because data and web development are interconnected, complementing each other. Understanding big data will enable me to design applications that handle large-scale information efficiently, and perhaps ultimately a more personalized user experiences. My motivation is simple: smiles. There’s a quiet magic in crafting something of joy, lighting up faces with effortless delight. Each smile is a ripple of connection, a silent thank-you that breathes life into my work. It’s the unseen reward that turns lines of code into moments of happiness, reminding me why I learn, why I persevere, and why I create — to bring a little more light into someone’s day.

column23: Through my co-op experience, and time as a developer at Scout, I have been able to gain knowledge to make impacts on each of those teams. column24: My first time winning a big hackathon of around 200 teams was when I was in a team developing Horizon, a bank-loaning application. column25: I believe my ability to learn and adapt quickly is what sets me apart as a candidate. column26: I’m confident in my full-stack web development skills, and one area I’d like to continue developing is mobile app development. column15: Software Designer on Platnm, Software Designer on Platemate
column23: Generate has been a very important part of my College experience thus far as a way to tap into the software development scene that’s happening all around me at my University. Looking no further than the Generate Slack, there are opportunities for work being posted all the time, a stream of miscellaneous info dumps on software development strategies and libraries, and resume resources at each member’s disposal. More importantly, the teamwork opportunities provide me with collaborative experiences that get me closer to fellow Generate members and could grant me opportunities with future professionals in the Boston area, and in my exact field of study. The more I’ve engaged with Generate as a community, the more I’ve personally benefited and had my efforts pay off. A big example of this is finding people from the club to play soccer with. After chatting around, I was able to fulfill my ambition since the first week of college of finding a team to play with, and the activity continued even after the semester wrapped up with the same people I had met. I truly hope to find more like minds to bond and work with in this next semester at Generate.
column24: One project that shaped me as a software engineer was a hackathon I did at BU. For starters, I came in with an incomplete group meaning, I picked up an extra partner at the event through discussing shared interests, and had to learn to work together effectively with him over the course of the next 12 hours. Due to the time crunch, the division of labor was also very important to get right. The project had to be separated into clear sections (mine was the api integration), each of which could be worked on and updated independently so all of our group could work concurrently before hooking them up together into a fully functional prototype. Finally, I also got good experience in using every resource I had at my disposal. To tackle problems I hadn’t seen before, I leveraged my teammates, stack overflow, the hackathon mentors, and various youtube tutorials all in service of my final goal. Ultimately, we were able to turn in a functioning computer vision shape-identification project at 2 AM, and returned later to the website to improve it to our standards. The project was a microcosm of all my team development experience and challenges.
column25: Returning to Generate after two previous software projects and a co-op in software engineering, I have a good sense of my strengths on a team like this. The attributes that help me most stand out are my creativity, time management, and communication. My co-op experience brought me a fairly similar team setup to that of Generate, with assigned tickets to complete every sprint that gradually improve an overarching web page. While working on these tickets, I applied creativity in coming up with workaround solutions when current implementations were lacking, and in adding improved functionality onto the Figma designs I was given. I stayed on top of schedule by always completing the first 10 percent of a ticket as soon as possible to give myself a sense of how much time the overall task was going to take and plan my sprint accordingly. Finally, I never hesitated to leverage slack to set up some time to clarify with a fellow engineer on how they implemented a section they worked on, or ask a designer if my work was conforming to their vision.
column26: One technical skill I have honed during my time at college is using my resources to cover an area I’m unsure of. When faced with a technique I haven’t implemented before, I have an order of procedures like checking elsewhere within the project for similar implementations that can be retooled, and checking the right internet resource to get myself unblocked. This learning compounds as well, since the process of researching it on my own builds knowledge of React hooks and Javascript methods that I can carry into future projects. I want to come out of this semester with a more well-rounded expertise in implementing backend schemas to expedite my efficiency on full-stack development mostly foregoing the research portion. My biggest motivators for taking on challenges is creative ambition and team spirit. I am someone who is inspired when seeing intriguing media, and projects, immediately jumping to ‘how can I make my own version of this’ or ‘what was successful here that I can apply to my work’. Additionally, having a team full of talent and watching the amazing solutions they come up with always pushes me to contribute my part to allow both of our ideas to shine.

column23: My professional and extracurricular experiences have most impacted my growth in collaborative and communication skills. These experiences have not only shaped how I approach problem-solving but have also deepened my appreciation for the value of a strong, collaborative team environment.
column24: During my AI capstone class I had to work with a team to design and build an AI project for our final. From this experience I learned how important structure, design, and documentation is. One of the biggest things I realized from this project was that there will always be a delegation of tasks, even as a software engineer and different roles will require vastly different skill sets. I mainly worked on the logic and back end of the AI model and contributed to the design of the project, and I realized that laying out a good framework for the project is what enabled the entire team to work in a very organized and efficient manner. The other thing that was important was learning how to write meaningful documentation that wasn't too excessive but still explained the core necessities of the project. I found that these viewpoints/focuses shaped how I approached future group work and how I integrated into software development teams in my academic and professional life. They’ve helped me better understand how to contribute meaningfully to a team beyond just writing code.
column25: My unique value lies in combining deep technical expertise with strong communication and design skills that enable me to deliver high-quality products while elevating team performance. This QA background gives me a more rare perspective among developers: I instinctively write more reliable, maintainable code because I understand testing methodologies from the ground up. column26: One technical skill that I am confident in is Object Oriented Programming/Design. column19: Building a SwiftUI + NodeJS app that uses AI to create and track personalized learning plans.
column23: Leading the development of Clean Plate, an AI-powered food safety and ethics web app, was a key experience in my growth as a developer and leader. Tasked with building a full-stack application in just 24 hours at HopperHacks X 2025, I led a team of three people who had no prior web development experience. I guided them through both frontend and backend development, teaching them the fundamentals of full-stack engineering, API integration, and debugging. We built the app using React, TypeScript, Node.js, and Firebase, integrating the Gemini API for AI-powered food safety assessments. The experience sharpened my technical skills, but more importantly, it helped me develop strong mentorship, communication, and problem-solving abilities under pressure. Placing 2nd in our track was amazing, but the true reward was seeing how my leadership could elevate others while delivering a project I was proud of.
column24: On a long flight to the Dominican Republic, I set myself a challenge to render a single triangle using C++ and OpenGL. It may sound simple, but the process fundamentally shifted how I approach software engineering. I had no prior experience with graphics programming, but I wanted to understand how things worked under the hood and beyond abstractions like React or Supabase. Setting up the environment with CMake, C++ compilers and linkers, understanding shaders, configuring buffers, and finally seeing that triangle appear taught me patience, low-level problem solving, and how to learn independently in unfamiliar territory. I went from struggling with CMake errors for hours to grasping the graphics pipeline and appreciating how complex systems come together from simple pieces. That triangle represented the moment I fell in love with systems programming. It shaped my passion for graphics engineering and inspired me to keep exploring C++, rendering, and eventually building creative tools and visual applications. It was a small project, but it made me want to dive into graphics programming for my career.
column25: Coming into college, I had never written a line of code. I switched majors from film to computer science, and for the first half of my college career, I was often the least technical person in group projects. That experience taught me how to ask questions, stay patient, and learn from others, skills that still guide how I work on teams today. Over time, I grew more confident. I helped rebuild GeoLabs, a real-world education platform now used by schools like Columbia, and later led a team at a hackathon to build Clean Plate, an AI-powered web app. Most of my teammates had no development experience, and I found myself in a role I once relied on, supporting others, breaking down problems, and keeping us moving forward. What makes me effective on a team isn’t just technical ability. It’s that I’ve been on both sides, the person trying to catch up and the one helping others grow. That perspective helps me stay grounded, adaptable, and always focused on shared success.
column26: One technical skill I feel confident in is backend development for both web and mobile applications. I’ve built APIs and backend systems using Node.js, Express.js, Firebase, and Supabase to support real-world projects like educational platforms and mobile learning tools. I enjoy designing clean, scalable systems and connecting the backend with the frontend in a way that feels seamless to users. One area I’m actively working to improve is graphics programming. I’ve started learning OpenGL with C++, and I’m also developing a C# desktop app with a custom UI, which challenges me to think more deeply about rendering and low-level systems. What motivates me most is the feeling of bringing ideas to life. Whether it’s solving a tough bug or seeing something render for the first time, I’m driven by those moments of progress. Learning to code in college taught me how exciting it is to go from not knowing to understanding. That mindset continues to push me to take on new challenges.

column26: I’m confident in backend automation and distributed systems, especially with tools like NATS, Docker, and Kubernetes. column23: During the HackBeanpot hackathon, I was teamed up with a group with much more experience than me, two of whom were in Generate. Initially, I went into this hackathon thinking we would think of a random idea and immediately start developing. Instead, my group led us through a design process I’d never experienced. We worked out our Mongo schemas, created user flows, and one of our members stayed up and created hi-fidelity wireframes on Figma. This approach was much different than my normal plan of jumping straight to coding.  	This experience transformed me from a “coder” to a “developer”. Being in this process really sped up development time and created a more polished project. I realize I lacked this structure in my personal projects and end up with a messy codebase which requires lots of refactoring. Taking a UI/UX class in Seattle has made me appreciate how essential user research and design thinking are for a project’s success. This hackathon has shown me how powerful this collaboration is. This experience made me more aware of what type of developer I want to become: a developer that values user experience and collaboration while being able to robust, scalable code.
column24: Entering my position as CTO (Chief Technology Officer) at Campus, a ride sharing app, has been a pivotal point for me in my journey as a software engineer. Before this, I usually would build my projects from the beginning, where I would decide the structure and make the decisions. However, at Campus, I inherited a codebase that looked completely vibecoded. It contained hardcoded API keys, inconsistent project structure, and poor reusability. Before I could implement any features, first I had to spend time refactoring the code and untangling all the logic within it. After, I implemented a more secure route protection with NextJS middleware integrated with the Firebase Auth. I believe that this experience made me realize that clean code is not just a luxury, it’s a necessity. Inheriting this codebase pushed me from just understanding why maintainability matters to actually having the skill to make it happen.
column25: I have experience in the full stack (NextJS, React Native, Prisma, PostgreSQL, MongoDB), so I would be comfortable in whatever role I’d be placed in and could also work in multiple roles.  	I love to learn, so when there is a new technology I want to know more about, I’m able to do it quickly and efficiently. During the HackBeanpot hackathon, I had to quickly learn how to use React Native, Expo, and a little bit of AWS S3. Since we were under a very tight time constraint I was able to quickly learn how to use these technologies.  	I was also a coding tutor at CodeWiz, teaching many different kids how to code. This experience really helped me with my communications skills, making it more clear and efficient. I was able to explain hard concepts like recursion to kids ages 7-16 in a way that's easy for them to understand. I believe that that’s what helped me become a better teammate in projects as well.
column26: A technical skill I feel pretty confident in would be backend development and creating APIs. What motivates me to learn is that I love to build cool things and to build things people actually use. I love using new frameworks and experimenting with different tools. I also find it extremely satisfying when people use something I’ve built.

column24: My first full-stack project - a Cryptocurrency app - taught me what the development process really is like. I also believe projects like these force you to learn - which I did - and has made me a much better developer because of it.
column25: I can explain anything. Whether it's a volleyball concept or a Python function to a kid or shareholder - I convey things in a way that are easy to understand and will benefit the listener.
