column23: My experience as a SWE intern has shaped my growth not only as a developer, but a problem solver. At my current internship, I was recently tasked to make an end to end LLM workflow. The company primarily uses Python in the backend. While I am experienced with Python, using frameworks like FastAPI and Django was foreign to me. I had to quickly learn how to build REST APIs with FastAPI. Additionally, I had to learn how to work with internal data sources like an S3 data lake and create charts and graphs with D3.js. Through the help of the documentation and my mentor, I got the full stack app up in a week. Overall, this experience allowed me to be more confident working outside of my comfort zone and learn how to break down tasks, seek out resources, and develop faster.
column24: Recently, while building a job application tracking website to help optimize my job search, I needed to allow for resume uploads. I wanted to make sure the uploads were secure, so I researched Amazon S3 buckets. Ultimately, I decided that a private S3 bucket with presigned URLs was the best option for safety. Implementation took some time as I had never worked with S3 buckets before. I had to create a backend implementation that allowed for uploading with presigned POST URLs and PostgreSQL syncing. While challenging, this feature taught me how to be resourceful with documentation and learn how to implement new features with new technology.
column25: There are two aspects that allow me to excel in this role. First, my technical ability has allowed me to create web apps that are both functional and thoughtfully designed. However, I feel as if my mindset is what makes me truly stand out. In SWE, too often than not, people are putting each other down for not understanding a function, not landing that internship, or some other trivial reason. In a team, it is important to understand that everyone brings their own unique skill. A successful team will interweave these skills to create one cohesive unit. If a teammate gets stuck, we should be willing to help and provide our own insights. For me, the collaborative side is just as important as the technical side.
column26: I am confident in my ability to build RESTful APIs and backends. All of my prior experiences as a developer have taught me how to build clean backend systems. However, one skill I wish to learn is system design. More specifically, how to build apps that scale and are maintainable over time. Making high level design decisions is an extremely important skill to have as a software engineer. I am highly motivated by failure. When I do not know something, I take it as a loss and use it as an opportunity to learn. I enjoy the satisfaction of completion after getting stuck on an implementation. This process of learning keeps me engaged as a developer.

column23: During my co-op at Georgia Tech’s CIPHER Research Institute, I worked on a government application where I helped fix bugs using AngularJS. This experience strengthened my skills in TypeScript, HTML, and CSS. I tried challenging myself to solve issues on my own by relying on the foundation from my Web Development class. When I encountered unfamiliar concepts like Subjects and Promises in JavaScript, I looked through online resources such as YouTube to help me. This helped me grow more confident in learning on my own, especially when my coworkers were unavailable or unsure of the solution. At the same time, I learned the value of collaboration. While implementing search tags for the application, I submitted pull requests where my co-workers would give me tips to better style my html. This improved my attention to detail and helped me polish my CSS skills. I also developed my communication skills through daily scrum meetings where I shared updates, asked questions, and discussed my work with others. Overall, this experience taught me how to learn independently, collaborate effectively, and communicate clearly. These lessons have shaped my approach to technical challenges and teamwork, and I’m excited to bring this mindset and skills to Generate.
column24: One project that fundamentally shaped me as a software engineer was the Red Solo 7 game in my Object-Oriented Design (OOD) class. It was the first time I worked on a coding assignment without step-by-step guidance and unlimited freedom which helped me make my own choices. For the first time in a Northeastern class, I spent two full days planning before writing any code. The game involved colored cards sorted into piles, with each card color representing a rule to determine the winning pile. This challenged me to think algorithmically and use new data structures like HashMaps to efficiently track and evaluate the piles. I gained hands-on experience with Java while making key design decisions independently. After completing the project, I realized some of my design choices made the code harder to extend with future features, simply didnt work, or wasnt efficent. These mistakes became valuable learning moments. I had to refine my approach in the following assignment, which taught me what scalable, readable, and efficient code truly looks like. This project helped me grow both technically and as a thinker. It strengthened my planning, design, and problem-solving skills which I hope to bring to Generate.
column25: My co-op at Georgia Tech’s CIPHER Research Institute gave me a strong foundation in AngularJS, TypeScript, CSS, HTML, Java, and SQL. I also gained experience working under deadlines by ensuring all tasks were completed before the end of each sprint. Beyond technical skills, my co-op taught me how to work effectively within a team through daily stand-ups and collaborative problem-solving. In addition, I’ve participated in hackathons like HackBeanpot 2020 and Northeastern’s AI Hackathon, where I strengthened my React skills and teamwork abilities. These events required us to divide tasks, communicate constantly, and give each other constructive feedback. For example, while working on a website project, I suggested my teammate reposition a product info button for visual appealing design. In return, he pointed out functionality issues on my page and offered helpful suggestions for solving my problems and improvement. These experiences have helped me grow as a well-rounded developer who not only writes clean, functional code but also works well with others, gives and receives feedback, and adapts quickly to new challenges. My combination of technical skills, time management, and collaborative mindset make me confident that I’d be a valuable contributor to Generate’s team-focused and fast-paced environment.
column26: One technical skill I’ve developed confidence in is front-end development using Angular, TypeScript, JavaScript, HTML, and CSS. My co-op experience allowed me to apply these skills in real-world settings, helping me build responsive and functional interfaces without much help. A technical skill I’d like to improve is back-end development. While I gained some exposure to SQL during my co-op, I want to strengthen my ability to work with databases and apply object-oriented design principles to create scalable and reliable backend systems. What motivates me to learn new things is a mix of curiosity and a genuine love for computer science. For example, after seeing a friend use animations on their website, I became curious and started learning Framer Motion through YouTube recently so that I can add them to future projects. I also enjoy learning concepts that directly improve my code. In my Object-Oriented Design course, practicing design patterns helped make my game projects more efficient, readable, and scalable—something I found incredibly rewarding. I hope to bring this same curiosity and growth mindset to Generate. I’m excited to keep learning new technologies and applying them to build websites that continuously improve and provide the best experience possible for clients.

column23: Last semester I joined Oasis, which introduced me to the many different skills needed to make a website including JavaScript, React, and Git. Our project was a website that contained a database from Amazon of popular protein powders. We then displayed them in a list sorted by metrics like price or amount of protein per serving according to what the user was looking for. I learned a lot and I am excited to be a part of creating a more complex project with bigger impact. I am currently interning at a chip company and am part of the software team that is working with frameworks to run LLMs on chips. Although this is not a web development related role, I am able to adapt and learn about LLMs and hardware which I don’t have prior experience with through a lot of outside research on my own. I am proactive and able to take learning into my own hands which I apply to all of my educational and professional endeavors.
column24: In high school, I was president of the book club. My best friend (who was VP) and I would send out a weekly newsletter to the people on our email list which contained our thoughts on a book that we had recently read. As senior year came to end, I realized we wouldn’t have access to our school emails anymore, but we both wanted to continue the newsletter into college so I decided that I would make a website for our pieces. Despite having coding experience, I hadn’t made a website before so I wasn’t sure where to start. I found a template on Github which I thought would make things easier. Instead, I realized I wanted to change a lot of things about how it looked so I dug into the code and ended up learning quite a bit of CSS and HTML. Looking back now that I have some other website coding experience I would do things differently, but I am still really proud of it and it showed me how creative of a field computer science can be. (link: https://denimskirty.github.io)
column25: I am extremely collaborative and thrive in working in a team. I enjoy listening to other people and bringing their creative visions to life besides just focusing on my own. In both my high school book club and science book club here at Northeastern I was in charge of social media and making instagram posts for our club. I liked being creative and coming up with interesting visuals on Canva, but more importantly I was always asking for other club officers’ opinions and incorporating their ideas too. I am excited that at Generate there are designers who work closely with the engineers and the end project is the result of the collaboration and skills of both.
column26: Through Oasis and my current internship I have become familiar with Git and am able to independently work on parts of projects without disturbing others. I would like to improve my JavaScript to make websites that are complex and interesting both visually and logically. I also want to learn the tools that are often used with it like Agile and AWS. Having a project that is actually deployed and being constantly used and updated motivates me to keep learning. The thing that drew me to computer science is how it can help people be a part of a community or access a service no matter where they are. I want to be a part of a group that is facilitating this in a positive way which Generate is definitely doing.

column23: My professional experience has impacted my growth because it has provided me the space to be a part of an environment with like minded people who have helped inspire me to continue my career journey.
column24: I worked on a full-stack MERN application with a team of 5, which was a doctor database application for the massachuetts general hospital that allowed doctors to validate CRUD functionality on patient X-ray exams. I was able to learn from my teammates as they each brought a diverse set of experiences to the project. But I was also able to build my agile skills since we worked in sprit and used github projects to manage our tickets. The project in whole, gave me a talking point during interviews and prepared me well for the workplace.
column25: Whenever I am put in a group project, I naturally tend to hold a leadership/project manager role because I want to ensure the project is well planned and organized from start to finish. This set of people skills, but also technical skills has allowed me to be a good collaborator and problem solver in team settings.
column26: A technical skills that I am confident in would be react since a lot of the projects I have worked on have used javascript and react for its front-end component. A skill I would like to improve on would be databases since I've noticed from my experience it can be quite a complex process setting up and connecting a mongo database to a full stack app. What motivates me to learn new things is that fact that technology is changing FAST and to keep up, us swe's need to have a constant learning mindset and ready to tackle new challenges.

column23: Outside of computer science related topics, I am an avid musician. I was classically taught cello and have been teaching myself guitar for the past 2 years. Being a musician taught me perseverance, as I know that hard work will be rewarded in some way in the end. Along with this, I am an avid gym go-er and runner, both of which have also taught me about self discipline, as well as the consequences of slacking off. I’m also a lifeguard at my town beach, and have been one of the head guards the past year. As all my work is in teams, I have had to form effective communication skills to make me and my fellow coworkers all know about the task in hand.
column24: A project that has fundamentally shaped me as a software engineer is the first real project I made, that being a virtual guitar I built my junior year of high school. This project introduced me to working with audio files, file selection, and designing intuitive user controls. While basic, this project introduced me to new coding strategies and concepts that I had to teach myself. This project was really what got me into coding, and has shaped how  approach problems and find solutions
column25: As I mentioned beforehand, I’m one of the head lifeguards at my town beach, which has taught me effective teamwork and communication skills. Outside of this, I’m a relatively fast learner, and I’m not afraid to admit that I’m wrong or don’t know something. I am willing to learn whatever I need to help those around me also succeed, even if it means taking more time to learn new things.
column26: I am pretty confident in my debugging ability, knowing/ being familiar with multiple languages and frameworks like Bootstrap and Angular, however I really want to work on my code efficiency, though I haven’t taken Algo yet. I am motivated to learn when I have new challenges to face. I like to have something I know I have to figure out, and I enjoy the process of getting there. Along the way, I know I will also pick up secondary learnings, which opens doors for new challenges and learnings in themselves.

column23: I recently wrapped up my first software dev co-op at Johnson & Johnson, and I grew a ton from the experience. The biggest takeaway was how to collaborate on large-scale projects. My technical skills grew a bunch, and I became proficient in a lot of web technology I think would be super useful at Generate (Angular, TypeScript, HTML/CSS, Node). I learned good project hygiene like documentation and clean version control. Most importantly, I learned how to work well with others on technical projects. I became really close with my mentor and learned well from the other full-time engineers on my team.   In terms of extracurriculars at Northeastern, I’m pretty active at the fencing club here. I made some of my closest friends in college, and it’s been cool to be part of a community here. I’m really hoping Generate can become another one of those for me. More than anything, applying to Generate is a way for me to find people with similar interests to me.
column24: The most growth I’ve experienced as a software engineer occurred during my first co-op. Transitioning from personal projects and the classroom to production code was very daunting. I was worried that whatever I produced would break something and set my project back. I worked hard with my mentor over the first month to get over those fears and soon enough I was landing a lot of code onto our project and started to feel like a valuable, independent member of the team. Getting over that fear and realizing I could make a big impact on my team fundamentally shaped my growth as a software engineer, and I still carry the confidence I gained from that experience today.
column25: Having worked on personal projects and a co-op in full-stack development, I have the technical skills to immediately contribute to projects at Generate. I’m familiar with most popular web frameworks and would need little ramp-up to start contributing. More importantly, I’m just super interested in being part of Generate. A lot of the people I’ve met at Generate have been super nice as well as strong developers. I think I’d get along great with the type of people Generate attracts. It’s my top choice for a software club at Northeastern, and I’m super motivated to contribute to projects and meet new people.  I think I stand out by being a uniquely good fit for Generate. My technical skillset is super relevant to the projects Generate works on and based on the people at Generate I’ve met, I think I’d get along well with people there.
column26: What I’m most proud of as a software engineer is my ability to develop software that’s easy to collaborate on. I take pride in developing new, maintainable code and refactoring old messes to a workable state. I find it satisfying to make my own and other developer’s experience in a codebase more enjoyable.  In terms of new skills, I really want to learn more devops / infrastructure work; things like CI/CD pipelines or more in-depth work with Docker. I think those are really valuable skills to know and right now they’re basically magic to me.   I got into software development as a career, because I was really drawn to the crazy amount of things there are to learn. I wanted a field where I could continue to develop as a professional and be rewarded for it. That sense of progress and growth is really important to me, and there’s so much of it in software. It’s the same reason why I continue the competitive sports I practiced since I was young. Now that I’m in college, it’s less about the results, but I love the feeling that I keep getting better and that there's more to learn.

column16: Instagram, been interested in Generate for a while

column23: I haven’t been in Generate, but through my experiences at Bose Corporation and Quest Diagnostics, I’ve not only learned about building technical skills through real work, but also how important communication, organization, and teamwork are. Working on cross-functional projects that improved internal workflows and supported larger business goals helped me see the value of contributing to something bigger than myself. Outside of those roles, I also participated in HackBeanPot, where I formed a team of people with different backgrounds and levels of experience to build out an app idea I’ve been wanting to work on. We didn’t finish the whole thing, but I’m proud of what we got done and the leadership skills I built as the main point of contact for technical issues. These experiences have pushed me to step outside of my comfort zone, speak up more, and take initiative even when I’m not 100% confident. I've grown more comfortable with ambiguity, learned how to adapt quickly, and realized how much I enjoy working with others toward a shared goal and being someone people can rely on for help. I’m excited to keep building on that growth and continue learning from the people around me.
column24: At Quest Diagnostics, I worked on an automated instance scheduler designed to reduce cloud costs by stopping unused instances during off-hours. Even though I worked on the project on my own, I realized that it wasn’t just about writing code but rather understanding the company needs and adapting the tool accordingly. Throughout the project, I collaborated with multiple teams: the UI/UX team helped make sure the interface was on-brand and was intuitive for users, the DevOps team shared how automation like this eliminates manual work and improve reliability across environments, and the CloudOps team provided context on operational edge cases such as teams working in different time zones. In the end, I presented the finished product to the business side of the company and explained how the solution could be applied to other cloud platforms to save even more on operational costs. This project fundamentally shaped me as a software engineer because it was the first time I took full ownership of a product from start to finish, handling everything from implementation to communication and delivery. It pushed me to think beyond just solving a technical problem and taught me how to work cross-functionally and design with real users in mind.
column25: With a combination of working on a team with many experienced engineers during my internship/co-op, and a team with much less experience during a hackathon, I’ve developed the ability to communicate clearly, adapt my approach, and support others no matter where they’re starting from. Being in both roles (someone learning and someone leading) has helped me understand how to mediate, listen actively, and keep projects moving forward. I have a drive to solve everyday problems through technology which fuels my passion for developing software. I’m excited by opportunities where I can keep learning, but I also hope to contribute meaningfully through code, feedback, or helping someone else level up. I think that mix of empathy, curiosity, and initiative is what sets me apart.
column26: A technical skill I’m confident in is Swift as I’ve spent a lot of time working in it, especially through personal and professional mobile app development projects. One area I’d like to grow in is backend development. Since I often come up with ideas for apps or projects I want to build, I’m motivated to explore different tech stacks so I can bring those ideas to life from end to end. I’m especially interested in eventually launching my own app or even starting a company. Since startups are usually small, I want to be involved in every part of the process, meaning not just the front-end which I am the most comfortable in, but also the backend, infrastructure, and product decisions. This goal pushes me to take on challenges and learn new things, even if they’re outside of what I already know.

column19: No additional commitments at this time.
column23: While I haven’t been a part of Generate yet, I've grown significantly through projects and coursework. Building Redline, a full-stack social platform for car enthusiasts, taught me how to turn an idea into a real product by designing scalable backend APIs, managing auth and storage with Supabase, and creating a solid frontend with React and Tailwind. I also contributed to Code for Boston, where I collaborated with other developers on civic tech tools, gaining experience with real world codebases and version control in a team setting. These experiences have helped me grow as both a developer and a teammate, showing me how much I can learn by building with others.
column24: Working on Redline really helped me grow as a software engineer because it pushed me beyond writing algorithms and into building a full, working product. I had to think not just about writing code, but about how users interact with the app, how it looks and feels, and what features actually matter. I also had to make real decisions about data modeling and user security. It taught me how to balance functionality, user experience, and reliability all at once.
column25: I have a solid foundation in multiple languages and in both frontend and backend development, but I think what really sets me apart is how much I enjoy working in a team. I love collaborating with others to solve problems, and I learn best when we’re all contributing ideas and building off each other’s thinking. I believe that great software comes from strong teamwork (iron sharpens iron) and I always come out better after working with people who challenge and inspire me.
column26: This summer, I focused on learning TypeScript and how it fits into full-stack development. I took the time to understand how it works with frameworks like React and Node, and applied it to a real project by building Redline, my car enthusiast social platform. One area I’m excited to grow in is machine learning and AI, which is why I’m taking related classes this fall. I’m motivated to learn new things when I can clearly see their real-world potential, and I enjoy pushing myself to explore areas outside my comfort zone.

column23: My introduction to coding came during the summer of my sophomore year of high school through a program called Operation Sparks. While I was reluctant at first, I decided to sign up as I was free during the summer and thought it could be valuable to learn a new skill. That decision changed everything. From building simple games like Pong to understanding how websites function behind the scenes, I was hooked by the creativity and logic coding offered. From there, I decided that I wanted to major in computer science and dive deeper into the realm of coding, where the learning opportunities are endless. From writing “Hello World” for the first time to learning complex algorithms in classes and making a personal project this summer, my knowledge has only expanded. More than just learning to code, I’ve learned how to problem-solve, how to think systematically, and how to work through frustration. These experiences have shaped me not just as a student, but as someone excited to contribute to real-world projects. I’m now eager to join communities like Generate where I can apply what I’ve learned, collaborate with others, and continue growing both technically and professionally.
column24: I made my first personal project this summer. It was a Spring Boot To-Do List API. Since it was the first time I made a real project by myself, I learned a lot. When I started, I thought that software engineering included just writing code that works but as I kept on building, I realized its a lot more than that. It’s about building reliable systems and organizing code all in a professional manner. I initially just wanted to put everything in one class, but after looking at tutorials, I began to understand the value of separating concerns. Implementing dedicated layers for controllers, services, and repositories made the codebase cleaner and easier to manage I also learned the importance of thoughtful error handling, input validation, and returning meaningful HTTP status codes. These details made me realize how critical it is to think from the user’s perspective and ensure clarity and reliability at every layer of the application. This project gave me a strong foundation in backend development. I’m excited to bring this mindset into a collaborative setting like Generate, where I can continue learning and contribute to real-world projects with impact.
column25: One experience that positions me to excel in this role that requires technical and collaborative skills is when I was a Tech and Website Team Member for the PawHacks club. Me and my team played a key role in organizing the online hackathon that was going on at the time. I helped shape the website for participants by contributing to the platform content and ensuring everything was running smoothly. I was able to quickly adapt to the challenges, communicate with the team, and meet tight deadlines while also staying on top of my class assignments and tests. Whether it was updating information on the website or meeting up with the team to make sure everyone was on the same page, I grew more confident in my technical skills as well as problem solving. This experience showed me that I thrive in a team environment where I can not just contribute to the project, but also learn from others. I would like to bring this same mindset to a team like Generate.
column26: One technical skill I’ve developed and feel confident in is backend development using Java and Spring Boot. This summer, I built a full To-Do List API from scratch, which taught me how to design layered architectures, follow RESTful principles, and implement clean, maintainable code. I now understand how different backend components interact and how to build systems that are both reliable and professional. One skill I’m actively working to improve is frontend development, especially with frameworks like React. While I’ve had some exposure to JavaScript and HTML, I want to get better at creating interactive user interfaces that connect smoothly with backend APIs. What motivates me to learn new skills is the ability to bring my ideas to life in a meaningful way. For example, I’m excited about a project idea where users can upload an image and receive a song that matches its vibe, something useful for people looking to pair music with photos for Instagram. The more skills I learn, the closer I get to making ideas like that a reality, and that’s what drives me to keep learning and take on new challenges.

column23: This past fall, I was given two choices: move to Chicago, where I know nobody and have never even visited, to pursue what would be my dream co-op (working as a software engineer at a well-respected financial institution) or stay in Boston with all my friends and where I’m comfortable and work at a, stll-exciting but not exactly what I want to do in the future, biotech company as a software engineer. I chose the former. I knew that I had to choose to move away because it was the choice that terrified me. Move to a brand new city for a job I didn’t even know I would enjoy. I believe it is these decisions that make me who I am, someone who is constantly chasing discomfort to achieve more than I think is realistic. Now I’m not sure if these choices give me an advantage, but I know they certainly put me in spots to learn things that I wouldn’t necessarily get otherwise.
column24: During my last co-op at CIBC, I worked on creating an application for a business team within the bank. My first demo for the business was about 6 weeks into development, enough time to get a good layout of the application, but certainly not enough time to deliver a finished project. When I showed the business team, they appreciated the work I had done, but also made sure to mention this was not the vision they had for the application. This project taught me one of the most valuable software engineering lessons I've learned this far: build your software for the user. I had built something that I would like, not what the business would like.
column25: I am a rising junior with next-to-no project work that was a part of a group. During my co-op, I was able to experience just a taste of what it was like to work as a team and realized that it is a different way of programming than when you are on your own or in pairs for some assignment or personal project. So, coming in, I believe I would bring experience and maturity to my team with the confidence to navigate any challenge. However, I am also eager to get my hands dirty with my team to solve these difficult challenges that we will, without a doubt, encounter.
column26: I think my greatest technical ability is being able to put my code into English. Although this seems fundamental to any software engineer, I believe it often goes overlooked. At the end of the day, you are only creating a part or component of the entire package, so you need to be able to work with others to ensure that everyone is on the same page to result in a smooth overall program. On the other side, I believe that I have a lot to improve in my technical abilities when it comes to working on larger projects. I have only ever gotten to work on smaller-scale projects during my time at Northeastern, and even for most of my first co-op. So, the chance to gain some experience and comfort level in that area, I believe, would vastly improve my technical abilities.

column23: My professional experiences have impacted my growth by exposing me to a variety of Computer Science (CS) applications and helping me figure out what I want to pursue. In high school, I started connecting with startups and local companies to gain work experience and learn more about CS. Over the past few years, I've had the amazing opportunity to work with a variety of different organizations, where I've contributed to various projects across different fields, gained many new skills, and established a supportive network of mentors. My extracurricular experiences have helped me supplement my academic and professional journey by connecting me with my peers and letting me learn in more relaxed, collaborative environments. I recently took on the role of Event Lead for ACM at Northeastern, and I'm currently involved with CS-related volunteering work over the summer to help the community and hone my skills in a more informal setting. I also recently got connected with Professor Intille, where I work to analyze data relating to loneliness at Northeastern. Through these activities, I've fostered a strong passion for CS and gained soft skills that will help me in the future.
column24: My current role as a Software Engineer at Stateable has fundamentally shaped my entire perspective on Software Engineering by introducing me to new skillsets and motivating me to work with software in the future. Before this internship, I had never been exposed to anything software-related. I actually originally joined as a Data Scientist because it was the field I was most familiar with. After working here for a few months, my role was extended from managing data to working on the software directly, which automates document parsing for commission statements. On top of that, I also created an in-house software that automates sales outreach using automation workflows and LLMs. I worked a variety of platforms, including Retool, n8n, Lemlist, Linked Helper, and HubSpot, and gained a strong proficiency in JavaScript. Through my work here, I learned a lot about UI/UX design, software systems, and backend programming. I realized that being a Software Engineer would allow me to create processes that anyone could use, work with the latest tools, and combine knowledge from multiple fields of CS. This learning experience ultimately introduced me to Software Engineering and encouraged me to change my pursuit to software engineering.
column25: My former and current involvement in extracurricular leadership roles position me to excel as a Software Engineer at Generate. In high school, I served as my high school's Marching Band Head Drum Major, where I acted as a student leader during rehearsals and competitions, and the TED-Ed club president, where I facilitated club meetings and in-school public speaking events. Now, I currently serve as our one of our school's ACM Event Leads, where I plan weekly events, workshops, and Hackathons, and a Speaker Coach for TEDxNU, where I will work with a speaker to help them develop their TED talk. Through these roles, I learned how to work well with teams, communicate effectively, meet deadlines, and complete tasks efficiently. I believe my leadership experience also reflects my sense of commitment, as it shows how I dedicate a lot of time and effort to clubs and organizations I get involved with, and try to contribute as much as I can by taking on additional responsibilities.
column26: One skill I'm confident in is my ability to figure things out on my own. I can pick up new skills quickly and complete tasks without too much guidance. For example, a few weeks ago in my current job, my boss introduced me to a platform I've never used and told me to build something. After playing around with it for a day, I was able to complete the project without any guidance on specific implementation. One skill I'd like to improve on is workload management. I have a tendency to overload myself with work and get overwhelmed later. I sometimes feel the irrational need to prove that I can handle a lot of responsibilities. For example, when I first started at Stateable, I'd give myself unreasonable deadlines to complete my tasks. Eventually, I realized this and simply opted for saying, "I'll get it done as soon as possible." What motivates me to learn and challenge myself is the joy I get out of reaching a new milestone. I find it rewarding and exciting to create something new and have hard work pay off. Especially in the world of Computer Science, there's constantly new technology being released, and I want to be as up-to-date as possible.

column23: I haven’t been in Generate before, but starting Burrow Housing and working at MeasureOne have shaped my growth. At Burrow, I helped lead a dev team and build our MVP, which made me more confident in working with cross-functional teams and scoping real-world problems. At MeasureOne, I learned how to ship reliable software by building APIs and automations that handled thousands of users. These experiences pushed me to become more adaptable, curious, and collaborative—qualities I’m excited to bring to Generate.
column24: One project that really shaped me was building an AI-based document parser at MeasureOne. I worked on integrating GPT, Selenium, and automation tools to clean up parsing errors. It was the first time I tackled both backend challenges and real production bugs. I learned how to balance speed with reliability, how important data validation is, and how small improvements can impact thousands of users. That experience made me fall in love with solving messy backend problems.
column25: I bring a mix of startup hustle and technical depth. I’ve led dev sprints, made tech stack decisions, and also written production-ready code. My background spans cloud infra, automation, and product thinking, so I’m comfortable switching between roles. I love building things that solve real problems.
column26: I feel confident designing AWS cloud infra using Terraform—building out VPCs, EC2, and automating pipelines. I’d love to get better at Kubernetes because it’s key for scaling projects. I’m motivated by real impact—seeing something I build actually help people keeps me curious and driven to learn more.

column23: In my two years at Northeastern, my experiences have spanned research, teaching and internships, and span several domains. I’ve studied algorithms and logic through intensive coursework and self-exploration. I’ve been researching AI/ML since high school, and at college, I’ve even had the chance to build a full-scale RL model that worked on real-life financial data and performed dynamic hedging for options trading. I am currently working in data analytics for my co-op, and I have been involved in a few full-stack projects, mostly with backend and database integration work.   All this breadth in my experiences has really equipped me with the right skillset and confidence to explore new technologies and unchartered territory on my own. I also spent so much time trying to “take it all in” that I lost touch with what initially drew me to CS: I like building! It’s why I’ve refocused to Software Engineering and spent the summer really diving into personal projects, full-stack tools and trying to work on making products, even small functional ones, so I can enjoy myself. It’s what excites me about Generate! I want to interact and learn from people who have insights into thoughtful design and meaningful impact.
column24: I love planning! Frameworks, outlines and bullet lists. However, when I had personal project ideas, I would fixate on details such as knowing the most appropriate tech stack, future scalability and originality. Weeks of obsessing without any written code and procrastination turned into analysis paralysis! Eventually, I had to confront my inability to start.   That’s when I turned to writing. In creative writing, another indulgence of mine, it is often said that first drafts are meant to be bad and their primary purpose is to simply exist. Later drafts allow for refinement. And sometimes, it is important to write out the brute-force solution to know your baseline and then improve on it. And I think I learnt that I don’t need to know every little detail before starting. Famous applications too, have newer versions, updates and bug fixes, so I needn’t get it perfect the first time. Even if I fall short, I will always have the opportunity to improve, a never-ending, iterative process indeed. But for that, I should just start! And I did. And I don’t necessarily have everything figured out yet, but so far, I’ve really started to enjoy the process as much as the project.
column25: I will bring a blend of strong technical foundations and deep interpersonal skills, and it’s the intersection of the two that makes me a fit for Generate’s fast-paced, collaborative environment. I have a solid grasp on foundational CS concepts like algorithms, programming languages and object-oriented design, and I take pride in writing clean, efficient and thoughtful code. I enjoy working where logic, syntax and structure make a difference because it emphasizes understanding.   At the same time, I’m a people person! I’ve worked as a TA for introductory CS classes multiple times and it’s a very rewarding experience. It adds dimensionality to my love for teaching and learning. I’ve found it very helpful in walking other students through their user’s experience using design flowcharts, talking through different testing scenarios and helping them debug through extensive code reviews (rubber ducks were involved).   That experience has definitely sharpened my ability to communicate with other people and effectively collaborate when large codebases are part of the equation. In fact, pair programming was one of my favourite teaching models where we’d learn so much from each other, and I think it’s that level of collaboration that I truly aspire to integrate into my work.
column26: One technical skill that I’m confident in is algorithmic thinking. I have spent a lot of time honing my understanding of data structures, problem-solving techniques and optimization strategies, mostly through graduate-level coursework, projects and research. I enjoy breaking down problems, analyzing underlying patterns and designing mathematical approaches to solutions about runtime, structure or design, and the result is often empowering. My need to work near a whiteboard says it all! : )  I’d love to improve my ability to apply algorithmic problem-solving to real-world software engineering. It’s funny, I know so many interesting mathematical problems, yet I rarely encounter situations in code that demand solutions like those. I want to get better at identifying where my strengths in logic and math will be significantly helpful. I want to practice going from idea to solution and flowchart to functional code so I can work on real-world solutions, not just hypotheticals on paper.   What motivates me is the feeling that I am working on real solutions that are making a difference. I love the challenge of turning abstract concepts and streams of thought into working products that matter, and it reminds me why I love this field. It’s about innovation in every sense.

column23: My professional and extracurricular experiences have shaped my growth in many areas, especially in my curiosity, collaborative skills, and innovative problem-solving. My curiosity has driven me to embrace challenges outside my comfort zone - from throwing myself in a completely new field, to finding different ways to deepen my understanding of existing projects. Despite entering college as a Physics major, I decided to pursue Computer Science and a full-stack software engineering internship with the goal of developing new skills, and understanding the intricacies of a different field. Having this mindset has pushed me to continuously pursue new learning opportunities - a mindset that I believe has made me a more versatile engineer.  Working with others has also been central to my growth, whether through collaborating with full-stack development teams at GoPomelo and my current co-op, brainstorming ideas for various photography shoots, or organizing fundraising events for Operation Smile Thailand. I value these experiences as I believe in the benefit of leveraging different perspectives and skillsets to achieve a goal.   A growth mindset, combined with these professional and extracurricular experiences, has inspired me to seek out different opportunities: situations where I can both learn from others, drive innovation, and hopefully contribute something meaningful.
column24: At my previous co-op at Sparx Hockey, I conceived, implemented, and deployed a web application designed to streamline troubleshooting procedures. Despite having no prior experience with .NET or large-scale application development, I embraced the challenge through rigorous independent learning and continuous exploration. This experience proved to be a great growth experience because it immersed me in the complete software development lifecycle, compelling me to consider how each design decision would align with project specifications while thinking creatively about optimal feature implementation. I leveraged these skills to develop a restaurant management tool tailored specifically to my father's business workflow and operational requirements. Witnessing how software engineering can address tangible, real-world challenges initially drew me to the field, and observing the positive impact of these solutions continues to fuel my passion for building meaningful solutions to various problems.
column25: Growing up across three continents exposed me to diverse cultures and instilled a fundamental appreciation for learning from others' perspectives. During my backpacking travels, I developed the habit of capturing pieces of wisdom on postcards - whether advice from strangers or insights from new experiences - cultivating a growth mindset that continues to guide my approach today.  This perspective-seeking translates directly into my professional development through continuous adaptation and interdisciplinary application. At Sparx Hockey, I seamlessly integrated engineering principles into programmatic solutions despite having no prior .NET experience. Similarly, at my current quant firm, I leverage financial knowledge to enhance my problem-solving capabilities. My eagerness to understand diverse viewpoints has fostered a solution-oriented mindset: I founded a content creation company after deeply understanding F&B businesses' struggles, and built a restaurant management application tailored specifically to my father's operational needs.  What distinguishes me is my ability to leverage knowledge from varied environments and experiences. My multicultural upbringing has cultivated adaptability and resilience, while my curiosity consistently drives me toward challenging, unfamiliar territories. This combination of global perspective, interdisciplinary thinking, and avid curiosity enables me to approach problems with fresh insights and build meaningful solutions that bridge different areas of knowledge.
column26: A technical skill I’m confident is full-stack API development. My co-op experiences and personal projects have allowed me to develop web applications, APIs, and UI layers according to different business requirements. This end-to-end development experience, from database design to frontend implementation, has given me confidence in architecting scalable systems.  A skill I would like to improve on is cloud architecture and infrastructure management. While I've worked with Microsoft Azure and Google Cloud Platform for specific features like blob storage and billing systems, I want to deepen my understanding of designing cloud solutions. I’m particularly interested in understanding how to best implement CI/CD pipelines, and leveraging cloud technologies to develop and deploy ML applications.  My recent experiences in the software engineering realm have opened my eyes to the depth and complexity of these technologies and how they can be applied to find creative solutions to previously unsolvable problems. Attending educational seminars where speakers discussed leveraging ML and cloud technologies for sophisticated investment research and trading strategies exposed me to just how powerful these tools can be. I’m motivated to learn because of genuine curiosity, because I strive to continuously grow as a developer, and because I enjoy the challenge of combining skillsets and technologies to solve real-world problems.

column19: IT Tech Support Intern at Harbourvest Partners
column23: I haven’t been in Generate before, but I’ve had the opportunity to work and volunteer in a variety of fields. This includes test prep tutoring, daycare assistance, and political canvassing which have all helped me grow as a communicator and team player. These experiences taught me how to convey information clearly, listen actively, and stay patient in high-pressure or unpredictable environments. While I have obtained important soft skills in my previous experiences, I am now looking to hone my hard technical skills. My current co-op at HarbourVest Partners as a Technical Support Specialist has helped me understand the value of organized teamwork and clear communication in a professional setting. I’ve worked on company-wide system upgrades, device deployment, and help desk support, all while learning how to ask the right questions and collaborate across departments. I am excited to join the Software team in Generate and I am confident I will be able to make an impact both as a teammate and a developer.
column24: In my first year here at Northeastern, I attended HackBeanPot which was my first hackathon as a college student. I walked into the venue not knowing anyone and feeling extremely nervous. The project that I worked on at this hackathon was a website called Hobby Harbor which played into the theme of the event which was the vastness of the ocean. This website aimed to provide an accessible way to discover new hobbies. This was my first time developing a full-stack project in such a short timeframe and I learned a lot that weekend. Beyond the technical learning, the most memorable part of the experience was meeting new people and attending workshops. I had always seen software engineering as a solo pursuit, but HackBeanPot showed me the importance of collaboration. Since then, I’ve approached every project with an open mindset, eager to learn from others and contribute meaningfully. This project not only taught me important web development skills, but it also changed the way I approached software development entirely.
column25: I bring a unique combination of technical and creative experience thanks to my combined major in Computer Science and Game Development. One of the most valuable lessons I’ve taken from my game design courses is working within an Agile scrum environment in a realistic studio setting. This experience taught me how to communicate clearly, give and receive feedback, and deliver on tight deadlines as part of a team. On the technical side, I’ve completed coursework found in Northeastern’s CS track, including Object-Oriented Design, Algorithms & Data Structures, and Programming in C++. I also have experience with a range of programming languages and tools, and I pick up new ones quickly. If a project at Generate calls for a technology I haven’t used yet, I’m confident in my ability to learn it fast and contribute meaningfully from the start. What sets me apart is not just my technical background, but my interdisciplinary training and real-world team experience. This will allow me to bridge creative and technical work while staying collaborative and adaptable.
column26: One technical skill I’m confident in is designing and implementing software using object-oriented principles. I’ve developed a strong understanding of design patterns in OOD and how to apply them to build modular, maintainable code. Languages like Java, Python, and C++ have become second nature to me as I’ve used them across different projects and assignments, and I take pride in writing code that’s both effective and well-structured. One area I’m eager to learn more about is machine learning. As AI continues to become more influential in our day to day lives, I think gaining hands-on experience with ML concepts and tools would allow me to better understand and contribute to future technology.  What motivates me to learn new things is the excitement of overcoming challenges and seeing tangible improvement. I’ve always found a lot of satisfaction in tackling challenges I once found difficult, and software engineering provides endless opportunities for that kind of growth. Whether it’s debugging a strange bug or learning a new framework from scratch, I’m driven by the reward of measurable progress and the belief that every challenge is a chance to improve myself.

column23: I’ve grown the most when I learned alongside other people. On a two‑person Java calendar project, we met twice a week to demo and explain our code, then re-split tasks to keep a shared understanding of the whole system.. As an AP CSA Course Assistant, I reinforced that mindset by guiding students with questions instead of answers and meeting weekly with teachers to adjust plans for better understanding. Outside of tech, working retail at Trader Joe’s taught me to stay adaptable, kind, and outcome‑oriented in a fast-paced environment. Carrying that habit of collaborative growth forward, I’m ready to learn, support, and contribute meaningfully with Generate.
column24: In a two‑person team, we built a calendar application in Java using Swing with interactive, headless, and GUI modes so we could both validate behavior and make it usable. I owned the calendar creation/edit flows, the command‑line view, and the controller layer that routes CLI/GUI inputs to the model and updates the view, including --mode handling and the interactive/headless flows. Working together is what made the project succeed: we agreed on clear boundaries between input, controller, model, and view so we could build in parallel without stepping on each other. We alternated between who was driving and who was navigating on difficult issues, kept short daily syncs to surface blockers and reprioritized scope, and when we disagreed we discussed the trade‑offs and chose the simplest option that allowed the other person to continue to be productive. This turned individual effort into consistent progress. It also taught me the importance of clear, timely communication on a team.
column25: I bring a mix of builder and educator with a service mindset. On the engineering side, I have completed Java GUI projects and I am comfortable working across model, view, and controller boundaries. I document decisions so teammates move faster. In our calendar app, we delivered a command line mode, headless scripts, and a Swing interface with conflict checks and time zone support. I kept a living README and a design log while we demoed our work to each other and re split tasks, which helped us stay on track. I also built a JavaFX card game using MVC, which sharpened my separation of concerns instincts and improved maintainability. As a Course Assistant for APCSA, I practiced inclusive collaboration every day. I guided students with questions, tailored help for different learning needs, and coordinated weekly with the lead teacher and Google volunteers to plan effective lessons. Working at Trader Joe’s I learned to assess multiple issues in a fast paced environment by staying calm, communicating clearly, and choosing the next best action. This mix of clear code, teaching, strong communication,  and decisive action maps to Generate’s developmental, inclusive, and intentional culture.
column26: I am confident in testing through headless mode and deterministic scenarios. In the calendar app I split UI from the logic so the model ran without the GUI, built scenario files and a command line runner to drive the controller and model, and caught overlap and input bugs early, which kept the project stable and sped up iteration. I want to improve my system design basics so I can shape small services with clear boundaries, simple data models, and baseline reliability. To learn, I will design and build a personal project end to end. I will write a one page design with goals, an API sketch, and a data model, then implement it with logging, simple tests, and a short readme. I will know it worked when the service meets its acceptance criteria, recovers from basic failures, and I can add a new feature without rework. I am motivated by my friends around me. Seeing them succeed motivates me to push myself, learn, build, and raise the standards I hold for myself and those around me.

column23: Although I have not been a part of Generate, my professional and extracurricular experiences have been extremely formative. Working as a Data Engineering Intern at Leidos and an IT Co-op at Vineyard Offshore exposed me to real-world system design, cross-functional collaboration, and production-level tooling. This has sharpened both my technical and communication skills. At the same time, being a peer tutor has challenged me to translate complex ideas clearly, reinforcing my own understanding. These experiences have shaped my ability to learn quickly, work collaboratively, and take initiative-qualities I’m excited to bring to the Generate Community.
column24: One of the most formative experiences in my growth as a software engineer is my current internship at Leidos, which runs through the end of this month. I’m working on migrating two high-volume project databases to DoltLab, a version-controlled SQL database. The work involves normalizing schemas, resolving foreign key mismatches, and collaborating across teams to align on a sustainable data versioning strategy. Beyond implementation, I’ve been actively involved in designing normalized schemas and shaping how data versioning is integrated into engineering workflows. Collaborating with cross-functional teams has shown me how thoughtful design choices directly impact scalability, collaboration, and long-term maintainability. This experience is reinforcing that effective software engineering extends beyond writing code—it's about enabling others to build confidently on top of your work.
column25: What sets me apart is the combination of hands-on technical experience, cross-functional collaboration, and a strong foundation in both data and software engineering. I’ve contributed to production-scale database design and migration, while aligning with multiple engineering teams on system-wide versioning strategies. I have also worked through IT initiatives, gaining experience with automation, internal tooling, and secure data integration. Outside of work, tutoring peers in AI and algorithms has sharpened my communication skills and reinforced my ability to break down complex problems. This blend of systems thinking, adaptability, and collaborative drive allows me to both contribute meaningfully and grow within any team I join.
column26: One skill I'm confident in is designing systems that integrate cleanly into team workflows. I would like to learn more by gaining exposure to full-stack development and cloud-based deployment to better support real-world product delivery end-to-end. My drive to learn and take on challenges stems from seeing how thoughtful design and integration elevate not just functionality, but team collaboration and real-world impact. That synergy between technical depth and product-minded thinking deeply motivates me to expand my learning and take on challenges that drive tangible outcomes.

column23: Throughout my first year in college, I was not the type that was super involved. I was focused on my academics while sticking to myself and the one or two other friends I made since orientation. However, as the year progressed, I joined affinity clubs for latinx students, and as I got to meet more new people, I got comfortable stepping out of my shell that I kept myself in at the beginning of my first year. It wasn’t until I had my first leadership position as a program manager for a first generation workshop for incoming first gen hispanic students, that I realized how fulfilling and easy it was to have a positive impact on others’ lives. 	From getting involved as a leader on campus, I eventually made my way to becoming a resident assistant (RA) for first years in Stetson East (or Steast). In this role, I was able to step into my creativity when it came to coming up with event ideas that would yield the most engagement from my residents. At this time, Steast had also become home to the Khoury living learning community (RIP White Hall), so I worked with other Steast RAs to put together and lead the first Steast hackathon ever (to my knowledge at least). With a Flask + React workshop series, and a theme of developing for social good, the 40+ first year residents that attended would be sure to leave with something they could be proud of. From here, I left with a sense of pride in stepping out of my shell and into a world where I can impact others positively with some crazy ideas and a lot of effort.
column24: In attempting to get involved with more technical projects for software development, I decided to apply to Sandbox in my third year. With no prior web development experience, I was unable to complete the technical assessment. However, the way the technical assessment was set up, allowed for those who did not have prior web development experience to learn with a given list of resources to look into. The assessment involved the creation of RESTful API endpoints for a carpooling webapp. At the time of assessment, I had no idea how to get started with creating an API, however, with the list of resources, I was able to learn as I code. Often, I had gone down a rabbit hole of learning all about backend development, how HTTP requests worked, and much more. Learning all about a completely new subject in such a short amount of time and having to problem solve along the way, was an addicting, all too familiar feeling of why I like to code in the first place. Therefore, even as I failed the assessment, I decided to keep the learning snowball rolling and get into full stack development to build my own thing. 	At this point, I simply had to figure out what part of my life needed a webapp to make life a little bit easier. Being an RA, one of the most tedious tasks is having to create a bulletin board, which must meet certain pillars of ResLife, and then having to resize the bulletin board, such that the appropriate amount of pages were printed to cover a bulletin board case. This was the problem which I decided to solve with getting into fullstack development by the creation of a MakeMy Bulletin webapp, which holds a database of all bulletin boards created by other RAs, and allows the user to pick their favorite to keep the same or edit as desired! This allowed me to do all the research I needed into how databases worked (specifically PostgreSQL), and how to connect the backend to the frontend, and all things which allowed me to solidify my passion in learning by doing.
column25: As a software developer for Generate, my strength would be the drive I have to make an impact mixed with my creativity in planning events as an RA. My background in being a first gen hispanic student solidified how important it is to have others that care about my success as a student. Being a part of PODER (a hispanic first gen arrival group) during my first year, I was paired with a mentor, who gave me advice on academics and other little things, which I found comforting. Here, I felt firsthand what it was like to be impacted in a positive way, which is something I have dedicated myself to paying forward as a program manager for PODER for other incoming first years. In being an RA, I have had to come up with at least 4 event ideas which would draw the most attention and be the most helpful for my students. My first semester being an RA, I was mainly leading these events by myself with mediocre turn out. In my second and third semesters of being an RA, I had placed more of an effort to set up collaborations with other RAs. This allowed for a bouncing back and forth of ideas, which often got a shoutout from the residence director (RD). It was through this back and forth of ideas, that we had settled on creating a Steast hackathon, which was a first for all of the RAs in terms of running the event.
column26: With all of my side projects, I have worked the most with React and Javascript, so I would be most confident in frontend development. Specifically, turning a Figma design into the reality of a web application. One thing I continue to improve on is the ability to recognize the need for abstraction more quickly. To improve, I have gotten better at creating quick sketches of UML diagrams, which allow me to recognize a pattern of abstraction a lot faster than just thinking about the code in my head (when in doubt, draw it out)!  	The fulfillment of being able to look at a finished product and say “I did that” or “We did it!” is what motivates me to learn new things and take on challenges. A side project which I have also fully dived into is installing the Arch Linux distro on my computer as my first GNU/Linux distro. The reason: Arch is notorious for being the most difficult distribution for beginners to Linux distributions. However, getting to a point where I could look back and say “I conquered the Arch mountain!” was a thrill I knew I would enjoy from the beginning. The fulfillment in that thrill would be well worth all the keys I may or may not have smashed on my keyboard (this is a joke, I promise, I am a calm individual)!

column23: Working as an online programming tutor on Wyzant and as a TA, I’ve taught over 40 students across a range of topics—from Python fundamentals to advanced algorithms. Teaching others has sharpened my own understanding, strengthened my communication skills, and reinforced the importance of developing clear, maintainable code. On the extracurricular side, participating in hackathons like FinHacks and CS Base’s Climate Hackathon challenged me to solve real-world problems under time constraints, enhancing both my technical skills and my ability to collaborate under pressure. These experiences collectively developed my confidence, adaptability, and technical breadth
column24: I helped to develop Breathe Easy, a mobile navigation app that utilizes real-time air quality data to guide you along the healthiest routes for the CS Base Climate Hackathon. This was my first long-term project with a hard deadline of one month. Working on this project taught me end to end skills (frontend, backend, and everything in between) as well as to stick with an idea, especially during the tough times, when many challenges presented themself
column25: My programming background consists of a wide variety of languages, meaning that I can easily identify the best tools for a project and assimilate myself to feel comfortable using them. Additionally, 300+ hours as an online programming tutor and experience as a TA has allowed me to hone my communication skills, and thrive in a team setting.
column26: I'm highly confident in object-oriented programming, particularly in Java and Dart. I’ve applied OOP principles rigorously in academic assignments (CS 3500), personal projects like Breathe Easy and Portal Card, and teaching sessions, allowing me to write scalable, modular code. One area I’m eager to improve is understanding the best practices when it comes to designing a user interface that best conveys an idea for a brand or product line. What drives me to keep learning is curiosity and the thrill of solving new problems. Whether it’s debugging a tough issue or experimenting with a new API, I’m motivated by the idea that every challenge is a step toward becoming a more effective engineer

column23: One of the most impactful experiences in my growth was working as a food server and cashier at Jollibee. At first, I saw myself as just another cog in the system. But I came to realize that I had the power to shape the experience, not just by delivering food but by creating a welcoming atmosphere. I started engaging in light banter with customers and connecting with coworkers, which made the job more enjoyable for me and left customers with a more positive impression of the store. As our location gained popularity, some people even driving from 3 hours away, we faced new challenges. The biggest issue was the messy state of the parking lot outside of the store. During slower hours, I began going outside to clean up litter: cigarette butts, straw wrappers, and used napkins. It wasn’t officially part of my job, but I felt a sense of ownership. I wanted to contribute to the full customer experience, not just the food. My time at Jollibee taught me the value of taking initiative and going beyond the bare minimum. I learned that I’m not just someone who follows instructions, rather I’m someone who can make a difference.
column24: One project that fundamentally shaped me as a software engineer was a stock market simulator that I built from scratch. When I started, I designed everything, every object, class interaction, and feature. But as I kept planning, the design became more complicated, and I found myself adding features endlessly. A month later, my friend asked to see my project, and I had no working code to show for what I had been working on, just pages upon pages of plans and flowcharts.Through this experience, I realized that while planning is important, it’s even more valuable to start, as planning is not able to create any impact. Later in the project, I found an improvement that I could change to the project, and when I did, I realized that software development is not about building something perfect in one shot; rather, it is an iterative process through steady progress and attention to detail. Since then, I’ve brought this mindset to every project. I’ve learned that starting small, testing often, and allowing room for change leads to better software and less burnout.
column25: Growing up being called the wrong name, being confused with someone else, and spending almost every minute of my time with another person shaped how I understand identity, collaboration, and independence. As a twin, I had to learn how to work closely with someone, having the same things, sharing the same friend groups, and even being in the same classes, while still figuring out who I was on my own. This experience allowed me to understand who I was beyond simply being “one of the twins.” Additionally, it helped me to understand what it means to work as a team. Sharing many experiences, we partnered together on many occasions: planning birthday parties, pulling pranks, and playing music together. This helped me to understand that collaboration is two-sided, not just one person helping someone else. Even further, it also made me comfortable asking for help. I learned that receiving help from others does not mean I am less capable, but rather that I am willing to learn from others. These experiences have helped me become someone who is self-aware, adaptable, collaborative, and ready to contribute to any team.
column26: One technical skill that I am confident in is flowcharting and designing programs. Before I write any code, I map out the overall program flow, which gives me a clear sense of structure and guides me as I write individual functions. This approach allows me to create well-designed programs rather than code with functionality scattered across it. While I am confident in program design, I would like to improve my skills in deployment and program architecture. Currently, most of my code remains on my local machine, where it solves problems for me, but doesn’t reach beyond that. Learning how to properly structure applications, manage dependencies, and deploy to platforms like the web would allow me to make the ideas I've built more accessible. Improving in this area would allow me to better share my ideas and make a real impact. In addition to creating meaningful programs that can help others, I am motivated by overcoming challenges of turning confusion into clarity. This helps me to not only have more confidence in my code but also in myself.

column23: Over this past year, Generate has given me something invaluable: a community of inspiring leaders, seasoned engineers, and innovative thinkers who've shaped both my technical skills and personal growth. I've built genuine connections, learned to write production-quality code, and collected experiences I'll carry with me long after graduation. It's this community that stays connected whether we are in the Sherm, struggling through the latest feature or the streets of Brooklyn looking for some grub.   Not only is the community supportive socially, but they also challenge me to become a better student and software engineer. I am surrounded by some of the most talented engineers at Northeastern and am able to share in the wealth of knowledge that comes with that. As I progress through my own journey as an engineer, I would love to not only listen to my peers in Generate but also contribute to the conversation by introducing my own learnings and personal experiences which I have accrued from my personal projects, past generate semesters, and co-op. I want to be able to do onto others as they have done for me and help all members of my team and Generate community grow.   Community is what sets Generate apart from the pack. Community is why I chose to apply today.
column24: It's a Friday at work and I got a DM from the founder. I jumped on a call and learned that I was the head of engineering for the day because my manager was out; and oh, by the way – a feature on prod is down and we need to fix it ASAP. The sink or swim environment of my co-op, while at times stressful, has led me to accelerate my learning like few experiences have. Every day it's a new bug, last minute feature addition or “Hey Josh can you do me a favor”. All this at a SAAS company with two engineers; my manager and myself. This has meant that I quickly needed to become an expert in could infrastructure, information ingestion pipelines, software architecture, API design, database entity design, quality assurance testing, git conflicts and much more. Aside from the technical, I also learned how to interpret ambiguous specifications, ask penitent questions, address discrepancies head on, and work with clients to build their “dream” functionality.  I would love to continue to apply technical and interpersonal skills I have learned at Kubera Health to my team and clients at Generate.
column25: Over my time in college, I have worn many hats whether that be as a student, TA, researcher, generate member or software engineer, they have all prepared me to succeed and exceed expectations as a member of Generate. Through my previous experiences in Generate, I have been able to learn the tech stacks that we use, build extensible software, apply best practices for pair programming and make meaningful contributions to team meetings. I did my best to be more than just a team member but a reliable source of support, an engaged friend and someone who brought careful attention to every detail. Through this endeavor, I spotted design flaws before they were implemented, supported my peers in learning new tools and helped drive the product to be polished and complete. My approach to teamwork centers on active collaboration. I listen carefully to different perspectives, contribute constructively to discussions, and adapt my communication style to what works best for each team member.   Aside from my past experiences in Generate, I also bring to the table skills I have learned from my roles as a teaching assistant and researcher. These roles required me to translate my own technical knowledge into analogies, metaphors, and examples that are digestible and comprehensible for new computer scientists. I have found that many of the lessons that I have learned in these leadership positions have been beneficial in my work at Generate. Along with having a better understanding of the material, I am also better equipped to explain new ideas, frameworks, and tools to fellow members. While many times these members are not new to the world of computer science, these experiences have helped me form a mental model of how best to help fellow students learn new skills. This directly translates to being an effective team collaborator who can bridge knowledge gaps and ensure everyone stays aligned.   Lastly, my recent experiences as a software engineer for Kubera Health have better prepared me for another semester of Generate and I would love to show all that I have learned and share this with others in the community.
column26: “So you just solve puzzles all day?” is the question I keep getting from my Mom since starting my co-op. While SWE work for her seems like a chore, for me it is an exciting opportunity to dive into a new problem or sink my teeth into the latest feature. It is the challenge itself that excites me to learn more and dive into a problem headfirst. Through the battle scars from the War on Bugs, I am left as a better problem (and puzzle) solver. This is why I feel so strongly about my ability to design software. Whether it be high level system architecture, database entities or object design, I have been able to apply my puzzle solving aptitudes to create readable and elegant software solutions that scale. While my design background is SOLID, I would like to work more on presenting my work. As I learned in my last interview, I at times struggle to find a balance between explaining the domain and the technical nature of the solutions I create. In the past, I have largely explained my code to others who understand the technical requirements and how they translate to the business needs. However, it is an important skill to also translate these ideas for those who do not know about the domain and shouldn’t need to. If I have the opportunity to serve as an engineer for Generate again this semester, I plan to challenge myself to talk with others in the community about the projects I am working on, share ideas, and continue to build on my ability to translate business requirements into plain English.

column23: Although I haven’t joined Generate yet, an experience in my California hometown shaped my growth as both a developer and a teammate. Last summer I volunteered with a Santa Ana food-pantry collective that struggled with volunteer no-shows. I led a small student crew to build a lightweight scheduling site and integrated Twilio texts that ping volunteers 24 hours before their shift. The no-show rate dropped from about 40 percent to under 15, and weekly distributions finally ran on time. Turning a community headache into a tested, documented, and hand-off-ready tool taught me that code only matters when it delivers real results for real people.
column24: This summer I built and open sourced a Java flash card CLI that implements the SM2 spaced repetition algorithm with my close friend from home. Turning dense research math into efficient Java logic showed me how to bridge theory with practical code.
