We backed the features with unit tests and wrote clear setup notes so classmates could get the tool running in minutes. Regular pull request reviews and three rounds of user feedback surfaced bugs and usability quirks I had missed. The whole process proved that strong problem solving only shines when paired with clear communication and constant iteration, a mindset I am excited to bring to Generate.
column25: My edge starts with math. I have earned straight As in every math and computer science course in college and highschool. When I look at numbers, I don’t just read them but I picture the ideas behind them and see how each graph, limit, or matrix reflects a rule that already exists in the world. That habit of visualizing complexity lets me spot patterns fast and break hard problems into clean steps, whether I am debugging code or mapping a data pipeline. I pair that analytic focus with real-world follow-through, like when I turned a food bank’s volunteer issues into a working scheduling site and text-reminder system that cut no-shows by more than half. Strong problem solving backed by user empathy means I can dive deep into the technical details and still ship tools the team can trust and users will actually love.
column26: I am most confident in object-oriented Java. I can design clear class hierarchies, refactor code without breaking behavior, and back everything up with solid unit tests. The skill I want to deepen next is full-stack web development, especially React on the front end and basic cloud deployment, so that I can own a project from database to polished interface. Curiosity fuels me. Whenever a complex idea clicks, whether it is a calculus proof or a tricky bug fix, I feel a surge of excitement that pushes me to tackle the next challenge and keep expanding my toolkit.

column19: ColorStack 1-2 hrs/week and SHAC Lab 3-5 hrs/week
column23: My extracurricular experiences at Northeastern have complemented the computer science education I have gotten in class. In Oasis, which I did during my first semester in college, I was able to create a React.js project with a team. We created PlaNUr, a site that allows for simple course section lookup and comparison based on CRN. This was the first time I made a project with a team. We had to learn how to communicate our ideas and figure out how to divide tasks effectively, utilizing GitHub. In Northeastern Electric Racing, I worked on Argos, a real-time data visualization tool for the race car. This was my first experience working with a large code base. I had to learn to understand what people before me had done and then make my own additions. In ColorStack, I found a community of people who were passionate about computer science and wanted to uplift those around them. This has provided me with the opportunity to talk with software engineers from companies like DataDog and Nvidia, which gave me a better idea of what a day in the life of a software engineer looks like and what I should emphasize learning.
column24: A project that has fundamentally shaped me as a software engineer is SeamCarver, which I created while taking Fundies II Accelerated. This is the most technically challenging project I have done and the one I am most proud of. It is a Java program that performs lossy compression on an image by deleting the least important seams, which are paths connecting the top and bottom or the left and right sides of the image. The energy of each pixel is found by applying a formula that calculates how much a pixel differs from its neighbors, which is then used to find the least important seam; seams are removed automatically or manually. This was the most fun I’ve ever had making a project. I worked day and night on it for ten days with my partner to complete it. This was also the first time I had effectively utilized pair programming as I had simply split the work with previous partners. We were bouncing ideas off each other all night because we were truly passionate about what we were creating. This experience reaffirmed to me that I had made the right choice in major because I was doing something that I loved.
column25: Growing up, I moved all over the world for my dad’s work, living in Switzerland, France, Niger, West Virginia, and Massachusetts. This has made me able to learning and growing across multiple languages and cultures. In a field where adaptability is key and skills must be translated over many different programming languages, I have thrived in part because of the way in which I grew up. Furthermore, I am a fast and eager learner. Up to this point, I have done well in classes and other extracurriculars, but I have not had the ability to create large projects from the ground up. I believe that is the next step for me in my development as a software engineer. I work well within a team because I enjoy collaborating with people who are motivated to achieve a goal. I enjoy being able to go to people I can trust to give me feedback and vice versa. By listening to others’ ideas, the team’s productivity will increase and everyone will learn from each other.
column26: I am confident in my ability to do React. I spent a semester learning it and making a project with a team using it. JavaScript was also the first coding language I was ever introduced to (not including Scratch). I wrote front-end and back-end code in React  as we used React exclusively for our project. I am interested in learning full-stack development that integrates multiple technologies to create more complex projects as I was not able to do this either in NER since I worked exclusively in Angular. I am and always have been a very curious person. I enjoy the process of learning new things, especially related to topics I am interested in. The feeling of conquering a concept is a great one and one that I strive for often. In computer science, this feels especially good because the process of learning is a problem-solving one. This knowledge can stack, allowing me to learn more complex concepts and make more interesting projects. Furthermore, I know that the more knowledge and skills I have, the more helpful I can be to my team, my company, my society, and myself.

column23: I really love learning, whether it’s in class or through hands-on projects, and Generate has been such a great place for that. During my first semester, I had the opportunity to work with Go and React Native for the first time. I learned a lot, but what stood out most was how driven and supportive everyone around me was. That culture of motivation and kindness pushed me to step outside my comfort zone, ask questions, and grow into a more confident engineer.  In addition, I think Generate cultivates an amazing, almost scrappy (in the best way) developer environment. We don’t have to jump through as many hoops to get things merged to master as in a regular company, which means developers can quickly build and innovate on top of each other’s work.  I’m incredibly grateful for that experience, and I’m excited to keep building, learning, and contributing even more moving forward.
column24: When I was working on a project for my database design class, my team included mostly non-CS majors, so I ended up stepping into more of a mentorship role. I helped teammates debug issues, taught them how to use GitHub, and shared best practices along the way.  When the class schedule got thrown off, we had to finish the entire project in just a couple of weeks, using a codebase we didn’t know. The stack was completely new to me: Python, Streamlit for the frontend, Flask for the backend, and a Docker container running a PostgreSQL database. As we ran into bugs, I figured out how to check the Docker logs and unblock myself. From there, I helped my teammates and recorded my debugging strategies to share with the team. A few other groups even reached out, so I shared the video with them too.  After years of learning from mentors, this was the first time I found myself in that role. It felt rewarding to support others and pass on what I’d learned. I still love solving technical problems, but I’m just as excited to help the people around me do the same.
column25: As an incoming fifth-year at Northeastern, I’ve had the opportunity to take a wide range of classes, join clubs, and complete four co-ops/internships. I’ve gotten to experience a lot, and I bring everything I’ve learned into each new opportunity.  What sets me apart most is my enthusiasm for both the work I do and the process of learning. I bring genuine energy and curiosity to every team I join. That helps me ramp up quickly, ask thoughtful questions, and contribute meaningfully early on. I’m not just excited about building things. I’m also excited about growing alongside the people I work with.  I enjoy diving into unfamiliar problems, figuring out how things work, and finding better ways to approach them. That mindset has helped me thrive in all sorts of collaborative environments.
column26: One technical skill I’ve developed and feel confident in is asking good questions. This has helped me clarify stakeholder needs and unblock myself when I’m stuck. At my current internship, I interviewed several support team members to understand issues they were facing with a complex SCIM service I wasn’t familiar with. By asking thoughtful, targeted questions, I was able to understand their pain points and build APIs that directly addressed their problems.  One area I’m continuing to grow in is backend engineering. At Couplet, I took on backend-focused tickets to push myself. I’ve also looked for internships where I could build those skills further. I’m currently doing backend work at Atlassian and will be joining HubSpot this fall as a backend engineering co-op.  What motivates me most is understanding how systems work together. At large companies, it can be difficult to get a full picture of the system. In Generate, I got to help build projects from the ground up. That gave me insight into how everything fits together and how design choices shape the system over time. I enjoy learning new tools and patterns, when they are able to take on and solve new challenges.

column23: As a first-time member, Generate changed my entire Northeastern experience. After matriculating in Fall 2025, I struggled with confidence in my coding ability, making friends, and finding my place at NU. Despite these insecurities, I always knew I wanted to be a part of something impactful–and after constantly seeing members of Generate hard at work in the makerspace on my way to class, I knew deep down this club would be the perfect place for me. So, after putting my heart into the application and getting accepted, I knew I had to make the most out of my spring semester. I became close with my technical leads, asking for extra tickets and staying up late with them, debugging issues in our codebase. One of my greatest influences from this experience came from meeting my Generate “buddy,” Linwood, as he showed me the projects he was passionate about, introduced me to other members, and helped me find my software niche. At the showcase, I vividly remember feeling incredibly proud of how much I had grown from that timid, unconfident kid I was before Generate. Never in my life have I been part of a product development team or been recognized for my software ability, and Generate helped me realize that I’m capable of genuinely contributing to real-world projects. I owe so much of my first-year experience to Generate–and I’m excited to keep growing, learning, and building alongside people who inspire me every day.
column24: This summer, thanks to a referral from a former Generate lead, I interned for two founders fresh out of pre-seed funding in Silicon Valley. After onboarding, they immediately assigned me to work on a client management tool that would connect them with leads in the furniture industry. This task had a one-day turnaround time, and–despite extreme stress–I have never learned more in a single session of programming. The tech stack was simple, but tasteful: Python, Tailwind, and Next.js. I started just before noon, and by the time the app was ready to ship, it was nearly 10 PM. I created one of my cleanest user interfaces yet, applying skills I developed in my previous semester at Generate—such as wireframing in Figma or integrating Lucide-React icons. My growth as an engineer felt real. The completed web app proudly showcased a variety of features, including real-time integration with a Google Form CSV to generate personalized emails for each client. It also supported customization, such as scheduling email blasts or altering email subjects and body content. Ultimately, the real value of the project wasn’t in the code output, but in the mental output. Faced with a tight deadline, I put my head down, stayed focused, and delivered something I am still genuinely proud of. It opened my eyes to the possibilities of larger-scale projects and left me with a ton of excitement for the future.
column25: Whenever I am elected for a position in a new organization, there is always one distinct, parallel impact that I foster: Focused optimism. Whether it be my previous position as Vice President of The National Honor Society, as Captain of my High School Ski Team, or even during my previous semester at Generate, I always find a way to uniquely motivate my team–either with expansive goals, light humor, or even constructive criticism–to get things done. As VP of NHS, I built a free tutoring system for low-income students; on the Ski Team, I developed three underclassmen into top-ranked racers; during my first semester in Generate, I didn’t want any special treatment for being new, so I focused on being a leader with my work ethic—taking on as many tickets as I could, learning from upperclassmen, and working closely with my technical leads to support the team however I could. When it comes to Generate, I want to leave a lasting impact with my focused optimism by building up other developers and using my unique problem-solving mindset to inspire solutions. Overall, I want to put my best foot forward and help develop the innovative products that Generate is known for!
column26: From a young age, my hard-working, deeply Polish father always expressed the importance of curiosity, motivating me with prominent Slavic scientists like Curie, Sakharov, and Kistiakowsky. Since my parents worked tirelessly to come to this country and create opportunities for me and my siblings, I always felt a profound responsibility to put forth my very best in every endeavor, driven by the desire to help build a better world. I have been programming and developing for the past three years, so I’ve encountered many techniques that I have grown to love and some I wish I were better at. After learning C through Harvard’s online CS50 course and exploring Java in high school, I really found my groove with problem-solving in Python. After I picked up the language in my junior year, I began creating as much as possible, starting with a basic planet simulator and advancing to one of my biggest projects yet: a 2D platformer with a custom, Python-built engine. This project sharpened my ability to troubleshoot, debug, and approach problems methodically. Now, after falling in love with Systems engineering over the past year, I’d love to become more mechanical in my backend skills–specifically in Go. While I have experience building functional endpoints, I want to reach the point where I can write backend code instinctively, with a strong grasp of system design patterns, database integrations, and authentication. As AI accelerates frontend development, I want to become a T-shaped developer with deep backend fluency, capable of building reliable, scalable systems from the ground up.

column23: Through volunteering in college, I’ve found meaningful ways to serve others from teaching English to kids in Chinatown after school, to spending time with the elderly and listening to their stories. These experiences taught me what it means to live with intention and to center others in the work I do. That’s why I’m excited about Generate and the chance to build technology that has real impact. One project from last year that stood out to me was Dearly, which uses technology to connect different generations. It reminded me of my own journey working with both children and seniors, and how much I’ve learned from serving people across all walks of life.
column24: From the beginning, we focused on breaking the project down into small, manageable tasks and iterating quickly, always making sure we stayed aligned with user needs and project timelines. Beyond the technical challenge of designing a distributed system, I ran into some of the toughest debugging I've ever faced. Hours spent tracking down mysterious buildups of “ghost” documents quickly taught me the importance of clear logging and visibility. Tracing AWS errors brought me into contact with data engineers, project managers, and even company executives, and taught me how to ask the right questions for the right audience.
column26: One thing that sets me apart is my ability to take initiative and learn independently, whether it’s picking up new AI technologies or adapting to unfamiliar domains. In my research, I needed to fully understand the text-to-image diffusion process before I could start engineering custom pipelines and running experiments. I read dozens of papers, explored the codebase in depth, and iterated on my own ideas. This work eventually led to a project I presented at CVPR, one of the top computer vision conferences in the world. At my co-op, I had to quickly learn about antibody biology. I used tools like ChatGPT to teach myself key concepts and terminology, which helped me contribute meaningfully within just a week. Across both experiences, I’ve developed not just technical depth, but the confidence to handle ambiguity, ask the right questions, and drive progress in unfamiliar territory. I’m excited to bring that mindset to Generate’s collaborative and fast-moving environment.

column23: My favorite part about every professional and extracurricular experience I’ve had has been getting the opportunity to learn from– and about – the people I work with. In each setting, I’ve gained invaluable domain knowledge: learning new coding languages and frameworks at hackathons, working with project management tools and development life cycles during my co-ops, all the way to conducting cognitive testing in a research lab. All of these things have allowed me to grow technically, but more important is how I’ve grown as a person and a worker. Above all, I’ve come to realize that my growth can be attributed to the inspiration that I find in the people around me. I’ve mentored people who motivate me to learn, work and give back, and I’ve been mentored by many whose hard-earned accomplishments challenge me to strive to be better. I believe there is power being in a space where people foster learning, sharing, and collaboration. It is from these experiences, and the people I’ve shared them with, that I’ve been able to build communication, teamwork, and leadership skills — and I hope I’ve had as much of a positive impact on them as they’ve had on me.
column24: It took me a long time to figure out what I wanted to do in my career. Each month it was something different: Psychology– no, Law. Actually, Computer Science. Wait– back to Psychology. It wasn’t until I participated in a project that I contributed zero lines of code to that I realized I wanted to do software engineering. It was my first hackathon, and I had shown up purely to learn. Luckily enough, my team let me shadow their technical work, even though my one and a half classes in computer science meant I could only help with non‑technical aspects. Over those 24 hours, I saw firsthand the creativity, skill, and problem‑solving that went into building something from nothing. Our project was rudimentary at best, but it was driven by passion– the problem we chose, the impact it aimed for, and the approach we took were entirely our own. I knew then that if I wanted to start creating projects I truly cared about, I would need to start seeking out opportunities to build and learn. Though I didn’t leave with as much technical knowledge as I’d hoped, that experience became the catalyst for my journey into software engineering.
column25: As committed as I am to programming now, I wasn’t always set in this career path. My first love was psychology. I’ve always been fascinated by the nuances of how people think, behave, and connect. As a discipline it sometimes feels like a far cry from the world of software and programming, but it has deeply impacted how I approach tech. Psychology has taught me to look at problems from a human-centered perspective, not just at how something works, but who it impacts, why it matters, and how it can make someone’s life better. This mindset has become one of my biggest strengths as a developer because it’s instilled in me the passion to create technology that is impactful. I believe in leading with empathy and intention, and it affects not only the technical work I do, but also how I collaborate and work with others. While I’m sure I’m not the only one who brings passion to the table, I believe that my love for tech, my skillset, and my desire to build things that serve people is a combination that makes me a stronger teammate, problem-solver, and builder.
column26: There is something inherently satisfying about seeing a static design come to life. To be able to watch your creative designs become interactive interfaces has been one of my favorite aspects of web development. In particular, I have been growing my skills in front-end development, learning how to translate UI designs into functional, efficient code. Through hackathons, personal projects, and work, I have built up experience with React and TypeScript, utilizing various design and state management libraries. Where I’m eager to grow is in bridging the frontend with the backend. In my current role, I’ve started gaining exposure to how APIs, databases, and server-side logic connect with front-end components in a real world application. Along the way, I hope to continue building the technical foundation to be able to create end-to-end projects on my own. Whether it’s getting a component perfectly aligned, fixing a bug, or making a successful query, each small win drives me to take on bigger challenges. I’ve found that I love the process just as much as the outcome, and it’s that curiosity and fulfillment that motivates me to keep learning.

column23: My professional and extracurricular experiences have taught me how powerful collaboration can be in building something meaningful. As a Teaching Assistant for Algorithms and Data Structures, I’ve learned to break down complex ideas into approachable steps, which not only helped students but also deepened my own technical understanding. Through projects like my crochet-based small business website and hackathon apps, I’ve gained experience in translating creative concepts into functional, user-centered products. My work on HackBeanpot, a beginner-friendly hackathon, taught me what it means to build with intention. It's not just about creating something that works, it’s about whether it serves its purpose for the user. These experiences have pushed me to adapt quickly, work across diverse teams, and consistently seek feedback to improve. They’ve shaped me into someone eager to contribute, grow, and bring both creativity and problem-solving to every project.
column24: During HackBeanpot, my team built a carbon footprint tracking app using React, FastAPI, and multiple APIs. It was my first time working on such a fast-paced, full-stack project, and I stepped outside my comfort zone by taking on unfamiliar front-end features. At that point I had doubts about my ability to individually own the front end. But when I immersed myself fully, I proved myself wrong. I learned how to integrate with backend APIs efficiently, resolve blockers quickly, and adapt designs on the fly based on team feedback and new UI. With this being my first full-stack project built with a team, it opened my eyes to how much collaboration is needed to push a project. I worked closely with both frontend and backend teammates, continuously syncing on features and priorities. This project taught me that being a good software engineer isn’t just about knowing a language or framework, it’s about problem-solving, communication, listening, and iterating quickly to make something functional and impactful.
column25: What sets me apart is that I lead with people, not just code. I’m not your stereotypical introverted CS major, I thrive in collaboration, mentorship, and community. I bring a unique blend of technical skill, design thinking, and genuine care for the people I work with. My background spans Java, Python, React, and UI/UX design, allowing me to move comfortably between frontend and backend tasks. Studying the intersection between technology and design has taught me to ask not just how we build things, but why, and for whom. I create software with the intention of making an impact, which is one of the reasons I remain passionate about the work I do. As President of the Women in the Enterprise of Science and Technology club, I’ve learned how to keep teams organized, motivated, and aligned toward shared goals. My experience building applications, from small personal websites to hackathon prototypes, gives me the ability to contribute quickly, while my leadership and teaching roles have trained me to give constructive feedback, adapt to different working styles, and keep collaboration productive and positive. I see myself as an engineer, but beyond that, I am a passionate, reliable, empathetic, and communicative friend.
column26: I’m confident in my ability to build responsive, accessible web interfaces with React and CSS, ensuring that a design not only looks good but also works well across different devices and screen sizes. One skill I’d like to improve is backend architecture for scalable systems, especially working with cloud infrastructure. I have not had as much exposure to this as I would like and am always looking to learn something new. Beyond that, my experience as an engineer is just beginning and I am nowhere near perfection. The idea that there is so much more to learn and apply keeps me going. I’m motivated by the process of turning an idea into something people can actually use. Every challenge is an opportunity to add another tool to my skillset, and I’ve found that the moments when I feel least “ready” are often the ones where I learn the most.

column16: Accel Fundies 2 TA Zainab, Generate's Instagram
column23: From operating our robot during matches as part of the drive team to contributing the most software commits as a member on my high school FRC team, I’ve learned how to lead under pressure while collaborating with students and mentors, and adapting to unexpected situations. In college so far, I’ve deepened my foundation in computer science and worked on hands-on projects like a seam carver, maze generator, and calendar application, where I built programs from the ground up using Java and object-oriented design. Outside of class, I’ve seen growth in unexpected places like auditioning multiple times for dance teams, which taught me how to handle rejection with resilience and re-channel my energy into new opportunities. These experiences have helped me see failure as redirection, not defeat. I’m always eager to learn, quick to adapt, and never afraid to start again. I believe this growth mindset will help me thrive in a team like Generate where innovation and learning are highly valued.
column24: One project that pushed me out of my comfort zone was building a maze generator and solver in Java. It was my first time applying graph algorithms like Kruskal’s, BFS, and DFS in a visual, interactive project, and I wasn’t sure where to start. I felt overwhelmed and was also under a time crunch, but instead of freezing, I broke the problem into smaller pieces, taught myself what I didn’t know, and built from there, trusting that I could figure it out if I gave it my full effort and focus. I didn’t get everything right the first time, and even if I did, I know I wouldn’t have truly learned. By the end of that initial learning curve, I had a working program that generated random mazes of arbitrary sizes, animated pathfinding, and traced the optimal solution from start to end. More than the technical learning, it showed me that I’m capable of tackling challenges as long as I’m willing to stay curious and put in the effort. That’s the core mindset that I bring into everything that I do, not just software engineering, and what I’d bring to Generate: resourcefulness, eagerness to grow, and fearlessness to start from scratch if it means building something meaningful.
column25: I bring a mix of technical curiosity, relentless work ethic, attention to detail, and optimistic energy. As someone who thrives in both structured environments (like my CS courses) and fast-moving team dynamics (like robotics drive team), I’ve learned how to be both a reliable contributor and an empathetic teammate. My friends have labeled me as the “mom of the group” because I’m organized, responsible, accountable, and always looking out for the people around me. I bring that same energy to team dynamics by being considerate of my teammates, adapting quickly to any situation, and helping create a space where everyone feels supported. I’m also deeply passionate about learning, whether it’s picking up a new coding language or teaching myself to crochet or learn a new sport, and I carry that same excitement into every project I work on. I'm thrilled at the possibility of bringing this enthusiasm and positive energy to my future team, creating an environment where we can all learn from each other and tackle challenging problems together.
column26: I'm confident in object-oriented programming and coding modular programs, which are skills I’ve gained through multiple java-based projects and labs. One area I’d love to improve in is frontend development and UI/UX design. I’ve always admired slick, user-friendly software products, and I’m looking forward to building interfaces that are not only functional but beautiful, giving my creative and artistic eye a chance to shine. As for motivation, I’m driven by personal growth itself. I love the feeling of looking back at something that once felt impossible and realizing it’s now second nature to me. Whether it's debugging a stubborn bug or learning a new dance style, I enjoy that feeling of progress and find satisfaction in overcoming challenges independently. The people pleaser in me might also have a minor role in my motivation, if I’m being honest, but I recognize that ultimately, the most meaningful progress happens when I'm learning for myself, not just to meet others' expectations. I learn because I’m curious, and I take on challenges because they help me become more capable both as a software engineer and as a person.

column23: My professional and extracurricular experiences have set me up to thrive in a collaborative, fast‑moving environment like Generate. As a Backend Software Engineer for Northeastern’s Student Government Association, I worked on a TypeScript and NestJS system for nominations and application tracking, plus an internal attendance manager in Next.js and Prisma, making sure everything was reliable by thoroughly testing changes before deployment. In NU‑CLEAR, a co‑op feedback platform, I built a Python and Flask REST API with MySQL, deployed in Docker, so users could filter and analyze feedback in real time. In Catalyst, I wrote algorithms to turn complex degree audits into clear, actionable guidance for students. These projects taught me how to take messy requirements and turn them into scalable, maintainable solutions while working closely with teammates. Outside of coding, being Webmaster for Northeastern Robotics and a Khoury Teaching Assistant has helped me get better at explaining complex ideas and working across different disciplines. I’ve learned that the best solutions come from rapid iteration, openness to feedback, and genuine collaboration, values I’m ready to bring to Generate while continuing to grow as a software engineer.
column24: A project that fundamentally shaped me as a software engineer was the Personal Portfolio Manager, a Python and AWS-based project. I was responsible for all things AWS, including S3, RDS, EC2, and Lambda. As we approached the final project, my teammates fell a little behind on class demos.  I broke down each component clearly: our EC2 instance would host the Streamlit app, serving as the interface for users. Any file a user uploaded would first go to our raw S3 bucket, preprocessed by our Lambda function and moved to our processed S3 bucket. Finally, the contents of the processed CSV were converted into a relational table in RDS, powering the app’s analytics and visualizations.   I created a simple architecture diagram and had each teammate walk through the system’s flow, while having them explain the pipeline back to me step by step, so they weren’t just memorizing details but actually understanding how the pieces worked together. This experience reinforced my belief that great engineering isn’t just building, it’s enabling your team to succeed through communication, teaching, and shared ownership. It gave me valuable experience as both a technical leader and a collaborator, preparing me to thrive in cross-functional, fast-moving environments.
column25: I bring a mix of technical depth and a collaborative, adaptable work style that helps teams actually get things done. column24: This summer, I interned at Amazon as a software engineer and built a customer-facing widget that let users connect to a healthcare agent within 30 seconds of searching on Amazon.com. This mix of engineering and product thinking is something I want to continue to pursue and bring to any team I’m a part of.

column24: Conducting an independent research project during high school played a major role in my desire to use technology to solve meaningful problems. The goal was to better uncover emotional intent during trials, which I believe is an important aspect that is often overlooked. While I had occasional check-ins with a professor, the project was largely self-driven and taught me the value of committing time to something I genuinely cared about. I'd like to improve upon my knowledge of the Spring Framework. I've started to scratch the surface of its potential, but I know that I have a long way to go. My primary motivator is just my need for mastery. I've always had the mindset that if I'm going to learn something, I want to finish it well, so I'll spend extra time figuring out why things work, instead of just the what. I love the feeling when I learn something "unnecessary" at the time, but later have a problem that fits that thing's exact use-case, similar to having a large toolbox of knowledge, and the satisfaction of having a tool for any occasion.

column23: Prior to now, I spent three years (on and off) at Artists for Humanity developing websites for internal and external clients and building projection-mapping displays for community interactive events in my neighborhood. This allowed for me to dip my toes in the software development sphere because what I built was directly contributing either to another Boston-based nonprofit, or to the organization that I worked for with a mission that I believed in.  This summer was a detour from the path I expected to take: I joined an investment management firm where the closest proximity to software development was my mentor (a software engineer who wasn’t even in my department) and a couple of Excel automations. However, I found that this summer experience allowed me to shape myself into a more well-rounded developer, especially in the fintech sphere. I think that pursuing finance for a summer developed my understanding of pursuing interdisciplinary ideas and exposing myself to more than just code, now intending to hone my abilities in Generate by working with more clients in a new environment.
column24: At my internship last summer at Hack Club, I organized Ascend (github.com/hackclub/ascend), a hackathon that brought 50 girls from around the world to Los Angeles and SpaceX to build projects. I built the event website (both for launch and day-of), ran a reinforcement learning workshop, mentored some of my peers building their projects during the event, and also built a project of my own.  Nonetheless, logistics - namely venue hunting, was quite difficult when no one on the organizing team was from LA. Though my role just happened to be mostly technical in retrospect, assisting my peers in roles I thought were undesirable made me a better organizer overall.  Being a hackathon organizer taught me the non-technical aspects of software engineering that are overlooked. Additionally, the number one thing I hear from my SWE mentors at my internship this summer at Manulife John Hancock Investments is how the job isn’t just typing away in an editor all day, but being intentful with what you are building and using those non-technical but equally important communicative skills to complete tasks.
column25: I think that having experience in nonprofits (promoting technology) and corporate (applying technology) exposed me to multiple types of software engineering atmospheres. Few people can say that they bought an island just to run a hackathon on it (shipwrecked.hackclub.com), or get flown (more like bused, in my case) up to middle-of-nowhere Vermont for a summer just to be one with nature and build  - that kind of energy alone is rare! These once-in-a-lifetime experiences shaped who I was as someone pursuing software development and computer science, and I think that joining a team with people from other whimsical backgrounds produces the coolest kinds of products.
column26: I can confidently say that I am well-versed in general-case web development - namely Next.js (and React) - but I am not very skilled in other web frameworks like Svelte. I find that the catalyst that taught me Next.js was the desire to realize something with it, or convert it to something practical that has actual use.  For example, I learned web development initially because I wanted to build a student portal for my school. After many iterations, I gradually picked up on more frameworks, with a pitchable product by winter of my junior year of high school. From there, it was showcased at my school’s course selection assembly and my student body of over 1,700 was able to use my work to help select their courses for the following year.

column23: My past professional experience has fundamentally changed my beliefs. While working with State Street, we were a close group of co-ops, tasked with  building a KPI dashboard for executives that initially seemed like a pure technical challenge. However, through regular meetings with numerous cross-functional departments and upcoming deadlines, I realized the importance of understanding their specific needs and to communicate efficiently not only with the client but within our team. This taught me to prioritize the “why” before the “how.” Software engineering isn’t only about the cleanliness of technical implementation. While it still matters, solving the right problems for the right people is just as crucial. When you have a clear idea of the requirements in mind, you’ll see a clearer path to the ultimate goal and do things differently. The decisions and implementations you choose to take will be purposeful to those requirements. Recently at Onshape, implementing search for the education platform reinforced this lesson. Working with UX engineers and the education team, I saw how my decisions are shaped by business requirements. Most importantly, I want to acknowledge the relationships built during these projects were as valuable as technical skills gained. The trust and help received from consistent communications with managers, mentors, and peers really helped me see why I chose this field in the first place, working with people to overcome challenges and create something meaningful that serves people's needs.
column24: As a passionate badminton player, I’ve seen people online trying to find local courts, stringers, and coaches. Therefore I created Shuttleverse, a centralized crowdsourcing platform to solve this information gap. I wanted to use this opportunity to learn and architect my first microservices system. Whether it was the API gateway or the service registry, each implementation challenge became an opportunity for growth. The most challenging aspect was implementing the Backend-for-Frontend pattern for authentication with Redis session management. While Spring Boot's powerful abstractions initially made implementation seem very easy, I soon encountered bugs that the framework's magic couldn't solve. I realized that relying on Spring Boot's high-level annotations without understanding what actually happens was insufficient. I dived into how Spring Security works under the hood and broke down the problem into separate parts and debugged which part of the process broke. This experience taught me how to break down complex problems into manageable components, just like how microservices break down complex applications into independent services, haha. I’m no longer satisfied with code that merely works but wanting to understand the fundamental principles. While people say not to reinvent the wheel and I could have just used an authentication API provider, I think deconstructing that wheel taught me valuable fundamental knowledge that transfer to future challenges.
column25: I’m grateful to have worked with many teams each with unique experiences. When I was working with the Forge team, I collaborated across engineering disciplines to create an innovative product. I was in charge of developing an app to communicate with the hardware. I quickly adapted and learned how to integrate hardware and software. Moreover, I also emphasized open communication to explain to each other who aren’t as familiar with the software or hardware discipline. This communication skill proved equally valuable at State Street and Onshape, where I regularly engaged with stakeholders to gather business requirements. At State Street, I developed an ETL pipeline work in Databricks. At Onshape, I created an Elasticsearch-powered search feature for educators. From working building data pipelines with Databricks, to working with Elasticsearch, each gave me unique experiences and allowed myself to enhance my skills in learning new technologies. After working with a good number of teams, I learned to value the people I work with and I believe that’s what’s important. While I excel in technical adaptability, I believe my ability to communicate efficiently with team members along with a positive vibe enable me to work well within a team of equally passionate software engineers.
column26: One technical skill I’m confident in is backend development and particularly Spring Boot. I’ve developed and maintained backend services using Spring Boot, working with RESTful APIs, security features, and database integration through Spring Data JPA. I’ve also leveraged Spring Security for authentication and OAuth flows, which has given me a strong understanding of how to build secure backend systems. While I’ve been working with microservices recently, I’m eager to learn more and dive deeper into the nuances of microservices. There are far more topics that I haven’t gotten a chance to dive into such as circuit breakers and message queues which are widely adopted in microservices architecture. Software engineering is a field of endless learning and there’s always new things to learn. I’m eager to continue learning and building even more efficient, scalable systems. This is also what ultimately motivates me to learn new things and take on challenges, so that I can gain the expertise needed to create meaningful solutions that have a positive impact on more people. Throughout every learning process I’ve taken on, challenges are almost always there waiting for me and I enjoy the sense of accomplishment that comes when I overcome them.

column23: For the past year, I served as planning and scheduling coordinator of NU Aaroh, a music club at Northeastern. This leadership experience has taught me valuable lessons about both university systems and personal growth. In this role, I was responsible for ensuring our events ran smoothly, which ironically taught me that something will inevitably go wrong at any event. I learned that the best way to deal with a problem like this is to maintain my composure to find methodical solutions. This ability to stay calm under pressure directly applies to client work at Generate, where meeting deadlines and managing client relationships require the same level-headed approach to problem-solving. This role also taught me the importance of planning ahead. Last year, our leadership team frequently found ourselves scrambling to finalize event details at the last minute. Now, as president of Aaroh, I'm learning from my prior experience and have implemented a system where we plan the majority of our semester in advance.
column24: During my co-op, I was assigned the task of setting up a CI/CD pipeline and deploying my team’s project using Kubernetes. I had no previous experience with anything CI/CD and nobody else on my team did either. As a result, I spent a very long time reading through documentation, starting with creating Docker containers for our frontend and backend, implementing automated testing through Github Actions, and finally configuring Kubernetes deployment. While I got the entire pipeline working, my employer decided not to pay for Kubernetes hosting. Though the outcome here was disappointing, this experience taught me that persistence and thorough research can overcome any technical challenge. This experience changed my mindset as a software engineer. I learned to value the knowledge gained through the problem-solving process, not just the final result.
column25: I worked as a software consultant at NExT Labs during Spring 2025. Through this co-op, I gained comprehensive experience in client-focused software development. This role taught me to interpret unclear client requirements, work collaboratively to develop solutions that meet their needs, and deliver presentations that demonstrate product value. The experience highlighted aspects of client work such as strong communication and project management that go beyond traditional software engineering. As a developer, I contributed to every layer of our software systems, from backend security and API integrations to frontend development. Working with a team of co-ops, I developed skills in an agile manner and learned how to coordinate with other developers to consistently deliver high-quality products that exceeded client expectations. I believe this experience makes me the perfect candidate for client-focused software development at Generate, and has made me more interested in wanting to explore software consulting, which is why I’m applying to Generate.
column26: I am confident in my ability to make a simple and functional backend for any web application. I have experience building web apps using both React (TypeScript) and Flask (Python) and have successfully built multiple projects that require a backend API with extensive underlying functionality. I want to explore DevOps more and definitely want to improve on that skill. I recently learned how important it is but don’t have much experience in this area, so I am interested in learning more about it. I enjoy the process of learning new things and taking on challenging work. I’ve found that the most rewarding aspects of a project for me are the ones where I have no idea what I’m doing, but somehow figure out a way to solve the problem. I always end up reflecting the most on those moments and growing from them more than any others, which is why I enjoy taking on challenging work.

column23: Generate is a close-knit group of friends who aspire to become better versions of themselves tomorrow than they are today. Each member supports the ambitions of the others, and many of them will remain lifelong friends. I perceive Generate as a space where Northeastern’s most ambitious, insightful, and collaborative friends congregate to assist clients in expanding their businesses. Within Generate, I have acquired valuable skills in interviewing individuals and collaborating with my team to expedite project execution. Generate has served as an incubator for my technical and interpersonal development. The supportive environment fosters lifelong friendships among its members. I view Generate as a platform where Northeastern’s most ambitious, insightful, and collaborative individuals gather to assist clients in business growth. Within Generate, I have honed my skills in interviewing and collaborating with my team, enabling me to develop essential interpersonal abilities as well.
column24: I developed a program to optimize calendars for the busy individuals. This project was deeply integrated with multiple languages and parts. This project helped me understand the importance of modular coding which is how I program today. This project combined front end website design. Optimization algorithms at the core of the program and file formatting and data processing. Without this modular approach I have since learned to love so much additional work would be necessary to needing to begin again.
column25: I stand out at the intersection of engineering rigor and product taste. As a computer engineering student with a robotics minor, I build systems that are both elegant and reliable—recently a calendar-optimization app (React + Flask) that blends algorithms, data structures, and a clean UX. I move quickly without breaking thinking: small, testable increments, measurable outcomes, and readable code. I’m fluent across the stack. Teammates trust me because I over-communicate, document decisions, and welcome critique.  Clients and friends value that I ask business questions before code. I bring curiosity, high standards, and a collaborative bias for producing the best work I know how.
column26: I have strong code literacy foundation and I seek to improve on my ability to fail faster. In the developing age of AI understanding how to read, interact with and respond is becoming an increasingly valuable skill. Leveraging this with my growing programming knowledge,  I can quickly translate abstract ideas into functional prototypes. When code doesn’t work, I can become attached to it but I want to develop systems like using timers to pivot to a new solution sooner. I am motivated by my desire to see the product and my ideas become a reality. It is oddly satisfying when the project just work.

column23: During my time as a fellow at Eduruna, I worked with a team of seven to build an online presence for a local nonprofit. Our goal was to create a website and social media strategy that felt accessible and authentic. As the main point of contact, I met regularly with the nonprofit’s leadership to understand their goals, values, and challenges, then translated those conversations into design and messaging that aligned with their mission. This experience pushed me to become a stronger communicator and listener. I learned to bridge the gap between a nonprofit with vague expectations and an inexperienced development team. Both sides had frustrations with the process, but I helped us stay focused on the end user. In the end, we delivered a solution that worked, and one the nonprofit was proud to use. I completed my time as a fellow with a greater confidence in my ability to lead with empathy, adapt quickly, and build with purpose.
column24: At my most recent hackathon, I built EcoJustice (https://github.com/rayyshen/ecojustice), a tool that highlights cases of environmental racism and empowers users to take action on a case-by-case basis. My goal wasn’t to solve a global problem outright but to offer a realistic and usable way to reduce its impact. I’ve seen many projects chase big solutions without thinking about real users. They build impressive products, but no one actually uses them. With EcoJustice, I did the opposite. I made the issue clear up front to give users a reason to care, then created a simple path from awareness to action. This focus on usability helped the project stand out and it won the Grand Prize. The experience changed how I build. I now focus on practical solutions and design with real people in mind.
column25: In many of my projects, I’ve taken on both the role of a builder and a collaborator. At hackathons, I’ve led development on tools like EcoJustice, where I focused less on creating something flashy and more on building something people would actually use. I started by identifying a clear problem, environmental racism, and worked backward to design a product that made it easy for users to take action. In groups, I tend to step in wherever I’m needed. Sometimes that means writing backend logic or designing an accessible frontend, and other times it means helping teammates debug, or making sure we stay aligned on goals. I’ve worked with people at all experience levels and learned to adapt my role to whatever helps the team move forward. What sets me apart is that I care deeply about usability and impact. I build not only with users in mind, but also my teammates, trying to be someone who can lead, support, or just listen when needed. I think that makes me a strong fit for this team.
column26: One technical skill I’m confident in is my ability to use AI tools to boost my efficiency without sacrificing code quality. I’ve learned how to pair AI suggestions and explanations with my own knowledge, not only letting me build and iterate much faster, but also teaching me new concepts. One area I want to improve is the diversity of my tech stack. Most of my projects are built in Next.js, and while that’s helped me go deep in one framework, I know there’s value in learning other tools and approaches. I want to get more comfortable switching between stacks depending on the problem. What motivates me to learn is the thought that someone might actually use what I build. That’s what pushes me to try new tools, ship quickly, and keep improving.

column23: Interning at Mosaic, a YC startup, accelerated both my technical and collaborative growth. I contributed to improving a video editing workflow for multimodal agents, redesigning the canvas editor to support pausing, selective node restarts, and internal versioning. These improvements reduced friction for users and increased editing efficiency by 40%. I led 80% of UI/UX updates, iterating closely with backend engineers to improve usability and agent control during runtime. Through this, I gained real-world experience in software engineering design principles, frontend architecture, and collaborative development practices. More importantly, I learned how to build for real users, handle ambiguity, and iterate with intention which are skills I hope to bring to Generate.
column24: At HackIllinois 2025, I developed a social finance app that gamified saving and peer accountability. Our project reached the top 5 out of 105 teams, boosting projected user retention by 60% through competitive challenges and shared goals. I built the full-stack system using TypeScript, Next.js, and Supabase, handling everything from authentication to real-time data sync for 100+ concurrent actions. This experience taught me how to quickly prototype, ship with speed and precision, and design around real-world user behavior. It sparked my passion for building full-stack products that are not just functional but also fun and impactful.
column25: I bring a diverse set of skills ranging from software engineering and UI/UX design. From building multiplayer games and AI agents to prototyping full-stack apps at hackathons and startups. I know how to create intuitive, polished interfaces while working within engineering constraints, and I love bridging the gap between user needs and technical implementation. My experiences span frontend systems, backend infrastructure, and creative tools which give me a flexible mindset and strong communication skills that work well in diverse teams. Whether in code reviews or whiteboarding sessions, I aim to listen, iterate quickly, and build responsibly.
column26: I’m confident in front-end engineering, especially building interactive UIs and editor tools. Game development, which I’ve pursued for years, trained me to balance usability, performance, and visual clarity. I’d like to grow stronger in backend architecture and infrastructure, especially around scalability and system design. I’m motivated by the challenge of turning vague ideas into reality and by the opportunity to build tools that empower others. Whether it's debugging a strange UI bug or shipping a feature that makes someone’s workflow smoother. For me, the learning never stops and that’s what excites me most.

column23: A few years ago, I interned in IT at a healthcare company, conducting technology audits and coordinatingIT support across over 50 medical offices. My biggest challenge was working with site managers to understand their unique needs in using technology. To address this, I established regular check-ins to gather insights and discovered that many locations struggled with outdated systems and were unsure how to submit support tickets. By actively listening and asking questions, I identified a gap in technology proficiency among the staff. Some issues required upgrading software or hardware, while others needed clearer documentation on processes. This experience taught me that effective solutions go beyond simple implementation; they are rooted in understanding the users. Seeing how streamlined, intuitive systems resulting from my efforts in the IT department directly improved patient care efficiency, I now prioritize comprehending user workflows and needs before proposing solutions, aiming to create designs that not only accomplish tasks but also address real human challenges, reinforcing that the best engineering solutions resolve genuine problems, not just technical ones.
column24: During my co-op, I was tasked with revamping an application used by field technicians for record tracking. The goal was to eliminate their reliance on physical data keys, which posed risks of data breaches and loss. I was tasked with a challenge of working with unfamiliar technologies. I first started with researching various databases and learning .NET WinForms. Then,, I started with what I already kneow.. I created UML diagrams to map the existing application architecture and identify modification points. After evaluating various database options, I decided that a local SQLite database would best meet the application requirements while maintaining security. By leveraging online resources and team expertise, I redesigned the GUI for database integration using .NET WinForms. I also conducted user experience testing with the project manager and field technicians to ensure the application met their needs. This experience not only changed how I approach unfamiliar technologies but also ignited my passion for mastering new tools. My software background enabled me to learn quickly and critically.Theever growing industry drives me to continue to learn how to develop software that leaves a positive impact.
column25: As a computer engineering and computer science student, I understand how to be an engineer from different yet closely related perspectives.  My engineering background helps me consider system constraints, performance optimization, and how software interacts with physical components. As such, I hold a unique thought process that allows for more, better, and beyond. . As the leader for my capstone project, I noticed how my team was more cohesive and efficient when I individually reached out to each member for their ideas and thus understood their skillset. This allowed us to accomplish what we wanted to create. With various other experiences in working with teams, I found my forte in identifying individual strengths to mesh together to accomplish great projects.  As the VP of my fraternity, I find myself meeting with various types of people and witnessing different perspectives to conflicts. My technical capabilities combined with my interpersonal skills to bring out the best in each person is what lets me stand out–when everyone else is too.
column26: Without any computer science background, I chose EECE at Northeastern precisely because it felt both daunting and exciting. While learning coding languages and computer systems, I discovered my real passion: developing solutions that genuinely help users. Software engineering appeals to me because I see endless possibilities for growth - not just in my own skills, but in how technology can improve communities and solve real-world problems. I've developed strong backend development skills, primarily in Java and C. What draws me to backend work is its fundamental role as the backbone of applications - it's where you determine efficiency, security, and overall system performance. I love the complex algorithmic thinking it requires, even though it is often the most challenging part. When I hit a wall with a complex problem, instead of getting frustrated, I get genuinely excited about the challenge ahead.

column23: Through my second semester at Northeastern, I’ve learned the importance of connecting with other people, whether it’s because I share a hobby or interest with them or we’re both struggling on an assignment together. It has helped me realize that though on the outside, it seems that everyone is perfect, we all have our own strengths and weaknesses, and that’s what makes everyone human. This has taught me to be more considerate of both myself and others and not be afraid to reach out when I need help or advice to others. This is how I was able to join so many of the organizations I’m currently a part of, as I took the extra step to learn about my environment and the people around me. In general, working with others has helped me realize that being part of a team that works towards a goal is an amazing experience, as we all get to learn along the way and bond!
column24: Building Minesweeper for Fundies 2 in Java was an important experience in my growth as a software engineer. As I was given only the project requirements and no guidance on structure, I had to plan and design everything from scratch. I initially started with a structure I thought was sound enough, but as I implemented more features, I realized my design needed to be adjusted to accommodate functionality I hadn't fully anticipated. This process taught me the importance of writing flexible, well-documented code. Furthermore, I found that working with TAs when I was at a roadblock and unable to move forward was incredibly helpful, as I not only figured out how to fix the problem at hand but also tackle any future problems using a similar process. In the end, not only did I meet all the project requirements, but I was also able to add extra features easily, thanks to the solid foundation I had meticulously worked to create.
column25: I believe my strengths as a team player, my perseverance, and my passion for learning new skills help me stand out. I’ve often found myself excelling in areas I never expected—whether it was leading my high school colorguard, performing hip-hop with ASU without prior dance experience, or even building a hackathon project in JavaScript with minimal background in the language. These experiences taught me that the most rewarding challenges come from stepping into unknown territory, working with others, and growing together. There's so much to learn, not just from the skills itself you pick up, but also from the people you collaborate and make connections with during the journey.
column26: I believe my strongest technical skill currently is thorough code documentation. I make it a priority to clearly explain my work, especially when making changes or writing complex code that I may need to revisit later. Good documentation not only helps me understand my own code but also makes it easier to efficiently, update, refactor, or add new features. One area I’m eager to grow in is understanding the full software development lifecycle. While I have experience coding individual parts of a project, I haven’t yet seen what full-stack development looks like from start to finish. Still, I find this “puzzle-piece” style of learning motivating as it pushes me to take on new challenges. Every time something clicks or I connect new concepts, it feels like a meaningful step forward since I’m able to build both my confidence and skill set.

column16: A friend also applying to Generate
column23: My extracurricular and professional experiences have been pivotal in shaping my engineering mindset by consistently placing me on teams to collaboratively account for user needs and come up with an efficient solution.  Working together to teach as well as learn UX Design principles to raise my school’s UX Design competitors of FBLA to become winners at the state level, taught me the importance of collaboration to give and receive feedback to make the whole team successful.  This foundational experience was invaluable during my internship at Urbalo LLC. There, I moved from theory to practice, implementing stateful UI components using Flutter and Dart. I saw myself doing more than just writing code, but to translate design specifications into a responsive and functional experience, ensuring the front end correctly interfaced with business logic. This taught me how to work within an existing codebase and contribute to a team's development velocity. Co-founding the Young Investors Society in senior year of high school also honed my sense of project ownership and initiative—skills essential for any engineer tasked with bringing a feature from conception to deployment.
column24: My software internship at Urbalo LLC was the experience that fundamentally shaped me as an engineer because it was my first time transitioning from solo academic projects to a collaborative, professional environment. While projects like my K-Means Clustering app taught me how to implement complex algorithms in Java, the internship taught me how to build software as part of a team. My task was to implement new UI components for their mobile app using Flutter and Dart. This was far more than just writing code. I got to design and implement beautiful front end features that adhere to established coding standards. I learned to think about edge cases I hadn't considered and to write modular widgets that could be reused elsewhere in the app. This experience fundamentally shifted my perspective. I learned that software engineering isn't just about solving a technical problem; it's about building a robust product, communicating effectively with a team, and writing code with a long-term vision. It solidified my understanding that the best code is code that other engineers can easily understand and build upon.
column25: My unique advantage lies in the combination of my broad technical execution and a proven, product-centric mindset. This duality allows me to not only build features correctly but to also contribute to building the right features. On the execution side, I have hands-on experience across domains. My internship at Urbalo LLC involved contributing to code using Flutter and Dart, while my personal projects in Java (K-Means Clustering), Python (Desk Manager), and Swift (Haven iOS app) demonstrate my ability to learn quickly and apply skills across different platforms and problem domains. However, what truly sets me apart is that I pair this technical ability with a deep understanding of the user. My experience in FBLA, where I mentored a team and won a state-level award for UX Design, trained me to think critically about user needs and product strategy. This means I can collaborate effectively with designers and product managers, understand the "why" behind a technical task, and even suggest improvements that enhance the user experience. Within a team, this combination makes me a more effective collaborator. I can speak the language of both engineering and product, ensuring that my work is always aligned with the team's ultimate goal: to build something valuable for all of our users.
column26: One technical skill I am confident in is Java. I’ve moved beyond basic syntax and applied it to build substantial projects that required a solid grasp of computer science fundamentals. For instance, in my K-Means Clustering app, I implemented the core algorithm from scratch, focusing on efficient data structures to manage and classify pixel data. In another project, an Image Processing app, I designed a modular, system for applying complex, layered filters. These experiences solidified my confidence in using Java to solve complex problems and manage intricate application logic. A skill I am actively working to improve is C++. While my coursework has given me a solid foundation, I am eager to gain a deeper understanding of its use in high-performance systems. My goal is to master advanced concepts like low-level memory management and concurrency to build highly efficient software, whether for performance-critical backend services or game development, which I also took a course on. My motivation for learning is driven by a fundamental passion for building solutions. I enjoy the process of identifying a challenge—like organizing my desktop, which led to my Desk Manager project—and architecting a technical solution to solve it. For me, learning a new skill isn't an academic exercise; it's about expanding my toolkit so I can build more powerful, elegant, and robust solutions to the next problem I encounter.

column23: My growth as a software engineer has been shaped by building. I feel that I have learned and grown the most through projects that can have a real-life impact.  At Scriptome.AI, I developed backend infrastructure and integrated third-party APIs for their Copilot platform, learning how software can enable cutting-edge biotech research. This experience taught me the importance of reliable, scalable systems when researchers depend on your code for their work. Through hackathons, I've learned to rapidly prototype and iterate. My most meaningful project, EcoScans, involved creating an app that scans grocery barcodes and calculates sustainability scores using API data and custom algorithms. Building this end-to-end solution, from UI to data processing to scoring algorithms, taught me how software can empower users to make informed decisions about everyday choices. At Simply Agile, my work as an intern wasn’t limited to optimizing their platform through AI integration. I also represented the company at an international business conference. This showed me how technical work translates to business value and gave me confidence in communicating complex solutions to diverse audiences. These experiences have shown me that learning impactful software engineering happens when you’re building and understanding the technical and human problem you're solving.
column24: EcoScans fundamentally changed how I approach software engineering. During a hackathon, I wanted to help consumers make more sustainable grocery choices, but realized the technical challenge was making complex environmental data accessible and actionable. The project required me to integrate multiple APIs to gather sustainability information, then develop algorithms to process disparate data points into a single, meaningful score. But the real learning came from user experience decisions - how do you present complex environmental data in a way that's instantly understandable while someone's shopping? I had to balance technical accuracy with usability, ensuring the barcode scanning was fast and reliable while the scoring algorithm was transparent enough that users trusted the results. This taught me that great software engineering isn't just about clean code or efficient algorithms - it's about understanding your users' context and constraints. The project showed me how software can democratize access to information and empower individual decision-making. When users could quickly scan a product and understand its environmental impact, technology became a tool for positive change. This experience solidified my passion for building applications that solve real problems and make complex information accessible to everyone.

column23: My most recent co-op at Verisk marked a big step forward in my development. Before that, I mostly worked on class projects with clear specifications and fixed timelines. column24: During my co-op at Verisk, I built a secure internal API module that automatically handled authentication using bearer tokens. The system retrieved a short-lived token from Vault, cached it in Redis to reduce load, and refreshed it only when expired—ensuring secure, efficient access to internal APIs. It was my first time working on a long-term, security-focused component, so I had to think through edge cases, token lifecycles, and reusability. The experience taught me the importance of writing clean, maintainable code—especially since I knew I wouldn’t be around forever to explain it.

column23: At Hope's Door, a domestic violence organization, I found myself in uncharted territory while organizing donations and managing events for survivors whose stories humbled me daily. That team became my first real CS community, and we still keep in touch to celebrate each other's achievements. Both experiences taught me that my best growth happens when challenged but not alone. column24: My AI Signal Processing research project has shaped me as a software engineer most fundamentally because it taught me the complete project development lifecycle. I developed and trained a classifier that was optimized to identify unknown sounds within a set of predefined audio classes. My project's premise was that exposing each sub-classifier to data from all other classes during training would be more inclined to provide a response that aligns with the unknown category, or "other sounds.” This project pushed me beyond my comfort zone, requiring me to independently learn bash scripting, database management, ML model metrics, and intimidating industry standards. Learning to integrate feedback from my two mentors—a senior developer and researcher—taught me to be confident throughout the development process. The most rewarding result was optimizing my algorithm from O(n²) to O(n) complexity after months of feedback and revision, making it viable for real-world applications such as healthcare monitoring. The following year, I practiced countless presentations, whether to myself in my room, peers in class for feedback, or senior researchers at Regeneron STS and state competitions. This experience showed me that software engineering extends beyond coding—it requires collaboration, mentorship, and refining solutions until they meet real-world needs.
column25: My ability to translate technical concepts across diverse audiences and build connections in challenging environments allows me to excel in software engineering and within teams because these skills directly apply to Generate's collaborative, client-focused work. Similarly, throughout my AI research, I've presented the same technical findings to PhD researchers, high schoolers, and competition judges—each requiring different levels of detail and framing. column23: Whether it be learning morse code for ciphers or building catapults, Science Olympiad has had a large impact on me as it not only allowed me to further my engineering skills, but also gave me motivation to work. My largest motivator has always been seeing something work. The most impactful of all these projects was my catapult. I built it on my own with its purpose being to precisely shoot at a target. For around a week, I would go to school, come home, and then go to my backyard to mess around with pvc pipes, beach balls, and buckets to make a rudimentary catapult. For a few days that was all I did, just building the catapult, the most important part was still ahead of me: testing. I spent nearly a week and a half going through different settings to find precise measurements and figure out how to configure my catapult to make sure it would do exactly as I wanted. In the end, It wasn’t pretty, it wasn’t perfect, it didn’t get 1st, but it was my first time properly making something, showing me not only that I was capable, but that it was something I loved.
column24: When I was around 7 I became infatuated by the Rubik’s cube. It was my sole obsession for nearly a decade, getting to a point where I competed in competitions solving one over 100,000 times. Fast forwarding to senior year of high school, something I had always wanted to code, was a Rubik’s cube. So, as a senior project, I worked with a local Rubik’s cube retailer and coded a program that would teach you how to solve a Rubik’s Cube. In doing this, there were two integral parts to making this, the graphics, and the functionality. The graphics were tedious and difficult but easy enough to do. The functionality was much more difficult, I spent around a week or so just thinking of different ways to represent the cube until I decided to represent each face as an array with the color of it the data on the given node. The most important part was that I spent time thinking about what I would do before I got myself tunnel visioned on one idea. This is now something I do with all code I write and has helped me not only be more efficient, but be more concise.
column25: When I learn something new that I am interested in, It will get to a point where all I want to do is learn. I want to finish what I start and will do just about anything to get it done. However, to be the best at what you do, you can’t do it alone, it's important that you not only help your peers but also help yourself by listening to your peers. I remember a few months back, I was learning a song on guitar where I just couldn’t play it no matter what I did. So, I asked a more experienced friend within five minutes, I broke past that plateau. Shortly after, I helped him the same way he helped me. In many of these cases, I always saw it as an inconvenience to be helped, I thought I was doing everyone a favor by struggling in silence, however I have found joy not only in helping my peers, but being helped and learning something new.

column23: My internship experience at Observe.ai, a Series C startup, transformed my entire perspective about software engineering success.
