column24: Leading software development for my robotics team has had a lasting impact on my approach to solving engineering challenges. Managing a team of 10 people to build software for a robot seemed straightforward, as I thought sufficient technical skill and task delegation would be enough to have a working product. However, I quickly realized that successful software engineering extends beyond individual coding ability. As a group, we encountered challenges in integrating computer vision algorithms, control systems, and decision logic. I began to understand that engineering was not solely about writing better code, as it was about using your interpersonal skills, continuously collaborating and understanding how each individual's efforts contribute to the outcome. When our team succeeded in executing working features, the victory was not a result of one person's code, but primarily our efficiency as a team to quickly regroup and innovate, share knowledge across different teams, and keep an eye on the goal. This experience established my approach to team engineering: that collaboration and communication is just as or more valuable than having pure technical ability.
column25: My background combines practical expertise, leadership roles, and a focus on implementing meaningful solutions. column23: I haven't been part of Generate before, but my experiences so far have really shaped how I think about building things and working with others. My most impactful experience was working with a teammate on an AI-powered Code Documentation Assistant. We noticed that developers often struggle with writing clear documentation, so we built a tool that automatically generates human-readable docs from source code using AI and vector databases. The biggest challenge came when we were integrating OpenAI's API with our FAISS database. Neither of us had worked with Retrieval-Augmented Generation before. I spent a weekend learning RAG techniques, then walked my teammate through the concepts. When our code retrieval finally worked, we were both genuinely excited about what we'd built. Working as a duo taught me how to balance technical ambition with practical deadlines. When our initial web interface was getting too complex, I suggested we use Streamlit instead of building from scratch. It meant scrapping some earlier work, but we ended up with a clean, functional product that users could interact with in real-time. What I loved most was how we combined curiosity about AI with genuine problem-solving. We weren't just building something cool – we were addressing a real pain point that developers face. That experience of creating something that actually helps people is exactly what draws me to Generate.
column24: Working on the Archimedes Web Portal as a front-end developer co-op has fundamentally changed how I approach software engineering. This project taught me that building software isn't just about writing code – it's about creating something that actually works for real people. The biggest learning moment came when I was integrating our React frontend with the Laravel backend. The APIs weren't behaving like I expected, and users were getting error messages that made no sense. Instead of just fixing the immediate bug, I realized I needed to understand the entire data flow. I started using Postman to test every endpoint, worked closely with the backend team to understand their MySQL queries, and made sure our frontend state actually synced with what was happening on the server. This experience taught me the importance of thinking beyond just my piece of the puzzle. When I was building the role-based access system, I had to consider how different user types would interact with the platform, not just whether my React components looked right. It made me a much more thoughtful developer. What really shaped me was learning to write proper tests using Jest and React Testing Library. At first, testing felt like extra work, but when our automated tests caught bugs before they reached users, I understood why it mattered. Setting up CI/CD with GitHub Actions showed me how good engineering practices protect both the product and the team. This project taught me that great software engineering is about communication, testing, and thinking about the user experience – not just writing clever code.
column25: What sets me apart is my unique combination of AI/ML expertise with full-stack development skills, backed by real business experience. column23: I’ve been a part of Forge for two semesters: Fall 2023 and Fall 2024. During the Fall of 2023, I worked as a hardware member on a project that developed an automated pasta machine. column23: The first thing I did when my interest in software grew was seek a structured exposure to web development. Starting with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript gave me just enough freedom to see my ideas take shape on screen. However, it stopped feeling abstract when I built a website for a local bakery. At first, I followed the skeletal template that the business owner outlined. But, her vision evolved with each prototype. She wanted a site that was clean and easy to navigate for elderly customers, yet still engaging and visually appealing for teens. So I shifted priorities– researching color theory, reorganizing visual clutter, and integrating subtle JavaScript transitions to keep the interface lively to an extent.  Once I adopted her perspective, programming became a blueprint for a purposeful design. I learned that building software is not just technical, but also about serving real people. That project reshaped how I approach development. Thus, I hope to continue that growth with others by programming with intention, listening to users, and building in a community.
column24: I have a habit of making bad habits. One of them used to be scrapping everything and starting from scratch when something was not working. Rebuilding, reorganizing, and reevaluating seemed like the quickest way to address problems and enhance design. Exhibit A: the calculator. The task was simple: program a calculator that adds, subtracts, and multiplies, without following the order of operations. It seemed easy enough. However, when I encountered a persistent bug, I found myself repeatedly rewriting and discarding methods. My code was disorganized, as were my thoughts – a mess feeding a mess. Eventually, I hit a wall. The final time I started over, I forced myself to slow down. I reframed the problem. Wrote pseudo-code. Countered the drawbacks of said pseudo-code. Then reused helper functions I had previously dismissed. By debugging and revising instead of abandoning, I saw code evolve rather than be replaced.  This project reshaped my approach to software. I try to leave behind the“wipe-the-slate” mentality and work with iteration, clarity, and ways to improve the current state. Though I have not perfected this mentality, I have learned not to underestimate its value.
column25: I cannot pinpoint when my overthinking turned from being a burden to a creative outlet. Obsessive thinking became my tactic for solving problems–and creating them. There was satisfaction in conjuring up scenarios, edge cases, and alternate inputs to overload any system, whether it be programming a calculator, infinitely looping a formula in Excel, or predicting outcomes of a true-crime podcast. Eventually, I learned that creating thoughtful, even useless, problems is often the key to better solutions. That curiosity fueled my endeavors, but it faltered during the Karim Kharbouch Coding Fellowship. There, we were expected to collaborate, share opinions, and face judgment. It felt like I accidentally signed up for a free trial of self-doubt. After failed attempts to articulate my thoughts on our website’s design or code, I realized speaking imperfectly was better than staying silent. Soon, my overthinking began to offer a valuable perspective for the team and our projects. Overall, pushing through the discomfort taught me how to collaborate, build on others’ ideas, adopt new perspectives, and approach challenges creatively. I now bring a combination of curiosity, problem-solving, and team adaptability–skills that let me contribute to unconventional ideas while helping a team turn them into functional, impactful solutions.
column26: Although there is always room for improvement, I have gained confidence in my ability to write thorough tests and apply design patterns to structure projects clearly and efficiently. column23: Interning at Google offered a fantastic opportunity to build on my nine years of private coding lessons by leading the development of an open-source web development proxy server and centralized configuration file. Overall, I learned how to deliver impactful results for a massive user base, which I hope to bring to Northeastern Generate.
column24: One project that shaped me as a software engineer was building Headshot News. After competing in the Husky Startup Challenge, my teammates moved on to other projects. column24: My work on the medical machine learning project at Roux Institute fundamentally changed my perspective on engineering impact. Developing an LSTM model to predict amputation risk from 10,000+ patient records wasn't just about achieving 94% accuracy - it forced me to confront the real-world consequences of technical decisions.  The pivotal moment came when we first tested our model on Stanford Hospital's external dataset. Seeing predictions directly correlate with actual patient outcomes made me realize:  How model architecture choices (like using time-aware LSTMs) could literally affect treatment plans  Why explainability matters just as much as accuracy in healthcare AI  The critical importance of data integrity when working with sensitive EHRs  This experience transformed how I approach all projects now - whether building financial systems at eMoney or classroom tools as a TA. I constantly ask: "Who will this affect, and how?" That mindset shift from purely technical solutions to human-impact engineering has been my most valuable growth.

column23: I spent a year away from Northeastern working full-time as a full-stack engineer at RepRally, where I joined as engineer #3. I worked across our TypeScript stack (React Native, backend services, build tooling, CI/CD), building systems used by thousands of reps and hundreds of brands. The pace and autonomy pushed me to become a more rigorous and self-directed engineer, but also a practical technical leader. I helped set engineering direction, onboarded new team members, and maintained high review standards as we scaled. I worked closely with our GTM and ops teams to identify pain points, shape solutions, and keep projects moving. column24: I led the development of a complex goal and reward system that tracked performance metrics for thousands of users and triggered automated payouts. I designed the data model, built the backend logic, exposed the data through a typed API, and implemented the frontend views in React Native and our Next.js web app. I also built a flexible filtering system to support dynamic constraints. This project shaped how I think about systems design: breaking problems into logical pieces, planning for edge cases, and building for long-term maintainability. I worked closely with the GTM lead to translate abstract business goals into concrete product requirements, which pushed me to stay focused on usability and impact. Once the system was built, I onboarded other engineers by walking them through the architecture, live usage patterns, and associated UI—making sure the system could be extended and supported by others. Collaborating across engineering and product made this one of the most well-rounded and meaningful projects I've worked on.

column23: From being a retail worker at HomeGoods, being a Teen Leader at Cradles to Crayons, and a Discrete Structures Teaching Assistant, I have interacted with a diverse range of people with different perspectives. column24: My experience at BostonHacks 2024 shaped me as a software engineer. This was my first time building a project outside of school. At BostonHacks, my team and I built SVS Lunar Client, a deep reinforcement learning model that simulates space environments to train AI to complete specific tasks. At first, this idea seemed very ambitious since none of us had experience with reinforcement learning, and I had never used Unity before. When trying to install the language, libraries, and frameworks, we ran into many compatibility issues. We spent hours researching documentation, Googling, and watching YouTube videos to just figure out how to set the project up. Many more hours were spent just learning about the tools we were going to use. This struggle taught me that real engineering is messy, unlike homework assignments with a clear solution. We stayed up the whole night working on this project, submitting minutes before the 24-hour deadline. To our surprise, we ended up winning the AI/ML track. The thought of how we turned an idea that seemed like a reach into a working project amazed me. Since then, I’ve been tackling more ambitious projects and trying to learn as much as I can.
column25: I have attended a decent amount of hackathons in the past year. I believe the skills I have gained from these hackathons will allow me to thrive in this role and within a team. column23: Though I have not been in Generate before, my professional experiences have really shaped the way I work with others and learn independently. column24: The first few months working at Priceline during my first co-op were truly a learning experience for me. column16: Spoke with a current member, Abhik Ray.
column23: Ever since I joined InstaLily AI, I truly understood the meaning of building with a purpose. As a company, InstaLily is all about the clients and developing to make operations more efficient for them. Time and time again talented developers build with no intention which leads to products that may seem cool, but don't actually close a gap or solve a need. After witnessing the real-time impacts my development at InstaLily had on the client's growth, I had a new found drive to make a difference. These experiences taught me to develop inclusively, intentionally, and measure success by user impact. I no longer see the use in building a project that shows I can code, I'd rather build something that shows I can produce with the intention of closing a gap. This led to my development of Speakl.ai, an AI powered speaking coach that revitalizes the speaking process and helps individuals find THEIR voice. Speakl.ai, isn’t an effort to show off my technical skill, rather it is a reflection of my understanding there are so many gaps that exist and I wanted to build something that can help do that.
column24: Working on Speakl.ai has been one of the most shaping experiences in my growth as a software engineer. It’s a project I’m still actively building with a small team, and it continues to stretch my thinking every day. What began as a rough concept between friends has grown into a platform that helps people improve how they speak, communicate, and express themselves with confidence.  I had to learn fast. From building LLM workflows and integrating speech APIs to designing cloud infrastructure that could scale, it pushed me in every direction. But what shaped me most wasn’t just the technical side. It was learning how to build with empathy, listen to users, and focus on real impact.  Every feature we ship has a purpose. Whether it's analyzing clarity or simulating ideal delivery, the goal is always to create something that genuinely helps people. This experience has taught me that great software isn't about showing off what you can build. It's about understanding the problem, collaborating with your team, and staying close to your users.  That mindset has fundamentally changed how I approach engineering, and it’s something I carry into every project I take on.
column25: The notion that software engineering is a job that doesn’t require communication is completely false. In every role I’ve had, whether working on client-facing development at InstaLily AI or co-founding Speakl.ai, I’ve realized that great products don’t come from code alone. They come from clear conversations, shared goals, and a deep understanding of the people we’re building for. What makes me stand out is the way I combine technical depth with a real focus on people. I’ve worked across backend systems, AI pipelines, and cloud tools, but the real impact has always come from collaborating with teammates, listening to feedback, and aligning on what actually matters. Working on a small team has taught me to take initiative, jump into different roles, and support whoever needs help. I care just as much about the process as the outcome. I bring a builder’s mindset, a teammate’s attitude, and a real commitment to making things better with every project. That’s the kind of energy I’d bring to Generate.
column26: One technical skill I’ve grown confident in is backend development. That said, I really want to improve my frontend skills, more specifically creating smoother, more intuitive user experiences. I’ve realized that no matter how strong the backend is, if the interface doesn’t feel right, users won’t connect with the product. I want to be able to contribute across the stack and bring that full vision to life. What keeps me learning is honestly just curiosity. I love the feeling of solving problems that once felt impossible and seeing ideas go from sketch to shipped. Taking on challenges is exciting because it means I’m growing, and I’m someone who genuinely enjoys the process as much as the outcome.

column23: Since I spent my first year of Northeastern at the Oakland campus, I have not been able to participate in Generate, however, as a research assistant working with Dr. Abi-Hassan, I'm currently building a Python-based API script to analyze Supreme Court case data, providing me with valuable experience in applied software development. column24: Creating a media company from ground up high school was an experience that shifted my perspective as a software engineer to the startup side. Now when I approach software projects, I instinctively consider who I am solving issues for and the community I am affecting. That is why with my current northeastern app project, I am approaching it with a heavy, client-oriented approach, where the students will come first in every step of development.

column23: Looking back on my first semester and my first day at the Northeastern Electric Racing Club (NER), I couldn’t have imagined where I’d be today. column24: One project that has fundamentally shaped me as a software engineer is the CNM Chatbot Project I worked on during the Breakthrough Tech Program. I collaborated with a team of five developers to build a chatbot for a nonprofit organization in California. The goal was to reduce employee response time by enabling the chatbot to answer common questions, ultimately improving workflow efficiency.  This was a valuable hands-on experience that pushed me beyond the classroom. We had three weekly meetings—one with our internal team, one with a program assistant, and one with the client—to clarify project requirements and discuss progress. I found the collaborative environment especially impactful, as it encouraged us to share ideas, provide feedback, and iterate quickly.  We organized ourselves using Discord for communication, GitHub for version control, Google Docs to track our progress, and Zoom for team syncs. Through this experience, I strengthened both my technical skills—like working with large language models and APIs—and my soft skills, such as client communication and project management.  This project confirmed my passion for using technology to solve real-world problems and showed me what it means to function as part of a real software engineering team.
column25: I believe the combination of my Computer Science and Business Administration major, with a concentration in Supply Chain, gives me a unique perspective. column26: One technical skill I’ve developed and feel confident in is Java. I’ve used it in several projects, particularly for building backend functionality and writing object-oriented solutions. column23: I have been on co-op for one month now, working as a full-stack developer where I am helping build a web application from the ground up. There were two co-ops before me, and I am continuing their work, so coming into this position, I’ve had to learn how to read someone else’s code and build upon it effectively. Outside of co-op, I’ve also been actively coding with friends. We’ve completed a full project together and are currently working on another one. These collaborative, passion-driven experiences really spark my love for software development. That’s the type of environment where I grow and have grown the most.
column24: One project that fundamentally shaped me as a software engineer is strategy.town, the most recent project on my resume. Going into it, I was unfamiliar with many of the technologies we used, both the languages and libraries were new to me,but through collaboration and persistence, my team and I delivered a successful final product. This experience taught me how to effectively onboard onto an existing codebase, understand unfamiliar code, and eventually build on top of it. I gained valuable technical skills in full-stack development, but more importantly, I developed the ability to work through communication barriers within a team and contribute to a fast-paced, high-functioning environment. We followed the agile methodology, held regular SCRUM meetings, and practiced iterative development,mirroring the structure of many corporate engineering teams. I learned how critical it is to stay aligned with both your team and your goals. Most importantly, this project taught me to always consider the needs of stakeholders. We had to clearly define a minimum viable product and deliver exactly the functionality we promised to our TA. It reinforced that being a good software engineer isn’t just about writing code, it’s about delivering what matters most to your users.
column25: My ability to quickly learn new languages and libraries, paired with strong communication skills, will allow me to excel as a software engineer. In my most recent project, as mentioned earlier, I was completely unfamiliar with the tech stack we used. I had to learn new languages, frameworks, and libraries from scratch. Much of my initial time was spent reading documentation, watching YouTube tutorials, and researching online to better understand the codebase and contribute non-buggy code. While I do my best to persevere independently, I’ve also learned that knowing when to seek help is just as important as problem-solving on your own. One of my teammates was highly experienced with the tools we were using, and instead of feeling discouraged or intimidated, I viewed them as a valuable resource. Asking questions, learning from their insights, and engaging in technical discussions significantly accelerated my growth. I’ve come to understand that reaching out for guidance isn’t a weakness, it’s a skill. It reflects a willingness to learn and a drive to improve. Combining that mindset with my independent learning habits has made me a much more adaptable and resourceful engineer.
column26: One technical skill I am confident in is backend development. Through multiple full-stack projects, I’ve gained experience designing database models, creating API endpoints, and handling live events with Socket. I enjoy building the backend because it handles much of the core logic and functionality of an application. One area I’d like to improve is UI/UX design. While my frontends are functional, I recognize they could be more intuitive, visually appealing, and accessible. I want to build interfaces that not only work, but feel seamless to users in any situation. What motivates me to take on challenges and learn new things is the sense of ownership and accomplishment I feel when I complete a project. Despite a slow start with team collaboration and communication, we pulled through and delivered something I was proud to present. That final moment of sharing our work, and the pride and validation that come with it, is what drives me to keep improving and tackling bigger challenges.

column19: Scout: Tech Lead, Fall Co-op (in Boston)
column23: My development work with clients at Scout as well as my current co-op at Chewy has shown me how little I actually know, and driven me to learn more.  Last semester at Scout a client needed a main content management system so they could update their site without code changes. Learning and connecting a new CMS for them within days taught me all about headless architectures and set me up perfectly to join Chewy's CMS team. At Chewy, working in production environments only opened my eyes to all the effort and vast systems that go into managing only one website. Through my co-op I’ve grown immensely by learning tools and patterns I’d never heard of, adopting Agile sprints, using CI/CD pipelines, and translating technical specs to non-technical clients or those without context.  Not a week goes by that I don't learn more, or have to reach out to a new team asking about something I don't know. Every time I encounter the unknown, I get the opportunity to level up technically and communicatively. An attitude I'm eager to channel into Generate's projects
column24: Before working as a developer at Scout, I had only written code for myself or professors. Delivering real world websites for clients transformed how I think about accessibility and scalability.  One of the core elements of Scout is the extensive design process that goes on before the developer handoff. After attending countless design meetings and iterating over the smallest details in the name of accessibility, the idea of designing for accessibility is second nature. Why not allow everyone possible to use your product?  Scale is another factor I never considered, what happens when ten thousand people are requesting the resource at once? A hundred thousand? Building a website for a popular product taught me a lot about the considerations of scale and reliability. When the goal is to sell your product, you should prepare to be popular, so the idea of scaling or preparing for that is always something I keep in mind.  Both of these considerations have been ingrained within me and changed my engineering mindset as a whole, now at every project I'm not only asking “Will it work?” but “Who might be excluded?” and “Can this handle the scale?"
column25: What sets me apart is my extensive full-stack experience, direct client engagement, and building solutions for both internal and external stakeholders. At Scout, I lead the implementation of a new website with a headless CMS and deployment pipeline within a month, translating designer hi-fis into production code with weekly updates to non-technical clients. At the JAAGO Foundation I worked on design research and building out a new website, both experiences teaching me the client/customer side perspective when to comes to development. Today at Chewy, I work on the CMS team delivering features for other internal teams and building workflows to increase efficiency. The mix of delivering external client projects, optimizing for end‑user impact, and ensuring internal reliability, has taught me to think of the edge cases, align priorities across teams, and mentor teammates in best practices. My past experience and emphasis on collaboratively is what prepares me to excel in this role and within any team.
column26: I'm very confident in my ability to translate Figma designs/features into development as designers intended based on past experience and I would really like to improve my debugging skills or finding where bugs are. My motivation to continue learning and take on challenges stems from the fact that I find challenges fun and the feeling of knowing something I hadn't before and being able to apply it is unique and addicting.
